1	\chapter{Section graded ring infrastructure: tensor powers and graded sections}
2	\label{chap:Picard_SectionGradedRing}
3	
4	% This chapter blueprints the Mathlib-absent infrastructure that the section
5	% graded ring R(X_s,L_s)=⊕_m Γ(X_s,L_s^{⊗m}) and section graded module
6	% M(X_s,F_s,L_s)=⊕_m Γ(X_s,F_s⊗L_s^{⊗m}) of Picard_QuotScheme.tex
7	% (def:sectionGradedRing / def:sectionGradedModule) depend on. Those two
8	% definitions are NOT restated here; this chapter supplies the three separable
9	% infrastructure layers they \uses{}. The target Lean file is
10	% AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/SectionGradedRing.lean (1:1 slug, to be scaffolded).
11	
12	\providecommand{\PshMod}{\mathrm{PresheafOfModules}}
13	\providecommand{\ShMod}{\mathrm{SheafOfModules}}
14	\providecommand{\Modules}{\mathrm{Modules}}
15	
16	The Hilbert-polynomial route of \cref{chap:Picard_QuotScheme} represents the
17	degree-\(m\) graded component of a fibre by the global sections
18	\(\Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\) of a twist of a coherent
19	sheaf by a tensor power of a line bundle, and it organises these components into
20	a graded ring \(R(X_s, L_s) = \bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m})\)
21	(\cref{def:sectionGradedRing}) and a graded module
22	\(M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s) = \bigoplus_{m \ge 0}
23	\Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\)
24	(\cref{def:sectionGradedModule}). Three pieces of that picture have no direct
25	Mathlib counterpart and must be built here, each in its own section:
26	
27	\begin{enumerate}
28	  \item \textbf{Tensor powers of a sheaf of modules}
29	    (\cref{sec:sgr_tensor_powers}). Mathlib equips the category
30	    \(\PshMod\) of \emph{presheaves} of modules over a presheaf of commutative
31	    rings with a symmetric monoidal structure
32	    (\cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}) and provides the
33	    sheafification functor \(\PshMod \to \ShMod\)
34	    (\cref{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}); but the category
35	    \(\ShMod\) of \emph{sheaves} of modules carries \emph{no} monoidal
36	    structure. The tensor product of sheaves of modules
37	    \(\mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G}\) must therefore be built
38	    as the sheafification of the pointwise tensor presheaf, and the iterated
39	    tensor power \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes m}\) recursively from it.
40	  \item \textbf{Lax-monoidal global sections} (\cref{sec:sgr_lax_sections}).
41	    The global-sections functor \(\Gamma\) is only \emph{lax} monoidal: from the
42	    pointwise-strict monoidality of the presheaf tensor product and the
43	    sheafification unit one obtains a natural multiplication
44	    \(\Gamma(\mathcal{F}) \otimes_{\Gamma(\mathcal{O}_X)} \Gamma(\mathcal{G})
45	    \to \Gamma(\mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G})\), together with
46	    the associativity and unit coherence those products must satisfy.
47	  \item \textbf{Graded-ring / graded-module assembly}
48	    (\cref{sec:sgr_graded_assembly}). The section multiplications and the
49	    tensor-power comparison isomorphisms assemble, through Mathlib's
50	    external-direct-sum graded API (\cref{lem:directSum_gcommSemiring_mathlib},
51	    \cref{lem:directSum_gmodule_mathlib}), into the graded \emph{ring} structure
52	    on \(\bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m})\) and the graded
53	    \emph{module} structure on
54	    \(\bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\).
55	\end{enumerate}
56	
57	\section{Tensor powers of a sheaf of modules}
58	\label{sec:sgr_tensor_powers}
59	
60	We work over a fixed scheme \(X\) (in the application \(X = X_s\), a fibre over a
61	field \(\kappa(s)\)). Its category of sheaves of modules is
62	\(X.\Modules = \ShMod(\mathcal{O}_X)\), with global-sections notation
63	\(\Gamma(X, \mathcal{M}) = \mathcal{M}(X)\) for the value of the underlying
64	presheaf on the top open. The structure sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_X\) is a sheaf of
65	commutative rings, so its underlying presheaf factors through the category of
66	commutative rings; this is the hypothesis under which Mathlib's presheaf-level
67	monoidal structure applies.
68	
69	\begin{lemma}[Monoidal structure on presheaves of modules]
70	  \label{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}
71	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.monoidalCategory}
72	  \mathlibok
73	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
74	  (\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Presheaf.Monoidal}).}
75	  Let \(R : \mathcal{C}^{\mathrm{op}} \to \mathrm{CommRingCat}\) be a presheaf of
76	  commutative rings. Then the category \(\PshMod(R)\) of presheaves of modules
77	  over \(R\) carries a symmetric monoidal structure whose tensor product is
78	  computed objectwise: for presheaves of modules \(\mathcal{M}_1, \mathcal{M}_2\)
79	  and an object \(U\),
80	  \[
81	    (\mathcal{M}_1 \otimes \mathcal{M}_2)(U)
82	      \;=\; \mathcal{M}_1(U) \otimes_{R(U)} \mathcal{M}_2(U),
83	  \]
84	  with restriction maps the tensor products of the restriction maps. The unit is
85	  the presheaf \(R\) viewed as a module over itself, and the associator,
86	  unitors, and braiding are induced objectwise from those of
87	  \(\mathrm{ModuleCat}\). In particular evaluation at a fixed object \(U\) is a
88	  \emph{strict} monoidal functor
89	  \(\PshMod(R) \to \mathrm{ModuleCat}(R(U))\).
90	\end{lemma}
91	
92	\begin{lemma}[Sheafification of presheaves of modules]
93	  \label{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}
94	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.sheafification}
95	  \mathlibok
96	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
97	  (\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Presheaf.Sheafification}).}
98	  Let \(R\) be a sheaf of (commutative) rings on a site, with underlying
99	  presheaf \(R_0\). There is a sheafification functor
100	  \[
101	    (-)^{\#} \;:\; \PshMod(R_0) \longrightarrow \ShMod(R),
102	  \]
103	  left adjoint to the forgetful functor, together with the unit
104	  \(\eta_{\mathcal{M}_0} : \mathcal{M}_0 \to (\mathcal{M}_0)^{\#}\) of the
105	  adjunction. On underlying sheaves of abelian groups it agrees with the
106	  ordinary sheafification, and it sends a presheaf of \(R_0\)-modules to the
107	  associated sheaf of \(R\)-modules.
108	\end{lemma}
109	
110	\begin{lemma}[The structure sheaf as the unit module]
111	  \label{lem:moduleUnit_mathlib}
112	  \lean{SheafOfModules.unit}
113	  \mathlibok
114	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
115	  (\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Sheaf}).}
116	  For a sheaf of rings \(R\) (in the application \(R = \mathcal{O}_X\)) the
117	  structure sheaf, viewed as a module over itself, is a distinguished object
118	  \(\mathbf{1} = \mathcal{O}_X \in \ShMod(\mathcal{O}_X)\). It is the value at
119	  the zeroth tensor power, \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes 0} = \mathcal{O}_X\), and its
120	  global sections \(\Gamma(X, \mathcal{O}_X)\) form the degree-zero component of
121	  the section graded ring.
122	\end{lemma}
123	
124	\begin{definition}\leanok
125	[Tensor product of sheaves of modules]
126	  \label{def:sheafTensorObj}
127	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj}
128	  \uses{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib,
129	    lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}
130	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Sheaves of Modules", \S "Tensor product" (Tag
131	  % 01CA), prose definition (read from references/stacks-modules.tex,
132	  % L2282-L2297).
133	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
134	  % "Let $(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$ be a ringed space and let
135	  %  $\mathcal{F}$ and $\mathcal{G}$ be $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules.
136	  %  We define first the {\it tensor product presheaf}
137	  %  $$
138	  %  \mathcal{F} \otimes_{p, \mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G}
139	  %  $$
140	  %  as the rule which assigns to $U \subset X$ open the
141	  %  $\mathcal{O}_X(U)$-module
142	  %  $\mathcal{F}(U) \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)} \mathcal{G}(U)$.
143	  %  Having defined this we define the {\it tensor product sheaf}
144	  %  as the sheafification of the above:
145	  %  $$
146	  %  \mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G}
147	  %  =
148	  %  (\mathcal{F} \otimes_{p, \mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G})^\#
149	  %  $$"
150	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Sheaves of Modules", Tag 01CA (tensor
151	  product).}
152	  Let \(\mathcal{F}, \mathcal{G}\) be sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. Their
153	  \emph{tensor product} is the sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules
154	  \[
155	    \mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G}
156	      \;=\; \bigl(\mathcal{F} \otimes_{p,\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G}\bigr)^{\#},
157	  \]
158	  the sheafification (\cref{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}) of the
159	  tensor product presheaf \(\mathcal{F} \otimes_{p,\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G}\),
160	  whose value on an open \(U\) is the \(\mathcal{O}_X(U)\)-module
161	  \(\mathcal{F}(U) \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)} \mathcal{G}(U)\). Concretely, the
162	  tensor product presheaf is the Mathlib presheaf-level monoidal product
163	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}) of the underlying presheaves of
164	  modules of \(\mathcal{F}\) and \(\mathcal{G}\), and
165	  \(\mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G}\) is its sheafification. This
166	  construction is symmetric and associative because the presheaf tensor product
167	  is, and sheafification preserves the coherence isomorphisms.
168	\end{definition}
169	
170	\begin{definition}\leanok
171	[Tensor power of a line bundle]
172	  \label{def:sheafTensorPow}
173	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorPow}
174	  \uses{def:sheafTensorObj, lem:moduleUnit_mathlib}
175	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Sheaves of Modules", \S "Invertible modules",
176	  % `definition-powers' (Tag 01CU) (read from references/stacks-modules.tex,
177	  % L4220-L4228).
178	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
179	  % "Let $(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$ be a ringed space. Given an invertible sheaf
180	  %  $\mathcal{L}$ on $X$ and $n \in \mathbf{Z}$ we define the
181	  %  $n$th {\it tensor power} $\mathcal{L}^{\otimes n}$ of $\mathcal{L}$
182	  %  as the image of $\mathcal{O}_X$ under applying the equivalence
183	  %  $\mathcal{F} \mapsto\mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{L}$
184	  %  exactly $n$ times."
185	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Sheaves of Modules", Tag 01CU (tensor
186	  powers).}
187	  Let \(\mathcal{L}\) be a sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules (in the application
188	  an invertible sheaf / line bundle \(L_s\)). For \(m \in \mathbb{N}\) the
189	  \emph{\(m\)th tensor power} \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes m}\) is defined by recursion
190	  on \(m\):
191	  \[
192	    \mathcal{L}^{\otimes 0} = \mathcal{O}_X
193	      \quad(\text{the unit module, \cref{lem:moduleUnit_mathlib}}),
194	    \qquad
195	    \mathcal{L}^{\otimes (m+1)}
196	      = \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{L}
197	        \quad(\cref{def:sheafTensorObj}).
198	  \]
199	  We restrict to non-negative powers, which is all the section graded ring uses;
200	  the Stacks definition extends \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes n}\) to all
201	  \(n \in \mathbf{Z}\) using invertibility, but negative powers are not needed
202	  here. For \(\mathcal{L}\) invertible each \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes m}\) is again
203	  invertible.
204	\end{definition}
205	
206	\begin{definition}\leanok
207	[Twist of a coherent sheaf by a tensor power]
208	  \label{def:sheafModuleTwist}
209	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.moduleTensorPow}
210	  \uses{def:sheafTensorObj, def:sheafTensorPow}
211	  Let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules (in the application
212	  a coherent sheaf \(\mathcal{F}_s\)) and \(\mathcal{L}\) a line bundle. For
213	  \(m \in \mathbb{N}\) the \emph{\(m\)-twist} of \(\mathcal{F}\) by
214	  \(\mathcal{L}\) is
215	  \[
216	    \mathcal{F}(m) \;:=\;
217	      \mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m}
218	      \qquad(\cref{def:sheafTensorObj}, \cref{def:sheafTensorPow}),
219	  \]
220	  the degree-\(m\) carrier of the section graded module. By construction
221	  \(\mathcal{F}(0) = \mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{O}_X \cong
222	  \mathcal{F}\).
223	\end{definition}
224	
225	\begin{lemma}[Tensor-power comparison isomorphism]
226	  \label{lem:sheafTensorPow_add}
227	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorPowAdd}
228	  \uses{def:sheafTensorPow, def:sheafTensorObj}
229	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Sheaves of Modules", \S "Invertible modules"
230	  % (Tag 01CU, following `definition-powers') (read from
231	  % references/stacks-modules.tex, L4249-L4254).
232	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
233	  % "With this definition we have canonical isomorphisms
234	  %  $\mathcal{L}^{\otimes n} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X}
235	  %  \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \to
236	  %  \mathcal{L}^{\otimes n + m}$, and these isomorphisms
237	  %  satisfy a commutativity and an associativity constraint
238	  %  (formulation omitted)."
239	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Sheaves of Modules", Tag 01CU (canonical
240	  power isomorphisms).}
241	  For a line bundle \(\mathcal{L}\) and \(m, m' \in \mathbb{N}\) there is a
242	  canonical isomorphism of sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules
243	  \[
244	    \mu_{m,m'} :
245	    \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'}
246	    \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\; \mathcal{L}^{\otimes (m+m')},
247	  \]
248	  natural in \(\mathcal{L}\), and these isomorphisms satisfy a commutativity
249	  constraint (\(\mu_{m,m'}\) agrees with \(\mu_{m',m}\) after the braiding) and
250	  an associativity constraint (the two ways of combining
251	  \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'} \otimes
252	  \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m''}\) into \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes (m+m'+m'')}\) agree).
253	\end{lemma}
254	
255	\begin{proof}
256	  \uses{def:sheafTensorPow, def:sheafTensorObj,
257	    lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib,
258	    lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}
259	  We do \emph{not} appeal to a monoidal structure on the whole category
260	  \(\ShMod(\mathcal{O}_X)\) --- none is available --- but only to the comparison
261	  on tensor \emph{powers} of the single invertible sheaf \(\mathcal{L}\). On such
262	  locally free factors the construction is elementary, and right-exactness of the
263	  tensor product is never invoked.
264	
265	  \textbf{A local-iso criterion.} A morphism of sheaves of
266	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules is an isomorphism as soon as it is one locally, i.e.\
267	  after restriction to each member of an open cover of \(X\): two-sided inverses
268	  defined over the members of a cover and agreeing on overlaps glue to a global
269	  inverse. We invoke this criterion \emph{only} on tensor products of sheaves
270	  that are locally free of rank one. On a trivialising open \(U\) where
271	  \(\mathcal{L}|_U \cong \mathcal{O}_U\), every tensor power restricts to
272	  \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes k}|_U \cong \mathcal{O}_U\) as well; the tensor product
273	  presheaf there takes the value \(\mathcal{O}_X(U) \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)}
274	  \mathcal{O}_X(U) = \mathcal{O}_X(U)\); and the sheafification unit \(\eta\)
275	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}) restricts over \(U\) to the
276	  identity. Hence over each such \(U\) the structural comparison maps used below
277	  --- the unitor, associator, and braiding of \cref{def:sheafTensorObj},
278	  descended objectwise from \cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib} --- are
279	  visibly bijective, and the criterion promotes them to genuine isomorphisms in
280	  \(\ShMod(\mathcal{O}_X)\). The subtlety that obstructs a general monoidal
281	  descent --- that the sheafification comparison
282	  \(\eta_{\mathcal P} \otimes \mathrm{id}\) need not be locally injective when
283	  \(\mathcal P\) is arbitrary, the tensor product being only right exact --- does
284	  not bite here: that object is never among the ones we test. The only maps we
285	  test are comparisons between locally free factors, where the unit is locally an
286	  identity and the comparison is a genuine local isomorphism.
287	
288	  \textbf{Construction of \(\mu_{m,m'}\).} Fix \(m'\) and induct on \(m\). For
289	  \(m = 0\),
290	  \[
291	    \mathcal{L}^{\otimes 0} \otimes \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'}
292	      = \mathcal{O}_X \otimes \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'}
293	      \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'}
294	  \]
295	  is the left unitor of \cref{def:sheafTensorObj}, an isomorphism by the
296	  criterion. For the inductive step the recursive definition
297	  \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes (m+1)} = \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes \mathcal{L}\)
298	  (\cref{def:sheafTensorPow}) gives
299	  \[
300	    \mathcal{L}^{\otimes (m+1)} \otimes \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'}
301	    = (\mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes \mathcal{L}) \otimes
302	      \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'}
303	    \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes
304	      (\mathcal{L} \otimes \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'})
305	    \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes
306	      (\mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'} \otimes \mathcal{L}),
307	  \]
308	  using first the associator and then the braiding of \cref{def:sheafTensorObj},
309	  each an isomorphism by the criterion. A second associator and the inductive
310	  comparison \(\mu_{m,m'}\) whiskered by \(\mathcal{L}\) then identify the
311	  right-hand side with
312	  \[
313	    (\mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'}) \otimes
314	      \mathcal{L}
315	    \xrightarrow{\ \mu_{m,m'} \otimes \mathrm{id}\ }
316	    \mathcal{L}^{\otimes (m + m')} \otimes \mathcal{L}
317	    = \mathcal{L}^{\otimes (m + m' + 1)},
318	  \]
319	  the final equality being once more the recursive definition. The composite is
320	  \(\mu_{m+1,m'}\), an isomorphism as a composite of isomorphisms. Naturality in
321	  \(\mathcal{L}\) holds because each constituent comparison is natural.
322	
323	  \textbf{Commutativity and associativity constraints.} Every map appearing in
324	  the construction is a structural comparison isomorphism between locally free
325	  factors, so the required coherences are equalities of morphisms that may be
326	  checked locally on a common trivialising cover. Over such an open every sheaf
327	  in sight becomes \(\mathcal{O}_U\) and every comparison map becomes the
328	  identity, whence both sides of the commutativity square (\(\mu_{m,m'}\) versus
329	  \(\mu_{m',m}\) postcomposed with the braiding) and of the associativity
330	  diagram (the two bracketings of
331	  \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'} \otimes
332	  \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m''} \to \mathcal{L}^{\otimes (m+m'+m'')}\)) reduce to the
333	  same local identity. Gluing the local equalities yields the global cocycle
334	  relations: the family \(\{\mu_{m,m'}\}\) is coherent in the
335	  pentagon/hexagon sense.
336	
337	  \textbf{Remark on the principled route.} One could instead obtain \(\mu\) from
338	  a genuine monoidal structure on \(\ShMod(\mathcal{O}_X)\) descended from the
339	  presheaf-level monoidal structure (\cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib})
340	  along sheafification, via the localized-monoidal / reflective-localization
341	  template for sheaf categories. That principled construction requires a
342	  monoidal-closed structure on \(\PshMod\) that is not built in this chapter, and
343	  is deferred. The bespoke line-bundle construction above is all the section
344	  graded ring requires.
345	\end{proof}
346	
347	\section{Lax-monoidal global sections}
348	\label{sec:sgr_lax_sections}
349	
350	The graded multiplication is supplied by the lax-monoidal structure of the
351	global-sections functor \(\Gamma(X, -) : \ShMod(\mathcal{O}_X) \to
352	\mathrm{ModuleCat}(\Gamma(X, \mathcal{O}_X))\). It is only \emph{lax} because
353	global sections do not commute with sheafification: a global section of a
354	sheafified tensor product is not in general a finite sum of elementary tensors of
355	global sections.
356	
357	\begin{definition}[Section multiplication]
358	  \label{def:sectionMul}
359	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.sectionsMul}
360	  \uses{def:sheafTensorObj, lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib,
361	    lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}
362	  Let \(\mathcal{F}, \mathcal{G}\) be sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. The
363	  \emph{section multiplication} is the \(\Gamma(X,\mathcal{O}_X)\)-bilinear map
364	  \[
365	    \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_{\Gamma(X,\mathcal{O}_X)}
366	      \Gamma(X, \mathcal{G})
367	    \;\longrightarrow\;
368	    \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G})
369	  \]
370	  defined as follows. A pair of global sections \((\sigma, \tau)\) determines,
371	  by the objectwise formula of \cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib} at the
372	  top open, the elementary tensor \(\sigma \otimes \tau \in
373	  (\mathcal{F} \otimes_{p,\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G})(X)\), a global section of
374	  the tensor product presheaf. Composing with the global-sections component of
375	  the sheafification unit
376	  \(\eta : \mathcal{F} \otimes_{p,\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G} \to
377	  (\mathcal{F} \otimes_{p,\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G})^{\#} =
378	  \mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G}\)
379	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib},
380	  \cref{def:sheafTensorObj}) yields a global section
381	  \(\sigma \cdot \tau \in
382	  \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G})\). Bilinearity over
383	  \(\Gamma(X,\mathcal{O}_X)\) is inherited from bilinearity of the elementary
384	  tensor and \(\Gamma(X,\mathcal{O}_X)\)-linearity of \(\eta\).
385	\end{definition}
386	
387	\begin{lemma}[Coherence of the section multiplication]
388	  \label{lem:sectionMul_coherent}
389	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.sectionsMul_assoc_unit}
390	  \uses{def:sectionMul, lem:moduleUnit_mathlib, lem:sheafTensorPow_add}
391	  The section multiplications (\cref{def:sectionMul}) are associative and unital
392	  in the following sense. For sheaves \(\mathcal{F}, \mathcal{G}, \mathcal{H}\)
393	  the two composites
394	  \[
395	    \Gamma(\mathcal{F}) \otimes \Gamma(\mathcal{G}) \otimes \Gamma(\mathcal{H})
396	    \longrightarrow
397	    \Gamma\bigl((\mathcal{F} \otimes \mathcal{G}) \otimes \mathcal{H}\bigr)
398	    \cong
399	    \Gamma\bigl(\mathcal{F} \otimes (\mathcal{G} \otimes \mathcal{H})\bigr)
400	  \]
401	  agree after transport along the associator of \cref{def:sheafTensorObj}; and
402	  the unit element \(1 \in \Gamma(X, \mathcal{O}_X)\)
403	  (\cref{lem:moduleUnit_mathlib}) acts as a two-sided identity, i.e.\ the section
404	  multiplication by \(1\) is, after the unitor isomorphism
405	  \(\mathcal{O}_X \otimes \mathcal{F} \cong \mathcal{F}\), the identity on
406	  \(\Gamma(X, \mathcal{F})\).
407	\end{lemma}
408	
409	\begin{proof}
410	  \uses{def:sectionMul, lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib,
411	    lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}
412	  Both statements reduce to the corresponding identities at the level of the
413	  tensor product presheaf, where by \cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}
414	  evaluation at the top open is strictly monoidal, so the associator and unitors
415	  act on elementary tensors by the usual formulas
416	  \((\sigma \otimes \tau) \otimes \upsilon \mapsto
417	  \sigma \otimes (\tau \otimes \upsilon)\) and
418	  \(1 \otimes \sigma \mapsto \sigma\). The sheafification unit \(\eta\) is a
419	  natural transformation, so it commutes with the associator and unitor
420	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}); applying \(\Gamma(X,-)\)
421	  to the naturality squares transports these presheaf-level identities to the
422	  section multiplications.
423	\end{proof}
424	
425	\section{Graded-ring and graded-module assembly}
426	\label{sec:sgr_graded_assembly}
427	
428	The components and multiplications of the previous two sections are assembled
429	into the graded ring and graded module through Mathlib's
430	\emph{external-direct-sum} graded API: the relevant inputs are families of
431	abelian groups indexed by \(\mathbb{N}\) together with multiplication maps
432	landing in the index-sum component, not submodules of a pre-existing ring.
433	
434	\begin{lemma}[External-direct-sum graded commutative semiring]
435	  \label{lem:directSum_gcommSemiring_mathlib}
436	  \lean{DirectSum.GCommSemiring}
437	  \mathlibok
438	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib (\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.DirectSum.Ring}).}
439	  Let \(A : \mathbb{N} \to \mathrm{Type}\) be a family of additive commutative
440	  monoids equipped with a graded multiplication
441	  \(A_i \times A_j \to A_{i+j}\) and a unit \(1 \in A_0\) satisfying graded
442	  associativity, two-sided unitality, distributivity, and graded commutativity
443	  (the data of a \(\mathrm{GCommSemiring}\) on \(A\)). Then the external direct
444	  sum \(\bigoplus_i A_i\) is a commutative semiring, with multiplication the
445	  bilinear extension of the graded multiplication and identity the image of
446	  \(1 \in A_0\).
447	\end{lemma}
448	
449	\begin{lemma}[External-direct-sum graded module]
450	  \label{lem:directSum_gmodule_mathlib}
451	  \lean{DirectSum.Gmodule}
452	  \mathlibok
453	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib (\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Module.GradedModule}).}
454	  Let \(A : \mathbb{N} \to \mathrm{Type}\) carry a graded semiring structure as
455	  in \cref{lem:directSum_gcommSemiring_mathlib} and let
456	  \(M : \mathbb{N} \to \mathrm{Type}\) be a family of additive commutative
457	  monoids with a graded scalar action \(A_i \times M_j \to M_{i+j}\) satisfying
458	  the graded module axioms (the data of a \(\mathrm{Gmodule}\) of \(M\) over
459	  \(A\)). Then the external direct sum \(\bigoplus_j M_j\) is a module over the
460	  semiring \(\bigoplus_i A_i\), with scalar multiplication the bilinear
461	  extension of the graded action.
462	\end{lemma}
463	
464	\begin{lemma}[Graded semiring structure on the section components]
465	  \label{lem:sectionGradedRing_gcommSemiring}
466	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.sectionGradedRing_gcommSemiring}
467	  \uses{def:sheafTensorPow, lem:sheafTensorPow_add, def:sectionMul,
468	    lem:sectionMul_coherent, lem:directSum_gcommSemiring_mathlib,
469	    lem:moduleUnit_mathlib}
470	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Sheaves of Modules", \S "Invertible modules",
471	  % preceding `definition-gamma-star' (Tag 01CV) (read from
472	  % references/stacks-modules.tex, L4257-L4267).
473	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
474	  % "Let $(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$ be a ringed space. We can define a $\mathbf{Z}$-graded
475	  %  ring structure on $\bigoplus \Gamma(X, \mathcal{L}^{\otimes n})$ by mapping
476	  %  $s \in \Gamma(X, \mathcal{L}^{\otimes n})$ and
477	  %  $t \in \Gamma(X, \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m})$ to the
478	  %  section corresponding to $s \otimes t$ in
479	  %  $\Gamma(X, \mathcal{L}^{\otimes n + m})$.
480	  %  We omit the verification that this defines a commutative
481	  %  and associative ring with $1$."
482	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Sheaves of Modules", Tag 01CV (the graded
483	  ring structure on \(\bigoplus \Gamma(X,\mathcal{L}^{\otimes n})\)).}
484	  Let \(L_s\) be a line bundle on \(X_s\). The family of
485	  \(\Gamma(X_s,\mathcal{O}_{X_s})\)-modules
486	  \(m \mapsto \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m})\) carries a graded commutative
487	  semiring structure (a \(\mathrm{GCommSemiring}\),
488	  \cref{lem:directSum_gcommSemiring_mathlib}) whose degree-\((m,m')\)
489	  multiplication is the composite
490	  \[
491	    \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m}) \otimes_{\Gamma(X_s,\mathcal{O}_{X_s})}
492	      \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m'})
493	    \xrightarrow{\ \cdot\ }
494	    \Gamma\bigl(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m} \otimes L_s^{\otimes m'}\bigr)
495	    \xrightarrow{\ \Gamma(\mu_{m,m'})\ }
496	    \Gamma\bigl(X_s, L_s^{\otimes (m+m')}\bigr),
497	  \]
498	  the section multiplication (\cref{def:sectionMul}) followed by the
499	  tensor-power comparison isomorphism (\cref{lem:sheafTensorPow_add}), with unit
500	  \(1 \in \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{O}_{X_s}) = \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes 0})\)
501	  (\cref{lem:moduleUnit_mathlib}). Consequently
502	  \(R(X_s, L_s) = \bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m})\) is a
503	  commutative semiring; this is the graded-ring structure underlying
504	  \cref{def:sectionGradedRing}.
505	\end{lemma}
506	
507	\begin{proof}
508	  \uses{lem:sheafTensorPow_add, lem:sectionMul_coherent,
509	    lem:directSum_gcommSemiring_mathlib}
510	  The graded multiplication maps are the composites displayed in the statement.
511	  Graded associativity is the associativity coherence of
512	  \cref{lem:sectionMul_coherent} combined with the associativity constraint of
513	  the comparison isomorphisms \(\mu\) (\cref{lem:sheafTensorPow_add}); graded
514	  commutativity is the unit/commutativity of the section multiplication combined
515	  with the commutativity constraint of \(\mu\); two-sided unitality is the unit
516	  coherence of \cref{lem:sectionMul_coherent} together with
517	  \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes 0} = \mathcal{O}_X\). These are exactly the axioms of a
518	  \(\mathrm{GCommSemiring}\), so \cref{lem:directSum_gcommSemiring_mathlib}
519	  endows \(\bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m})\) with its
520	  commutative-semiring structure.
521	\end{proof}
522	
523	\begin{lemma}[Graded module structure on the twisted-section components]
524	  \label{lem:sectionGradedModule_gmodule}
525	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.sectionGradedModule_gmodule}
526	  \uses{def:sheafModuleTwist, lem:sheafTensorPow_add, def:sectionMul,
527	    lem:sectionMul_coherent, lem:directSum_gmodule_mathlib,
528	    lem:sectionGradedRing_gcommSemiring}
529	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Sheaves of Modules", \S "Invertible modules",
530	  % `definition-gamma-star' (Tag 01CV) (read from
531	  % references/stacks-modules.tex, L4279-L4286).
532	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
533	  % "Given a sheaf of $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules $\mathcal{F}$ we set
534	  %  $$
535	  %  \Gamma_*(X, \mathcal{L}, \mathcal{F})
536	  %  =
537	  %  \bigoplus\nolimits_{n \in \mathbf{Z}} \Gamma(X,
538	  %  \mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{L}^{\otimes n})
539	  %  $$
540	  %  which we think of as a graded $\Gamma_*(X, \mathcal{L})$-module."
541	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Sheaves of Modules", Tag 01CV (the graded
542	  module \(\Gamma_*(X,\mathcal{L},\mathcal{F})\)).}
543	  Let \(\mathcal{F}_s\) be a coherent sheaf and \(L_s\) a line bundle on
544	  \(X_s\). The family
545	  \(m \mapsto \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\)
546	  (\cref{def:sheafModuleTwist}) carries a graded module structure (a
547	  \(\mathrm{Gmodule}\), \cref{lem:directSum_gmodule_mathlib}) over the graded
548	  semiring of \cref{lem:sectionGradedRing_gcommSemiring}, with degree-\((m,m')\)
549	  action
550	  \[
551	    \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m}) \otimes_{\Gamma(X_s,\mathcal{O}_{X_s})}
552	      \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m'})
553	    \xrightarrow{\ \cdot\ }
554	    \Gamma\bigl(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m} \otimes
555	      (\mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m'})\bigr)
556	    \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }
557	    \Gamma\bigl(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes (m+m')}\bigr),
558	  \]
559	  the section multiplication followed by the comparison isomorphism that
560	  reassociates and merges the line-bundle factors (using
561	  \cref{lem:sheafTensorPow_add} and the symmetry of the tensor product).
562	  Consequently \(M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s) = \bigoplus_{m \ge 0}
563	  \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\) is a graded module over
564	  \(R(X_s, L_s)\); this is the graded-module structure underlying
565	  \cref{def:sectionGradedModule}.
566	\end{lemma}
567	
568	\begin{proof}
569	  \uses{lem:sectionMul_coherent, lem:sheafTensorPow_add,
570	    lem:directSum_gmodule_mathlib, lem:sectionGradedRing_gcommSemiring}
571	  The action maps are the displayed composites. The graded-module axioms ---
572	  compatibility of the action with the ring multiplication
573	  (\(r \cdot (r' \cdot x) = (r r') \cdot x\)) and unitality
574	  (\(1 \cdot x = x\)) --- follow from the associativity and unit coherences of
575	  the section multiplication (\cref{lem:sectionMul_coherent}) together with the
576	  associativity and commutativity constraints of the comparison isomorphisms
577	  (\cref{lem:sheafTensorPow_add}), exactly as in the proof of
578	  \cref{lem:sectionGradedRing_gcommSemiring} but with the third tensor factor
579	  carrying \(\mathcal{F}_s\). These are the axioms of a \(\mathrm{Gmodule}\), so
580	  \cref{lem:directSum_gmodule_mathlib} produces the module structure of
581	  \(M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s)\) over \(R(X_s, L_s)\).
582	\end{proof}

1	\chapter{Flattening Stratification of a Coherent Sheaf}
2	\label{chap:Picard_FlatteningStratification}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/FlatteningStratification.lean
4	
5	% STRATEGY NOTE. This chapter packages the classical Grothendieck/Nitsure~\S4
6	% \emph{generic flatness} theorem: over a noetherian integral base, a coherent
7	% sheaf on a finite-type \(X \to S\) becomes flat over a non-empty open
8	% subscheme of \(S\). Both its algebraic form (generic freeness of a finite
9	% module over a finite-type algebra over a noetherian domain) and its
10	% geometric form are recorded here as the prover-ready specification for
11	% \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/FlatteningStratification.lean}.
12	%
13	% This chapter is independently parallel-startable: it does not gate on the
14	% Route~A.1.a relative-Spec chapter \cref{chap:Picard_RelativeSpec}.
15	
16	\section{Setup and motivation}
17	\label{sec:flatstrat_setup}
18	
19	Generic flatness is the foundational input to the flattening-stratification
20	construction underlying Grothendieck's existence theorem for the Quot scheme
21	(\cite{nitsure-hilbert-quot}, \S4--\S5).  Its content is that flatness of a
22	coherent sheaf is a generic property over an integral base: over a noetherian
23	integral scheme \(S\), a coherent \(\OO_X\)-module on a finite-type morphism
24	\(X \to S\) becomes flat after restricting to a non-empty open subscheme of
25	\(S\). Algebraically this is the statement that a finite module over a
26	finite-type algebra over a noetherian domain becomes free after inverting a
27	single non-zero element of the base ring.
28	
29	We work throughout with a noetherian (integral, where required) base scheme
30	\(S\), a finite-type morphism \(p : X \to S\), and a coherent \(\OO_X\)-module
31	\(\F\).
32	
33	\section{Generic flatness}
34	\label{sec:generic_flatness}
35	
36	The construction of the flattening stratification proceeds by Noetherian
37	induction on \(S\). The induction step relies on the following classical
38	\emph{generic flatness} theorem of Grothendieck: over a noetherian integral
39	base, a coherent sheaf is flat over a non-empty open subscheme. Algebraically,
40	this is the assertion that a finitely generated module over a finite-type
41	algebra over a noetherian domain is generically free after inverting a single
42	non-zero element of the base ring.
43	
44	\begin{theorem}
45	
46	
47	
48	\leanok
49	[Generic flatness, algebraic form]
50	  \label{thm:generic_flatness_algebraic}
51	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.genericFlatnessAlgebraic}
52	  % NOTE (iter-022): CLOSED, axiom-clean {propext, Classical.choice, Quot.sound}.
53	  % The Lean decl is single-universe `(A B M : Type u)` (was `Type*`): L5 and
54	  % `free_localizationAway_of_away_tower` are single-universe, and the dévissage
55	  % applies them to `C = B ⧸ 𝔭`, forcing A, B into one universe. Faithful for the
56	  % geometric use (scheme patches are single-universe).
57	  \uses{lem:gf_torsion_base, lem:noeth_prime_filtration, lem:gf_splice_shortExact,
58	        lem:gf_noether_clear_denominators, lem:gf_polynomial_core, lem:gf_finite_module}
59	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness''
60	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1711-L1716).
61	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
62	  % "Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain,
63	  %  and \(B\) a finite type \(A\) algebra. Let \(M\) be a finite \(B\)-module.
64	  %  Then there exists an \(f\in A\), \(f\ne 0\), such that
65	  %  the localisation \(M_f\) is a free module over \(A_f\)."
66	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness''.}
67	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain, \(B\) a finite-type \(A\)-algebra, and \(M\) a finite
68	  \(B\)-module. Then there exists \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\), such that the localisation
69	  \(M_f\) is a free \(A_f\)-module.
70	
71	\end{theorem}
72	
73	% INTENDED LEAN SIGNATURE (the scaffold pass creates the decl pinned above with
74	% exactly this signature):
75	%   (A B M : Type u) [CommRing A] [IsDomain A] [IsNoetherianRing A]   % single-universe (iter-022)
76	%     [CommRing B] [Algebra A B] [Algebra.FiniteType A B]
77	%     [AddCommGroup M] [Module B M] [Module.Finite B M]
78	%     [Module A M] [IsScalarTower A B M] :
79	%     ∃ f : A, f ≠ 0 ∧
80	%       Module.Free (Localization.Away f) (LocalizedModule (Submonoid.powers f) M)
81	
82	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Over the quotient field \(K\) of \(A\), the \(K\)-algebra
83	% \(B_K = K\otimes _AB\) is of finite type, and \(M_K = K\otimes_AM\) is a
84	% finite module over \(B_K\). Let \(n\) be the dimension of the support
85	% of \(M_K\) over \(\spec B_K\). We argue by induction on \(n\), starting
86	% with \(n=-1\) which is the case when \(M_K =0\). [...]
87	% Now let \(n\ge 0\), and let the lemma be proved for smaller values.
88	% As \(B\) is noetherian and \(M\) is assumed to be a finite \(B\)-module,
89	% there exists a finite filtration
90	% \(0=M_0 \subset \ldots  \subset M_r =M\)
91	% where each \(M_i\) is a \(B\)-submodule of \(M\) such that for each \(i\ge 1\)
92	% the quotient module \(M_i/M_{i-1}\) is isomorphic to \(B/\pp_i\) for
93	% some prime ideal \(\pp_i\) in \(B\). [...] By Noether normalisation lemma,
94	% there exist elements \(b_1,\ldots,b_n \in B\), such that \(K\otimes_AB\) is
95	% finite over its subalgebra \(K[b_1,\ldots, b_n]\) [...]"
96	% (Source: nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1719-L1772; full
97	% proof omitted for length, structural steps restated below in project notation.)
98	\begin{proof}
99	
100	  \textbf{Primary route — the finite \(A\)-module case.}
101	  Suppose first that \(M\) is module-finite over \(A\) itself. Since \(A\) is noetherian,
102	  \(M\) is finitely presented over \(A\). The generic fibre \(M_K = K \otimes_A M\)
103	  (where \(K = \mathrm{Frac}(A)\)) is a finite-dimensional \(K\)-vector space, hence free
104	  over \(K\); by \cref{lem:gf_finite_module} this freeness descends to a single basic open:
105	  there exists \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\), with \(M_f\) free over \(A_f\). This closes the
106	  theorem whenever \(M\) is finite over \(A\).
107	
108	  \textbf{Surviving residue — reducing the finite-type \(B\)-algebra case to the
109	  finite \(A\)-module case.} The genuine content not supplied by Mathlib is the
110	  reduction of the general hypothesis (\(M\) finite over a \emph{finite-type}
111	  \(A\)-algebra \(B\)) to the finite \(A\)-module situation handled above. Concretely,
112	  the missing piece is the polynomial-ring core of generic freeness: a finite module
113	  over \(A[b_1, \dots, b_n]\) becomes free over \(A_f\) after inverting a single
114	  \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\). The classical \S4 dévissage below is the fallback route
115	  for exactly this residue.
116	
117	  \emph{Fallback (Nitsure \S4 induction).}
118	  The \S4 dévissage is assembled from the sub-lemmas of
119	  \cref{sec:gf_devissage_chain}. If \(M_K = 0\), the torsion base case
120	  \cref{lem:gf_torsion_base} gives \(f \ne 0\) with \(M_f = 0\) free. Otherwise the
121	  prime-filtration induction principle \cref{lem:noeth_prime_filtration} over the
122	  noetherian ring \(B\), combined with the splicing fact
123	  \cref{lem:gf_splice_shortExact}, reduces to \(M = B/\mathfrak{p}\) and hence to
124	  \(B\) a domain, \(M = B\); Noether normalisation with clearing of denominators
125	  \cref{lem:gf_noether_clear_denominators} makes \(B_g\) finite over a polynomial
126	  ring \(A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n]\), and the polynomial-ring core
127	  \cref{lem:gf_polynomial_core} — bottoming out at the finite-\(A\)-module leaf
128	  \cref{lem:gf_finite_module} of the primary route — produces the required
129	  \(f \ne 0\) with \(M_f\) free over \(A_f\).
130	\end{proof}
131	
132	\subsection{Dévissage chain for the finite-type residue}
133	\label{sec:gf_devissage_chain}
134	
135	The following sub-lemmas decompose the surviving residue of
136	\cref{thm:generic_flatness_algebraic} — the case where \(M\) is finite over the
137	finite-type \(A\)-algebra \(B\) but not module-finite over \(A\) — into the
138	classical \cite{nitsure-hilbert-quot}~\S4 induction. They are ordered so that each block
139	depends only on earlier blocks or Mathlib lemmas. Throughout,
140	\(A\) is a noetherian domain with fraction field \(K = \mathrm{Frac}(A)\), \(B\) a
141	finite-type \(A\)-algebra, and \(M\) a finite \(B\)-module with the compatible
142	\(A\)-module structure via the scalar tower \(A \to B \to M\).
143	
144	\begin{lemma}\leanok
145	[Finite-module leaf — finite \(A\)-module case]
146	  \label{lem:gf_finite_module}
147	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.exists_free_localizationAway_of_finite}
148	  \uses{lem:fp_free_descent}
149	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain and \(M\) a finite \(A\)-module. Then there
150	  exists \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\), such that \(M_f\) is free over \(A_f\).
151	\end{lemma}
152	
153	\begin{proof}
154	
155	  This is the \(d = 0\) leaf of the induction. A finite module over a noetherian ring is
156	  finitely presented, so
157	  \(M\) is finitely presented over \(A\). Over the generic point
158	  \(K = \mathrm{Frac}(A) = S^{-1}A\) (\(S = A \setminus \{0\}\)) the localisation
159	  \(M_K = S^{-1}M\) is a finite-dimensional \(K\)-vector space, hence free over
160	  \(K\). The finite-presentation free-descent lemma \cref{lem:fp_free_descent}
161	  then descends this freeness to a single basic open: there is \(r \in S\), i.e.\
162	  \(r \in A\) with \(r \ne 0\), such that \(M_r\) is free over \(A_r\). Take
163	  \(f := r\).
164	\end{proof}
165	
166	\begin{lemma}[Finite-presentation free descent]
167	  \label{lem:fp_free_descent}
168	  \lean{Module.FinitePresentation.exists_free_localizedModule_powers}
169	  \mathlibok
170	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
171	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring, \(S \subseteq R\) a multiplicative subset, and
172	  \(M\) a finitely-presented \(R\)-module such that the localisation \(M_S\) is
173	  free over the localisation \(R_S\). Then there exists \(r \in S\) such that
174	  \(M_r\) is free over \(R_r\) (of the same finite rank).
175	\end{lemma}
176	
177	\begin{lemma}\leanok
178	[L1 — torsion base case \(M_K = 0\)]
179	  \label{lem:gf_torsion_base}
180	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.exists_free_localizationAway_of_torsion}
181	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'', proof, base case
182	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1719-L1726).
183	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
184	  % "We argue by induction on $n$, starting with $n=-1$ which is the case when
185	  %  $M_K =0$. In this case, as $K\otimes _AM = S^{-1}M$ where $S = A - \{ 0\}$,
186	  %  each $v\in M$ is annihilated by some non-zero element of $A$. Taking a finite
187	  %  generating set, and a common multiple of corresponding annihilating elements,
188	  %  we see there exists an $f\ne 0$ in $A$ with $fM=0$. Hence $M_f =0$, proving
189	  %  the lemma when $n=-1$."
190	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'' (base case of the
191	  induction).}
192	  Suppose \(K \otimes_A M = 0\), where \(K = \mathrm{Frac}(A)\). Then there exists
193	  \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\), with \(f M = 0\); consequently \(M_f = 0\) is free over
194	  \(A_f\).
195	\end{lemma}
196	
197	\begin{proof}
198	
199	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1719-L1726).
200	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (base case).}
201	  Since \(K \otimes_A M = S^{-1}M\) with \(S = A \setminus \{0\}\), the hypothesis
202	  \(M_K = 0\) means every element of \(M\) is killed by some non-zero element of
203	  \(A\). Choose a finite \(B\)-generating set \(v_1, \dots, v_k\) of \(M\) (which
204	  exists because \(M\) is a finite \(B\)-module), and for each \(i\) an
205	  \(a_i \in A\), \(a_i \ne 0\), with \(a_i v_i = 0\). The product
206	  \(f := a_1 \cdots a_k\) is non-zero (\(A\) is a domain) and annihilates every
207	  generator, hence \(f M = 0\). Therefore \(M_f = 0\), which is free over \(A_f\).
208	\end{proof}
209	
210	\begin{lemma}[L2 — prime-filtration induction principle]
211	  \label{lem:noeth_prime_filtration}
212	  \lean{IsNoetherianRing.induction_on_isQuotientEquivQuotientPrime}
213	  \mathlibok
214	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
215	  Let \(B\) be a noetherian commutative ring and let \(P\) be a predicate on finite
216	  \(B\)-modules that (i) holds for every subsingleton module, (ii) holds for every
217	  cyclic quotient \(B/\mathfrak{p}\) with \(\mathfrak{p}\) prime, and (iii) is
218	  closed under short exact sequences (\(P(N_1)\) and \(P(N_3)\) imply \(P(N_2)\)
219	  for \(0 \to N_1 \to N_2 \to N_3 \to 0\)). Then \(P\) holds for every finite
220	  \(B\)-module. In particular every finite \(B\)-module \(M\) admits a finite
221	  filtration \(0 = M_0 \subset \cdots \subset M_r = M\) with each quotient
222	  \(M_i / M_{i-1} \cong B/\mathfrak{p}_i\) for a prime \(\mathfrak{p}_i\), so to
223	  prove generic freeness for \(M\) it suffices to prove it for each
224	  \(B/\mathfrak{p}\) and to splice along the filtration's short exact sequences.
225	\end{lemma}
226	
227	\begin{lemma}\leanok
228	[L3a — localisation of a short exact sequence is exact]
229	  \label{lem:gf_splice_shortExact_localized_exact}
230	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.exact_localizedModule_powers_of_shortExact}
231	  Let \(0 \to M' \xrightarrow{i} M \xrightarrow{q} M'' \to 0\) be a short exact
232	  sequence of \(B\)-modules, and let \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\). Restricting scalars
233	  along \(A \to B\) and localising every term at the powers of \(f\) yields a short
234	  exact sequence
235	  \[
236	    0 \to M'_f \xrightarrow{i_f} M_f \xrightarrow{q_f} M''_f \to 0
237	  \]
238	  of \(A_f\)-modules; in particular \(i_f\) is injective and \(q_f\) is surjective.
239	\end{lemma}
240	
241	\begin{proof}
242	
243	  Localisation at a multiplicative set is an exact functor, preserving injections,
244	  surjections, and exactness. Applying it to the scalar-restricted \(A\)-linear maps
245	  \(i\) and \(q\) (via \(A \to B\)) returns the displayed sequence.
246	\end{proof}
247	
248	\begin{lemma}\leanok
249	[L3b — free transport across a finer localisation]
250	  \label{lem:gf_splice_shortExact_free_transport}
251	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.free_localizationAway_of_free_of_eq_mul}
252	  Let \(N\) be a \(B\)-module and \(f', f'' \in A\) with \(f = f' f''\). If the
253	  localisation \(N_{f'}\) is free over \(A_{f'}\), then the further localisation
254	  \(N_f\) is free over \(A_f\).
255	\end{lemma}
256	
257	\begin{proof}
258	
259	  Because \(f = f' f''\), the localisation away from \(f\) factors through the
260	  localisation away from \(f'\): the ring \(A_f\) is the localisation of \(A_{f'}\)
261	  at the image of \(f''\), and likewise the module \(N_f\) is the localisation of
262	  \(N_{f'}\) at the image of \(f''\). A localisation of a free module is free over
263	  the localised base ring — a basis of \(N_{f'}\) over \(A_{f'}\) maps to a basis of
264	  \(N_f\) over \(A_f\). Hence \(N_f\) is free over \(A_f\). Stated as a standalone
265	  fact, it is applied to both ends of the spliced sequence.
266	\end{proof}
267	
268	\begin{lemma}\leanok
269	[L3c — a short exact sequence with free ends has a free middle]
270	  \label{lem:gf_splice_shortExact_split}
271	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.free_of_shortExact_of_free_free}
272	  Let \(0 \to M'_f \to M_f \to M''_f \to 0\) be a short exact sequence of
273	  \(A_f\)-modules in which both ends \(M'_f\) and \(M''_f\) are free over \(A_f\).
274	  Then \(M_f\) is free over \(A_f\); concretely \(M_f \cong M'_f \oplus M''_f\).
275	\end{lemma}
276	
277	\begin{proof}
278	
279	  A free module is projective, so the free quotient \(M''_f\) is projective and the
280	  short exact sequence splits: \(M_f \cong M'_f \oplus M''_f\). A finite direct sum
281	  of free \(A_f\)-modules is free, hence \(M_f\) is free over \(A_f\).
282	\end{proof}

295	  %  $M'_{f'}$ and $M''_{f''}$ are free over respectively $A_{f'}$ and $A_{f''}$,

301	  Let \(0 \to M' \to M \to M'' \to 0\) be a short exact sequence of \(B\)-modules,
302	  and let \(f', f'' \in A\) be non-zero with \(M'_{f'}\) free over \(A_{f'}\) and

314	  of \(f\) (after restricting scalars to \(A\)) produces a short exact sequence

367	  % NOTE (encoding): there is NO canonical `Algebra (Localization.Away g)
368	  %   (FractionRing A)` instance, so the landed Lean does not use the literal
369	  %   `algebraMap (Localization.Away g) (FractionRing A)` above. It encodes the same map
370	  %   as `IsLocalization.map (FractionRing A) (RingHom.id A) hle` with
371	  %   `hle : powers g ≤ comap id (nonZeroDivisors A)` — mathematically identical, just the
372	  %   well-typed form. The proof is axiom-clean.
373	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'' (common denominator).}
374	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain with fraction field \(K = \mathrm{Frac}(A)\), and let
375	  \(p \in K[X_1, \dots, X_n]\) be a polynomial with coefficients in \(K\) (for instance,
376	  one coefficient polynomial of an equation of integral dependence over
377	  \(K[b_1, \dots, b_n]\)). Then there exists \(g \in A\), \(g \ne 0\), such that \(p\)
378	  lies in the image of the coefficient-extension map
379	  \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n] \to K[X_1, \dots, X_n]\); equivalently, \(p\) has all of its
380	  coefficients in \(A_g = A[g^{-1}] \subseteq K\).
381	\end{lemma}
382	
383	\begin{proof}
384	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1755-L1759).
385	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (common denominator).}
386	  The polynomial \(p\) has finitely many non-zero coefficients
387	  \(c_1, \dots, c_r \in K = \mathrm{Frac}(A)\). Each \(c_j\) is a fraction
388	  \(c_j = a_j / s_j\) with \(a_j \in A\) and \(s_j \in A \setminus \{0\}\). Put
389	  \(g := s_1 \cdots s_r \in A\); since \(A\) is a domain and each \(s_j \ne 0\), also
390	  \(g \ne 0\). For each \(j\) the denominator \(s_j\) divides \(g\), so
391	  \(c_j = a_j / s_j\) lies in the subring \(A_g = A[g^{-1}] \subseteq K\). Hence every
392	  coefficient of \(p\) lies in \(A_g\), and \(p\) is the coefficient-extension of a
393	  polynomial in \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n]\).
394	\end{proof}

428	  % generators b_j are φ(X_j). Compared with an earlier draft using three anonymous
429	  % instance existentials (∃ (_ : Algebra A_g B_g) (_ : Algebra (MvPoly … A_g) B_g)
430	  % (_ : IsScalarTower …)), this form keeps only the single `Algebra A_g B_g` binder

439	  % inverting g (see the SOURCE QUOTE PROOF below).
440	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'', proof, normalisation step
441	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1747-L1758).
442	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
443	  % "By Noether normalisation lemma, there exist elements $b_1,\ldots,b_n \in B$,
444	  %  such that $K\otimes_AB$ is finite over its subalgebra $K[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$ and
445	  %  the elements $b_1,\ldots, b_n$ are algebraically independent over $K$. [...]
446	  %  If $g\ne 0$ in $A$ is chosen to be a `common denominator' for coefficients of
447	  %  equations of integral dependence satisfied by a finite set of algebra
448	  %  generators for $K\otimes_AB$ over $K[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$, we see that $B_g$ is
449	  %  finite over $A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$."
450	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'' (Noether
451	  normalisation with denominators).}
452	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain with fraction field \(K\) and \(B\) a finite-type
453	  \(A\)-algebra that is a domain with \(B_K = K \otimes_A B \ne 0\). Write
454	  \(A_g = A[g^{-1}]\) and \(B_g = B[g^{-1}]\) for the localisations away from \(g\)
455	  and its image. Then there exist a natural number \(n\), an element \(g \in A\) with
456	  \(g \ne 0\), and an injective \(A_g\)-algebra homomorphism
457	  \[
458	    \varphi : A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n] \longrightarrow B_g,
459	  \]
460	  where \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n]\) is the polynomial ring on \(n\) algebraically
461	  independent variables, such that \(B_g\) is module-finite over the image polynomial
462	  subalgebra via \(\varphi\). The injectivity of \(\varphi\) records the algebraic
463	  independence of the generators \(b_j := \varphi(X_j)\), and module-finiteness is
464	  taken over the algebra structure that \(\varphi\) puts on \(B_g\).
465	\end{lemma}
466	
467	\begin{proof}
468	  \uses{lem:noether_normalization_fg, lem:gf_clear_one_denominator}
469	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1747-L1758).
470	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (Noether normalisation with denominators).}
471	  % NOTE: the denominator-clearing middle of this proof is a Finset-fold over the finite
472	  % generating set sigma, but each fold step is the Mathlib collapsing lemma
473	  % IsIntegral.exists_multiple_integral_of_isLocalization applied to the generator AS A
474	  % WHOLE (not to its individual coefficient polynomials via gf_clear_one_denominator).
475	  % For each generator x, that lemma yields a single a_x in A^0 (nonzero, since A is a
476	  % domain) with C(a_x) * (1 (x) x) integral over MvPolynomial A; set g1 := prod_x a_x and
477	  % g := g0 * g1. The AlgHom assembly below then runs over A_g exactly as before.
478	
479	  \emph{Step 1 — Noether normalisation over \(K\).} Apply Noether normalisation over the
480	  field \(K\) (\cref{lem:noether_normalization_fg}) to the finite-type \(K\)-algebra
481	  \(B_K = K \otimes_A B\): there are algebraically independent
482	  \(\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n \in B_K\) with \(B_K\) module-finite over
483	  \(K[\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n]\). Clearing denominators, each \(\bar b_j\) is
484	  represented by some \(b_j \in B\) (writing \(1 \otimes b\) as \(b\)).
485	
486	  \emph{Step 2 — clear the integral-dependence denominators to a single \(g\).} Fix a
487	  finite set \(\sigma\) of algebra generators of \(B_K\) over
488	  \(K[\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n]\). By Step 1 each generator's image
489	  \(1 \otimes x \in B_K\) is integral over \(K[\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n]\). For each
490	  \(x \in \sigma\), a standard integrality-and-localization descent argument yields a
491	  non-zero \(a_x \in A\) such that \(a_x \cdot (1 \otimes x)\)
492	  is integral over \(A[b_1, \dots, b_n]\). Multiplying over the finite
493	  set, set \(g_1 := \prod_{x \in \sigma} a_x\) and \(g := g_0 \cdot g_1\) (with \(g_0\) the
494	  Step-1 denominator witness). After inverting \(g\), every chosen generator becomes
495	  integral over \(A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n]\) and the generators generate \(B_g\) as an algebra;
496	  hence \(B_g\) is module-finite over \(A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n]\). (The single-coefficient
497	  lemma \cref{lem:gf_clear_one_denominator} provides the same denominator-clearing primitive
498	  at the level of one coefficient polynomial; the proof discharges a whole generator
499	  at once using this integrality-descent step, folding that primitive over the coefficients
500	  internally.)
501	
502	  \emph{Step 3 — AlgHom assembly.} The algebra homomorphism \(\varphi\) of the statement
503	  is the \(A_g\)-algebra map \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n] \to B_g\) sending \(X_j \mapsto b_j\),
504	  where \(b_j \in B_g\) is the image of \(b_j \in B\). We establish injectivity and
505	  module-finiteness through a comparison with the situation over \(K\).
506	
507	  \emph{Step 3a — the comparison map \(\nu : B_g \to B_K\).} Since \(g \ne 0\) in the
508	  domain \(A\) and \(A \subseteq K\), the image of \(g\) is a unit of the field \(K\), so
509	  it is invertible in \(B_K = K \otimes_A B\). Hence the canonical \(A\)-algebra map
510	  \(B \to B_K\), \(b \mapsto 1 \otimes b\), sends \(g\) to a unit and, by the universal
511	  property of localisation, factors through a canonical \(A\)-algebra map
512	  \[
513	    \nu : B_g = B[g^{-1}] \longrightarrow B_K, \qquad \nu(b/g^k) = (1 \otimes b)\,(1 \otimes g)^{-k}.
514	  \]
515	  The map \(\nu\) is injective. Indeed, \(B\) is a domain, and both \(B_g\) and \(B_K\) are
516	  localisations of \(B\) at multiplicative subsets of the non-zerodivisors: \(B_g\) inverts
517	  the powers of \(g\), while \(B_K = K \otimes_A B\) inverts (in particular) the images of
518	  all non-zero elements of \(A\), a multiplicative set containing the powers of \(g\). A
519	  localisation of a domain at a set of non-zerodivisors is injective into any further such
520	  localisation. Concretely, the natural map \(B \to B_K\) is injective on the domain \(B\)
521	  (its kernel is the set of \(b\) annihilated by some non-zero element of \(A\), which in a
522	  domain over which \(A\) acts faithfully is \(0\)), so \(\nu(b/g^k) = 0\) forces
523	  \(1 \otimes b = 0\), hence \(b = 0\) and \(b/g^k = 0\). Under \(\nu\) the lifts
524	  \(b_j \in B_g\) of the Noether generators map to the elements \(\bar b_j \in B_K\) of
525	  Step 1: \(\nu(b_j) = \bar b_j\).
526	
527	  \emph{Step 3b — injectivity of \(\varphi\) by algebraic-independence descent
528	  \(K \to A_g\).} Injectivity of the \(A_g\)-algebra map
529	  \(\varphi : A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n] \to B_g\), \(X_j \mapsto b_j\), is equivalent to the
530	  algebraic independence of the family \((b_j)\) over \(A_g\): a non-zero element of the
531	  kernel is exactly a non-trivial \(A_g\)-polynomial relation \(P(b_1, \dots, b_n) = 0\).
532	  The localisation \(A_g = A[g^{-1}] \subseteq K\) embeds in the fraction field, giving an
533	  injection of \(A_g\)-algebras \(A_g \hookrightarrow K\). Now suppose
534	  \(P \in A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n]\) is non-zero with \(P(b_1, \dots, b_n) = 0\) in \(B_g\).
535	  Applying the injective map \(\nu\) and using \(\nu(b_j) = \bar b_j\) together with the fact
536	  that \(\nu\) is an \(A\)-algebra map extending scalars to \(K\), we obtain
537	  \(P^K(\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n) = 0\) in \(B_K\), where \(P^K\) is the image of \(P\)
538	  under the coefficient inclusion \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n] \hookrightarrow K[X_1, \dots, X_n]\).
539	  Since \(A_g \hookrightarrow K\) is injective, \(P \ne 0\) gives \(P^K \ne 0\); but the
540	  \(\bar b_j\) are algebraically independent over \(K\) (Step 1), so the only \(K\)-polynomial
541	  vanishing on them is \(0\), a contradiction. Hence no non-trivial relation exists, \((b_j)\)
542	  is algebraically independent over \(A_g\), and \(\varphi\) is injective.
543	
544	  \emph{Step 3c — module-finiteness of \(B_g\) over \(\operatorname{im}\varphi\).} By Step 1,
545	  \(B_K\) is module-finite over \(K[\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n]\); fix a finite set of algebra
546	  generators of \(B_K\) over \(K[\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n]\) (Step 2). Each such generator
547	  satisfies a monic equation of integral dependence whose coefficients are polynomials in
548	  \(K[\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n]\). The element \(g\) of Step 2 was chosen as a common
549	  denominator for the (finitely many) coefficient polynomials of these equations, so that
550	  after inverting \(g\) every such coefficient already lies in
551	  \(A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n] = \operatorname{im}\varphi\). Each chosen generator is the
552	  \(\nu\)-image of an element of \(B_g\), and its integral-dependence equation is the
553	  \(\nu\)-image of the corresponding monic equation holding in \(B_g\) with coefficients in
554	  \(\operatorname{im}\varphi\); since \(\nu\) is injective, that equation holds in \(B_g\)
555	  itself. Thus each generator of \(B_g\) is integral over the subalgebra
556	  \(\operatorname{im}\varphi\), and these generators generate \(B_g\) as an
557	  \(\operatorname{im}\varphi\)-algebra. A finite-type algebra that is integral over a
558	  subalgebra is module-finite over it; hence \(B_g\) is module-finite over
559	  \(\operatorname{im}\varphi = A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n]\). Transported along the injection
560	  \(\varphi\), this is exactly module-finiteness of \(B_g\) over \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n]\) for
561	  the algebra structure that \(\varphi\) puts on \(B_g\), as claimed. This recovers the
562	  source's conclusion that \(B_g\) is finite over \(A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n]\).
563	\end{proof}

565	\begin{lemma}\leanok
566	[injectivity of a localisation lift]
567	  \label{lem:gf_isLocalization_lift_injective}
568	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.isLocalization_lift_injective}
569	  Let \(S\) be a localisation of a ring \(R\) at a multiplicative set \(M\), and let
570	  \(g : R \to P\) be a ring homomorphism sending every element of \(M\) to a unit of
571	  \(P\), so that it induces the lift \(\widehat g : S \to P\). If both the localisation
572	  map \(R \to S\) and \(g\) itself are injective, then \(\widehat g\) is injective.
573	\end{lemma}
574	\begin{proof}
575	  The lift is characterised by \(\widehat g(r/m) = g(r)\,g(m)^{-1}\). By the universal
576	  criterion for injectivity of a localisation lift, \(\widehat g\) is injective iff for
577	  all \(r_1, r_2 \in R\) the equality of localisation images
578	  \((r_1 \mapsto S) = (r_2 \mapsto S)\) implies \(g(r_1) = g(r_2)\). Both the hypothesis
579	  and the conclusion of that implication reduce, via injectivity of \(R \to S\) and of
580	  \(g\), to the single condition \(r_1 = r_2\); hence the implication holds and
581	  \(\widehat g\) is injective. (This helper is applied twice in
582	  \cref{lem:gf_noether_clear_denominators}, to the comparison maps \(\nu : B_g \to B_K\)
583	  and \(\psi : A_g \to K\).)
584	\end{proof}

586	\begin{lemma}

589	\leanok
590	[L5a — the generic-rank short exact sequence]
591	  \label{lem:gf_generic_rank_ses}
592	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.gf_generic_rank_ses}
593	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'', proof, inductive step
594	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1760-L1765).
595	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
596	  % "Let $m$ be the generic rank of the finite module $B_g$ over the
597	  %  domain $A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$. Then we have a short exact
598	  %  sequence of $A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$-modules of the form
599	  %  $$0\to A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]^{\oplus m} \to B_g \to T \to 0$$
600	  %  where $T$ is a finite torsion module over $A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$."
601	  % LEAN SIGNATURE (the scaffold creates the decl pinned above with this signature).
602	  %   Write P_d := MvPolynomial (Fin d) A.
603	  %     (A : Type*) [CommRing A] [IsDomain A] [IsNoetherianRing A]
604	  %       (d : ℕ) (N : Type*) [AddCommGroup N]
605	  %       [Module (MvPolynomial (Fin d) A) N]
606	  %       [Module.Finite (MvPolynomial (Fin d) A) N]
607	  %       [Module A N] [IsScalarTower A (MvPolynomial (Fin d) A) N] :
608	  %       ∃ (m : ℕ) (φ : (Fin m → MvPolynomial (Fin d) A)
609	  %                        →ₗ[MvPolynomial (Fin d) A] N),
610	  %         Function.Injective φ ∧
611	  %         Module.IsTorsion (MvPolynomial (Fin d) A) (N ⧸ LinearMap.range φ)
612	  %   witness: m = Module.finrank (FractionRing P_d)
613	  %                  (LocalizedModule (nonZeroDivisors P_d) N), the generic rank;
614	  %            φ = Fintype.linearCombination P_d v, with v : Fin m → N the
615	  %            denominator-cleared lifts of a FractionRing P_d-basis of the localisation.
616	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'' (generic-rank SES).}
617	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain, \(d \ge 0\), and \(N\) a finite module over the
618	  polynomial ring \(P_d := A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\) (with its induced \(A\)-module structure
619	  through the scalar tower \(A \to P_d \to N\)). Then there exist \(m \in \mathbb{N}\) and
620	  an injective \(P_d\)-linear map \(\varphi : P_d^{\oplus m} \to N\) whose cokernel
621	  \(T := N / \operatorname{im}\varphi\) is a torsion \(P_d\)-module. Equivalently, there is
622	  a short exact sequence of \(P_d\)-modules
623	  \[
624	    0 \to P_d^{\oplus m} \xrightarrow{\ \varphi\ } N \to T \to 0,
625	    \qquad T \text{ torsion}.
626	  \]
627	
628	  The integer \(m\) is the \emph{generic rank} of \(N\): the dimension of the
629	  \(\mathrm{Frac}(P_d)\)-vector space
630	  \(\mathrm{Frac}(P_d) \otimes_{P_d} N\), i.e.\ of the localisation of \(N\) at the
631	  non-zero-divisors of the domain \(P_d\). This step lives over \(P_d\) directly —
632	  \emph{no inversion of any element \(g \in A\) is required here}; the element \(g\) of
633	  Nitsure's argument enters only at the reindexing step \cref{lem:gf_torsion_reindex}.
634	\end{lemma}
635	
636	\begin{proof}
637	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1760-L1765).
638	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (generic-rank SES).}
639	  Write \(P_d = A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\); since \(A\) is a noetherian domain, so is \(P_d\)
640	  (Hilbert basis theorem, and a polynomial ring over a domain is a domain). Let
641	  \(Q = \mathrm{Frac}(P_d)\) be its fraction field and
642	  \(N_Q := Q \otimes_{P_d} N\) the localisation of \(N\) at the non-zero-divisors of
643	  \(P_d\); as \(N\) is finite over \(P_d\), \(N_Q\) is a finite-dimensional
644	  \(Q\)-vector space. Put \(m := \dim_Q N_Q\), the generic rank.
645	
646	  Choose a \(Q\)-basis of \(N_Q\) and clear denominators: each basis vector is a
647	  \(Q\)-unit multiple of the image of some \(v_i \in N\), so the images of
648	  \(v_1, \dots, v_m \in N\) again form a \(Q\)-basis of \(N_Q\). In particular the family
649	  \((v_i)_{i}\) is linearly independent over \(Q\); restricting scalars along the
650	  injection \(P_d \hookrightarrow Q\), it is linearly independent over \(P_d\). Let
651	  \(\varphi : P_d^{\oplus m} \to N\) be the \(P_d\)-linear map
652	  \((c_1, \dots, c_m) \mapsto \sum_i c_i v_i\). Linear independence of \((v_i)\) over
653	  \(P_d\) makes \(\varphi\) injective.
654	
655	  Set \(T := N / \operatorname{im}\varphi\). Because the images of \(v_1, \dots, v_m\)
656	  already span \(N_Q\) over \(Q\), the localised map \(\varphi \otimes Q : Q^{\oplus m}
657	  \to N_Q\) is an isomorphism, so \(Q \otimes_{P_d} T = 0\). A finite \(P_d\)-module whose
658	  localisation at the non-zero-divisors of \(P_d\) vanishes is torsion; hence \(T\) is a
659	  torsion \(P_d\)-module. This is the asserted short exact sequence.
660	\end{proof}

672	\begin{lemma}[L5b anchor — a finite torsion module has a non-zero-divisor annihilator]
673	  \label{lem:annihilator_meets_nonZeroDivisors}
674	  \lean{Submodule.annihilator_top_inter_nonZeroDivisors}
675	  \mathlibok
676	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'', proof, base case $n=-1$
677	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1724-L1727)
678	  % -- the same torsion-annihilation primitive, here provided in general by Mathlib.
679	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
680	  % "each $v\in M$ is annihilated by some non-zero element of $A$. Taking a finite

690	\begin{lemma}\leanok
691	[L5b.1 — annihilator extraction for the torsion module]
692	  \label{lem:gf_torsion_annihilator}
693	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.gf_torsion_annihilator}
694	  \uses{lem:annihilator_meets_nonZeroDivisors}
695	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'', proof, base case $n=-1$
696	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1724-L1727).
697	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
698	  % "each $v\in M$ is annihilated by some non-zero element of $A$. Taking a finite
699	  %  generating set, and a common multiple of corresponding annihilating
700	  %  elements, we see there exists an $f\ne 0$ in $A$ with $fM=0$."
701	  % LEAN SIGNATURE (the scaffold creates the decl pinned above with this signature).
702	  %   Write P_d := MvPolynomial (Fin d) A. The conclusion is the existence of a
703	  %   non-zero annihilator; the downstream consequence "T is finite over P_d/(F)" is
704	  %   left to the assembly (a surjection P_d ↠ P_d/(F) pushes a finite generating set).
705	  %     (A : Type*) [CommRing A] [IsDomain A] [IsNoetherianRing A] (d : ℕ)
706	  %       (T : Type*) [AddCommGroup T] [Module (MvPolynomial (Fin d) A) T]
707	  %       [Module.Finite (MvPolynomial (Fin d) A) T]
708	  %       (htors : Module.IsTorsion (MvPolynomial (Fin d) A) T) :
709	  %       ∃ F : MvPolynomial (Fin d) A, F ≠ 0 ∧
710	  %         F ∈ Module.annihilator (MvPolynomial (Fin d) A) T
711	  %   (F ∈ annihilator unfolds to ∀ x : T, F • x = 0.)
712	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'' (torsion annihilation).}
713	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain, \(d \ge 0\), and \(T\) a finite \emph{torsion} module
714	  over \(P_d := A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\). Then there exists a non-zero
715	  \(F \in \operatorname{Ann}_{P_d}(T)\); consequently the \(P_d\)-action on \(T\) factors
716	  through \(P_d/(F)\) and \(T\) is a finite module over \(P_d/(F)\).
717	\end{lemma}
718	
719	\begin{proof}
720	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1724-L1727).
721	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (torsion annihilation).}
722	  The ring \(P_d = A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\) is a noetherian domain (a polynomial ring over the
723	  noetherian domain \(A\)), and \(T\) is a finite torsion \(P_d\)-module. By
724	  \cref{lem:annihilator_meets_nonZeroDivisors} the annihilator of \(T\) meets the
725	  non-zero-divisors of \(P_d\); choose \(F\) in that intersection. In the domain \(P_d\) a
726	  non-zero-divisor is exactly a non-zero element, so \(F \ne 0\), and \(F \cdot T = 0\).
727	  Since \(F\) annihilates \(T\), the \(P_d\)-module structure descends along the quotient
728	  map \(P_d \twoheadrightarrow P_d/(F)\); the image of any finite \(P_d\)-generating set of
729	  \(T\) generates \(T\) over \(P_d/(F)\), so \(T\) is finite over \(P_d/(F)\).
730	\end{proof}

732	\subsubsection{Nagata change-of-variables machinery}
733	\label{sec:gf_nagata_machinery}
734	
735	The Nagata normalisation lemma \cref{lem:gf_nagata_monic_lastVar} is assembled from
736	technical helper lemmas implementing the triangular change of variables and the
737	radix-encoding degree bookkeeping, following the Nagata trick of
738	\cite{nitsure-hilbert-quot}~\S4 over a noetherian domain. Throughout, \(k\) is a domain,
739	\(n \ge 0\), and \(f \in k[X_0, \dots, X_n]\) is a fixed polynomial; we write
740	\(\mathrm{up} := 2 + \deg_{\mathrm{tot}}(f)\) and the radix weights \(r_i := \mathrm{up}^{\,i}\)
741	for \(i = 0, \dots, n\) (so every exponent occurring in \(f\) is a base-\(\mathrm{up}\) digit).
742	The distinguished variable is \(X_0\).
743	
744	\begin{definition}\leanok
745	[Nagata substitution \(T_1\)]
746	  \label{def:gf_nagata_T1}
747	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.T1}
748	  \textit{Source: Nitsure \S4 (Nagata change of variables).}
749	  For a scalar \(c \in k\), the substitution \(T_1(c)\) is the \(k\)-algebra endomorphism
750	  of \(k[X_0, \dots, X_n]\) determined on generators by
751	  \[
752	    X_0 \mapsto X_0, \qquad X_i \mapsto X_i + c\,X_0^{\,r_i} \quad (i \ne 0).
753	  \]
754	  It is triangular in the variables \(X_i\) (\(i \ne 0\)) over the distinguished variable
755	  \(X_0\); this is the device realising the support-dimension drop.
756	\end{definition}
757	
758	\begin{lemma}\leanok
759	[\(T_1\) is invertible by the opposite shift]
760	  \label{lem:gf_t1_comp_t1_neg}
761	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.t1_comp_t1_neg}
762	  \uses{def:gf_nagata_T1}
763	  For every \(c \in k\), \(T_1(c) \circ T_1(-c) = \mathrm{id}\); the Nagata substitution is
764	  invertible, with inverse the substitution of opposite coefficient.
765	\end{lemma}
766	\begin{proof}
767	  \uses{def:gf_nagata_T1}
768	  It suffices to check the two composites agree on each generator: \(X_0 \mapsto X_0\), and
769	  \(X_i \mapsto X_i + (-c)X_0^{r_i} + c\,X_0^{r_i} = X_i\) for \(i \ne 0\).
770	\end{proof}
771	
772	\begin{definition}\leanok
773	[Nagata automorphism \(T\)]
774	  \label{def:gf_nagata_T}
775	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.T}
776	  \uses{def:gf_nagata_T1, lem:gf_t1_comp_t1_neg}
777	  \textit{Source: Nitsure \S4 (Nagata change of variables).}
778	  The Nagata change of variables is the \(k\)-algebra automorphism \(T := T_1(1)\) of
779	  \(k[X_0, \dots, X_n]\) — i.e.\ \(X_i \mapsto X_i + X_0^{\,r_i}\) (\(i \ne 0\)) and
780	  \(X_0 \mapsto X_0\) — packaged as an algebra equivalence with inverse \(T_1(-1)\) via
781	  \cref{lem:gf_t1_comp_t1_neg}.
782	\end{definition}
783	
784	\begin{lemma}\leanok
785	[Exponent bound is preserved by listing]
786	  \label{lem:gf_lt_up}
787	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.lt_up}
788	  If every entry of an exponent vector \(v\) satisfies \(v_i < \mathrm{up}\), then
789	  every entry of the list of values of \(v\) is also \(< \mathrm{up}\).
790	\end{lemma}
791	\begin{proof}
792	  The list of values of \(v\) consists exactly of the entries \(v_i\); each satisfies
793	  \(v_i < \mathrm{up}\) by hypothesis.
794	\end{proof}
795	
796	\begin{lemma}\leanok
797	[Injectivity of the radix encoding]
798	  \label{lem:gf_sum_r_mul_ne}
799	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.sum_r_mul_ne}
800	  \uses{lem:gf_lt_up}
801	  Let \(v, w\) be exponent vectors with all entries \(< \mathrm{up}\) and \(v \ne w\). Then
802	  the weighted base-\(\mathrm{up}\) sums differ:
803	  \(\sum_i r_i v_i \ne \sum_i r_i w_i\), where \(r_i = \mathrm{up}^{\,i}\).
804	\end{lemma}
805	\begin{proof}
806	  \uses{lem:gf_lt_up}
807	  The map \(v \mapsto \sum_i \mathrm{up}^{\,i} v_i\) is the value of the base-\(\mathrm{up}\)
808	  digit string \(v\); since every digit is \(< \mathrm{up}\) (\cref{lem:gf_lt_up}), this
809	  encoding is injective by uniqueness of base-\(\mathrm{up}\) expansions, so distinct
810	  \(v \ne w\) give distinct sums.
811	\end{proof}
812	
813	\begin{lemma}\leanok
814	[\(X_0\)-degree of a transformed monomial]
815	  \label{lem:gf_degreeOf_zero_t}
816	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.degreeOf_zero_t}
817	  \uses{def:gf_nagata_T}
818	  For a non-zero scalar \(a \ne 0\) and exponent vector \(v\), the degree in \(X_0\) of the
819	  transformed monomial is the radix value
820	  \[
821	    \deg_{X_0}\!\big(T(a\,X^v)\big) = \sum_i r_i v_i, \qquad r_i = \mathrm{up}^{\,i}.
822	  \]
823	\end{lemma}
824	\begin{proof}
825	  \uses{def:gf_nagata_T}
826	  Under \(T\), \(a\,X^v \mapsto a\,X_0^{v_0}\prod_{i \ne 0}(X_i + X_0^{r_i})^{v_i}\). Read as
827	  a polynomial in \(X_0\) over \(k[X_1, \dots, X_n]\), each factor
828	  \((X_i + X_0^{r_i})^{v_i}\) has \(X_0\)-degree \(r_i v_i\) and leading coefficient \(1\);
829	  the degrees add to \(\sum_i r_i v_i\) (the hypothesis \(a \ne 0\) keeps the top term from
830	  vanishing over the domain \(k\)).
831	\end{proof}
832	
833	\begin{lemma}\leanok
834	[Distinct monomials acquire distinct \(X_0\)-degrees]
835	  \label{lem:gf_degreeOf_t_ne_of_ne}
836	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.degreeOf_t_ne_of_ne}
837	  \uses{lem:gf_degreeOf_zero_t, lem:gf_sum_r_mul_ne}
838	  For distinct support exponents \(v \ne w\) of \(f\), the transformed monomials
839	  \(T(c_v X^v)\) and \(T(c_w X^w)\) (where \(c_v, c_w\) are the corresponding coefficients
840	  of \(f\)) have distinct degrees in \(X_0\), making the top \(X_0\)-degree of \(T(f)\) unique.
841	\end{lemma}
842	\begin{proof}
843	  \uses{lem:gf_degreeOf_zero_t, lem:gf_sum_r_mul_ne}
844	  By \cref{lem:gf_degreeOf_zero_t} the two degrees are \(\sum_i r_i v_i\) and
845	  \(\sum_i r_i w_i\). Each support exponent is bounded by the total degree of \(f\), hence
846	  \(< \mathrm{up} = 2 + \deg_{\mathrm{tot}} f\); radix-injectivity
847	  \cref{lem:gf_sum_r_mul_ne} then yields \(\sum_i r_i v_i \ne \sum_i r_i w_i\).
848	\end{proof}
849	
850	\begin{lemma}\leanok
851	[Leading coefficient of a transformed monomial]
852	  \label{lem:gf_leadingCoeff_finSuccEquiv_t}
853	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.leadingCoeff_finSuccEquiv_t}
854	  \uses{def:gf_nagata_T, def:gf_nagata_T1}
855	  For any exponent \(v\) with coefficient \(c_v := [X^v]\,f\), viewing \(T(c_v X^v)\) as a
856	  univariate polynomial in \(X_0\) over \(k[X_1, \dots, X_n]\), its leading coefficient in
857	  \(X_0\) is the constant \(c_v \in k\).
858	\end{lemma}
859	\begin{proof}
860	  \uses{def:gf_nagata_T, def:gf_nagata_T1}
861	  Each factor \((X_i + X_0^{r_i})^{v_i}\) is monic in \(X_0\) with leading coefficient
862	  \(1\); a product of monics is monic, so the leading coefficient of \(T(c_v X^v)\) in
863	  \(X_0\) is exactly the scalar \(c_v\).
864	\end{proof}
865	
866	\begin{lemma}\leanok
867	[Leading coefficient of \(T(f)\) is a non-zero constant]
868	  \label{lem:gf_T_leadingcoeff_eq}
869	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.T_leadingcoeff_eq}
870	  \uses{lem:gf_degreeOf_t_ne_of_ne, lem:gf_leadingCoeff_finSuccEquiv_t}
871	  Suppose \(f \ne 0\). Then there is a support exponent \(v \in \mathrm{supp}\,f\) such that
872	  the leading coefficient of \(T(f)\) in \(X_0\) equals the constant \(c_v := [X^v]\,f \ne 0\).
873	  Thus \(T(f)\) is, up to the non-zero scalar \(c_v\), monic in \(X_0\).
874	\end{lemma}
875	\begin{proof}
876	  \uses{lem:gf_degreeOf_t_ne_of_ne, lem:gf_leadingCoeff_finSuccEquiv_t}
877	  Expand \(f = \sum_{w \in \mathrm{supp}\,f} c_w X^w\). By \cref{lem:gf_degreeOf_t_ne_of_ne}
878	  the transformed monomials \(T(c_w X^w)\) have pairwise distinct \(X_0\)-degrees, so one of
879	  them — say at \(v\) — strictly dominates the others; the leading term of
880	  \(T(f) = \sum_w T(c_w X^w)\) is then that of \(T(c_v X^v)\), whose leading coefficient is
881	  the constant \(c_v\) by \cref{lem:gf_leadingCoeff_finSuccEquiv_t}. Since
882	  \(v \in \mathrm{supp}\,f\), \(c_v \ne 0\).
883	\end{proof}

913	\begin{lemma}\leanok
914	[L5b.2 — Nagata change of variables: monic in the distinguished variable]
915	  \label{lem:gf_nagata_monic_lastVar}
916	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.gf_nagata_monic_lastVar}
917	  \uses{def:gf_nagata_T, lem:gf_T_leadingcoeff_eq, lem:gf_finSuccEquiv_map_comm}
918	  % NOTE (variable convention): the project's univariate elimination uses
919	  %   MvPolynomial.finSuccEquiv, which singles out variable X_0 as the polynomial
920	  %   variable over the coefficient ring MvPolynomial (Fin n) A. The choice of which of
921	  %   the d = n+1 variables is "distinguished/eliminated" is immaterial (a renaming);
922	  %   the informal prose elsewhere says "last variable X_d" but the Lean uses X_0.
923	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'', proof, inductive step
924	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1766-L1768);
925	  % the Nagata substitution is the standard device realising the support-dimension drop.
