928	  %  as a $K\otimes_{A_g}(B_g)$-module is strictly less than $n$. Hence by
929	  %  induction on $n$ (applied to the data $A_g$, $B_g$, $T$),"
930	  % LEAN SIGNATURE (the scaffold creates the decl pinned above with this signature).
931	  %   A_g := Localization.Away g; map := MvPolynomial.map (algebraMap A A_g).
932	  %     (A : Type*) [CommRing A] [IsDomain A] (n : ℕ)
933	  %       (F : MvPolynomial (Fin (n + 1)) A) (hF : F ≠ 0) :
934	  %       ∃ (g : A) (_ : g ≠ 0)
935	  %         (e : MvPolynomial (Fin (n + 1)) A ≃ₐ[A] MvPolynomial (Fin (n + 1)) A),
936	  %         IsUnit
937	  %           (MvPolynomial.finSuccEquiv (Localization.Away g) n
938	  %             (MvPolynomial.map (algebraMap A (Localization.Away g)) (e F))).leadingCoeff
939	  %   The automorphism e is the triangular Nagata substitution; g is the (non-zero)
940	  %   leading coefficient that becomes a unit after inversion.
941	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'' (support-dimension drop).}
942	  Let \(A\) be a domain, \(n \ge 0\), and \(0 \ne F \in A[X_0, \dots, X_n]\). Then there
943	  exist a triangular \(A\)-algebra automorphism \(e\) of \(A[X_0, \dots, X_n]\) and a
944	  non-zero \(g \in A\) such that, after inverting \(g\), the image of \(e(F)\) in
945	  \(A_g[X_0, \dots, X_n]\), viewed as a univariate polynomial in \(X_0\) over
946	  \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n]\), has a \emph{unit} leading coefficient. Equivalently, \(e(F)\)
947	  is monic in \(X_0\) up to the unit \(g\) over \(A_g\).
948	\end{lemma}
949	
950	\begin{proof}
951	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1766-L1768).
952	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (support-dimension drop, Nagata normalisation).}
953	  Apply the Nagata substitution \(X_i \mapsto X_i + X_0^{r^{\,i}}\) for \(i = 1, \dots, n\)
954	  and \(X_0 \mapsto X_0\), with \(r\) larger than every exponent occurring in \(F\): it is a
955	  triangular (hence invertible) \(A\)-algebra automorphism \(e\) of \(A[X_0, \dots, X_n]\).
956	  Under this substitution the distinct monomials of \(F\) acquire distinct degrees in
957	  \(X_0\), so \(e(F)\), expanded as a polynomial in \(X_0\), has a unique top-degree term
958	  whose coefficient is the coefficient \(c \in A\) of the top total-degree monomial of
959	  \(F\); since \(F \ne 0\) this \(c \ne 0\). Put \(g := c\). The degree of \(e(F)\) in
960	  \(X_0\) equals the total degree of \(F\), and its leading coefficient in \(X_0\) is the
961	  constant \(g\). Passing to \(A_g = A[g^{-1}]\), the image of \(g\) is a unit, so the
962	  image of \(e(F)\) in \(A_g[X_0, \dots, X_n]\) is monic in \(X_0\) up to the unit \(g\).
963	\end{proof}

1009	  %             MvPolynomial (Fin n) R →+* (MvPolynomial (Fin (n + 1)) R ⧸ Ideal.span {p})).Finite
1010	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'' (single-variable elimination).}
1011	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring, \(n \in \mathbb{N}\), and \(p \in R[X_0, \dots, X_n]\) a
1012	  polynomial whose image under the isomorphism
1013	  \(R[X_0, \dots, X_n] \cong (R[X_1, \dots, X_n])[X_0]\) has a \emph{unit} leading
1014	  coefficient — i.e.\ \(p\) is monic in \(X_0\) up to a unit. Then the
1015	  quotient \(R[X_0, \dots, X_n]/(p)\) is module-finite over the coefficient subring
1016	  \(R[X_1, \dots, X_n]\) (embedded by \(X_j \mapsto X_j\), \(j = 1, \dots, n\)).
1017	\end{lemma}
1018	
1019	\begin{proof}
1020	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1766-L1768).
1021	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (single-variable elimination).}
1022	  Write \(S := R[X_1, \dots, X_n]\) and let
1023	  \(\Phi : R[X_0, \dots, X_n] \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } S[X_0]\) be the standard \(R\)-algebra
1024	  isomorphism, which sends \(X_0\) to the univariate variable and embeds \(S\) as the constants. Put \(q := \Phi(p) \in S[X_0]\); by hypothesis its leading
1025	  coefficient \(u\) is a unit, so the associate \(\tilde q := u^{-1} \cdot q\) is monic and
1026	  \((\tilde q) = (q)\) in \(S[X_0]\). The isomorphism \(\Phi\) carries the ideal \((p)\) onto
1027	  \((q) = (\tilde q)\), hence descends to a ring isomorphism
1028	  \(R[X_0, \dots, X_n]/(p) \cong S[X_0]/(\tilde q)\) that is \(S\)-linear, because \(S\) sits
1029	  inside both sides as the constants (respectively as the coefficient subring
1030	  \(R[X_1, \dots, X_n]\)). By \cref{lem:polynomial_monic_quotient_finite} the quotient
1031	  \(S[X_0]/(\tilde q)\) is module-finite over \(S\); transporting finiteness along the
1032	  \(S\)-linear isomorphism shows \(R[X_0, \dots, X_n]/(p)\) is module-finite over
1033	  \(S = R[X_1, \dots, X_n]\).
1034	\end{proof}

1040	[L5b — torsion reindex onto fewer variables]
1041	  \label{lem:gf_torsion_reindex}

1043	  \uses{lem:gf_torsion_annihilator, lem:gf_nagata_monic_lastVar,
1044	        lem:gf_mvPolynomial_quotient_finite_monic, lem:gf_pullback_module_transport,
1045	        lem:gf_finite_of_quotient_ringequiv, lem:gf_islocalizedmodule_restrictscalars}
1046	  % NOTE (shared engine): the single-variable Nagata change-of-variables together with
1047	  % the denominator-clearing after inverting one element of A is the SAME engine the
1048	  % Noether-normalisation step L4 (lem:gf_noether_clear_denominators, via its sub-lemma
1049	  % lem:gf_clear_one_denominator) requires. It should be built once in Lean and reused by
1050	  % both: a general "make a nonzero F monic in the last variable and clear the leading
1051	  % coefficient into one Localization.Away g" helper discharges this lemma and feeds L4.
1052	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'', proof, inductive step
1053	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1766-L1768).
1054	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
1055	  % "Therefore, the dimension of the support of $K\otimes_{A_g}T$
1056	  %  as a $K\otimes_{A_g}(B_g)$-module is strictly less than $n$. Hence by
1057	  %  induction on $n$ (applied to the data $A_g$, $B_g$, $T$),"
1058	  % LEAN SIGNATURE (the scaffold creates the decl pinned above with this signature).
1059	  %   Write A_g := Localization.Away g and T_g := LocalizedModule (Submonoid.powers g) T.
1060	  %     (A : Type*) [CommRing A] [IsDomain A] [IsNoetherianRing A]
1061	  %       (d : ℕ) (hd : 0 < d) (T : Type*) [AddCommGroup T]
1062	  %       [Module (MvPolynomial (Fin d) A) T]
1063	  %       [Module.Finite (MvPolynomial (Fin d) A) T]
1064	  %       [Module A T] [IsScalarTower A (MvPolynomial (Fin d) A) T]
1065	  %       (htors : Module.IsTorsion (MvPolynomial (Fin d) A) T) :
1066	  %       ∃ (g : A) (_ : g ≠ 0) (m' : ℕ) (_ : m' < d)
1067	  %         (_ : Module (MvPolynomial (Fin m') (Localization.Away g))
1068	  %                 (LocalizedModule (Submonoid.powers g) T))
1069	  %         -- NOTE: the redundant canonical `Module A_g T_g` existential was
1070	  %         -- dropped from the landed decl (it is always `inferInstance`; bundling it created an
1071	  %         -- opaque fvar breaking OreLocalization instance matching at the L5 use-site).
1072	  %         (_ : IsScalarTower (Localization.Away g)
1073	  %                 (MvPolynomial (Fin m') (Localization.Away g))
1074	  %                 (LocalizedModule (Submonoid.powers g) T)),
1075	  %         Module.Finite (MvPolynomial (Fin m') (Localization.Away g))
1076	  %           (LocalizedModule (Submonoid.powers g) T)
1077	  %   one may always take m' = d - 1 (single-variable elimination); the strong induction
1078	  %   accepts any m' < d.
1079	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'' (support-dimension drop).}
1080	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain, \(d \ge 1\), and \(T\) a finite \emph{torsion} module
1081	  over \(P_d := A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\) (with its induced \(A\)-module structure). Then there
1082	  exist a non-zero \(g \in A\) and an integer \(m' < d\) such that, after inverting \(g\),
1083	  the localised module \(T_g := T[g^{-1}]\) is module-finite over the polynomial ring
1084	  \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_{m'}]\) in \(m'\) variables over the base ring \(A_g = A[g^{-1}]\);
1085	  that is, \(T_g\) carries compatible \(A_g\)- and \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_{m'}]\)-module
1086	  structures (via the scalar tower \(A_g \to A_g[X_1, \dots, X_{m'}]\)) and is finite over
1087	  \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_{m'}]\). One may always take \(m' = d - 1\).
1088	\end{lemma}
1089	
1090	\begin{proof}
1091	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1766-L1768).
1092	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (support-dimension drop).}
1093	  \uses{lem:gf_torsion_annihilator, lem:gf_nagata_monic_lastVar,
1094	        lem:gf_mvPolynomial_quotient_finite_monic, lem:gf_pullback_module_transport,
1095	        lem:gf_finite_of_quotient_ringequiv, lem:gf_islocalizedmodule_restrictscalars}
1096	  Write \(d = (d-1) + 1\) and \(P_d = A[X_0, \dots, X_{d-1}]\) (the distinguished variable
1097	  \(X_0\) is the one eliminated in the single-variable step; cf.\ \cref{lem:gf_nagata_monic_lastVar}).
1098	
1099	  \emph{Annihilator.} By \cref{lem:gf_torsion_annihilator} there is a non-zero
1100	  \(F \in \operatorname{Ann}_{P_d}(T)\), and \(T\) is a finite module over \(P_d/(F)\).
1101	
1102	  \emph{Nagata normalisation.} Applying \cref{lem:gf_nagata_monic_lastVar} to \(F \ne 0\)
1103	  yields a triangular \(A\)-algebra automorphism \(e\) of \(P_d\) and a non-zero
1104	  \(g \in A\) such that the image of \(e(F)\) in \(A_g[X_0, \dots, X_{d-1}]\) has a unit
1105	  leading coefficient in \(X_0\). The automorphism \(e\) induces a ring isomorphism
1106	  \(P_d/(F) \cong P_d/(e\,F)\), so \(T\) is also finite over \(P_d/(e\,F)\). Localising at
1107	  the powers of \(g\) (localisation commutes with the quotient), \(T_g\) is finite over
1108	  \(P_d[g^{-1}]/(e\,F) = A_g[X_0, \dots, X_{d-1}]/(e\,F)\).
1109	
1110	  \emph{Elimination.} Apply the shared engine
1111	  \cref{lem:gf_mvPolynomial_quotient_finite_monic} with \(R = A_g\) and \(p\) the image of
1112	  \(e(F)\) (whose leading coefficient in \(X_0\) is a unit): the quotient
1113	  \(A_g[X_0, \dots, X_{d-1}]/(e\,F)\) is module-finite over the coefficient subring
1114	  \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_{d-1}]\), a polynomial ring in \(m' := d - 1\) variables over
1115	  \(A_g\).
1116	
1117	  \emph{Localisation transport.} The elimination step records module-finiteness of the
1118	  \emph{quotient ring} over its coefficient subring, but the conclusion of the lemma is
1119	  about a localisation of the torsion module \(T\). Making the two agree is the
1120	  mathematical content of this step, which proceeds as follows.
1121	
1122	  Two distinct localisations of \(T\) are in play and must be identified. The natural
1123	  setting for the elimination is the localisation \(T'_g := T[\,\mathcal{C}^{-1}]\) of \(T\)
1124	  at the multiplicative set \(\mathcal{C} = \{\,c \cdot g^{\,k} : k \ge 0\,\}\) of constant
1125	  polynomials \(g^k\) inside \(P_d\); this localisation carries, for free, the full
1126	  module structure over the localised polynomial ring
1127	  \(P_d[g^{-1}] = A_g[X_0, \dots, X_{d-1}]\), its finiteness, and the quotient structure by
1128	  \((e\,F)\). The \emph{goal}, however, is phrased with \(T_g := T[g^{-1}]\), the
1129	  localisation of \(T\) at the powers of the single element \(g \in A\). One first proves
1130	  \(T'_g\) module-finite over \(R := A_g[X_1, \dots, X_{m'}]\) and then transports that
1131	  finiteness to \(T_g\).
1132	
1133	  The change of variables \(e\) is transported to the localised polynomial ring as a
1134	  \emph{ring isomorphism} \(\bar e : P_d[g^{-1}] \xrightarrow{\sim} P_d[g^{-1}]\), obtained
1135	  by localising \(e\) at \(\mathcal{C}\) (which \(e\) preserves, since \(e\) fixes the
1136	  constants \(c\,X^0\)); it carries the localised annihilator \(F_g\) to the
1137	  Nagata-normalised generator \(G\) and fixes the constants coming from the base. Passing
1138	  to quotients, \(\bar e\) descends to a ring isomorphism
1139	  \(\psi : P_d[g^{-1}]/(F_g) \xrightarrow{\sim} P_d[g^{-1}]/(G)\) of the two quotient rings
1140	  (using that the image of the ideal \((F_g)\) under \(\bar e\) is \((G)\)). Both quotient
1141	  rings act on \(T'_g\), and module-finiteness over \(R\) of the elimination-side quotient
1142	  \(P_d[g^{-1}]/(G)\) is transported across \(\psi\) — a ring isomorphism of the
1143	  \emph{acting} ring compatible with the fixed \(R\)-algebra structure — by
1144	  \cref{lem:gf_finite_of_quotient_ringequiv}, yielding \(T'_g\) module-finite over \(R\).
1145	
1146	  Finally the two localisations are identified. Restricting scalars of the canonical
1147	  \(P_d\)-localisation map of \(T\) along \(A \to P_d\) exhibits \(T'_g\) as a localisation
1148	  of \(T\) \emph{at the powers of \(g\) over \(A\)} — this descent of the
1149	  localised-module property along the scalar tower is
1150	  \cref{lem:gf_islocalizedmodule_restrictscalars} — so the universal property produces a
1151	  linear isomorphism \(T_g \cong T'_g\) identifying the two.
1152	
1153	  \emph{Action-diamond subtlety.} The conclusion of the lemma requires an
1154	  \(A_g\)-module structure on \(T_g\) sitting in a scalar tower
1155	  \(A_g \to A_g[X_1, \dots, X_{m'}] \to T_g\). One cannot simply install an anonymous
1156	  \(A_g\)-action by fiat: \(T_g = T[g^{-1}]\) already carries its \emph{canonical}
1157	  localisation action of \(A_g\), and this canonical action is the one the scalar-tower
1158	  requirement sees. Hence the identification \(T_g \cong T'_g\) must be promoted to an
1159	  \(A_g\)-linear isomorphism — which it is, because the transported action agrees with the
1160	  canonical localisation action on the \(A_g\)-constants: the constant
1161	  \(\mathcal{C}\)-action of \(A_g\) on \(T'_g\) factors through the original \(A\)-action on
1162	  \(T\) exactly as the canonical \(g\)-localisation action does. With the isomorphism made
1163	  \(A_g\)-linear, the \(R\)-module structure and finiteness pull back from \(T'_g\) to
1164	  \(T_g\) by \cref{lem:gf_pullback_module_transport}, and the scalar tower
1165	  \(A_g \to R \to T_g\) follows.
1166	
1167	  \emph{Transitivity.} Composing the two module-finite extensions, \(T_g\) is finite over
1168	  \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_{m'}]\). Since \(m' = d - 1 < d\) (using \(d \ge 1\)), this is the
1169	  required reindexing; no Krull-dimension theory is needed, the number of variables
1170	  dropping by exactly one through the single-variable elimination.
1171	\end{proof}

1173	\subsubsection{Module-structure transport helpers (reindex)}
1174	\label{sec:gf_reindex_transport_helpers}
1175	
1176	The localisation-transport step of \cref{lem:gf_torsion_reindex} moves a module structure
1177	and its finiteness across (i) an additive equivalence of the underlying groups, (ii) a ring
1178	isomorphism of the acting ring, and (iii) a change of localising monoid along a scalar
1179	tower. The following helper lemmas package those three transport principles. They are general
1180	module-theory facts; no external source is cited.
1181	
1182	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1183	[Module transport along an additive equivalence]
1184	  \label{lem:gf_pullback_module_transport}
1185	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.pullbackModuleAddEquiv}
1186	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.finite_of_pullbackModuleAddEquiv}
1187	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.pullback_isScalarTower}
1188	  Let \(e : M \xrightarrow{\sim} N\) be an additive equivalence and let \(M\) carry an
1189	  \(R\)-module structure. Pulling the action back along \(e\) by
1190	  \(r \cdot y := e\big(r \cdot e^{-1}(y)\big)\) makes \(N\) an \(R\)-module for which \(e\)
1191	  is \(R\)-linear. Under this transported structure: (a) if \(M\) is module-finite over
1192	  \(R\) then so is \(N\); and (b) if \(M\) additionally carries a compatible action of a
1193	  base ring \(A_g\) with scalar tower \(A_g \to R \to M\), then the pulled-back actions on
1194	  \(N\) again form a scalar tower \(A_g \to R \to N\).
1195	\end{lemma}
1196	\begin{proof}
1197	  The module axioms for \(y \mapsto e(r \cdot e^{-1}(y))\) follow termwise from those on
1198	  \(M\) together with the fact that \(e\) and \(e^{-1}\) are additive and mutually inverse;
1199	  by construction \(e\) is then an \(R\)-linear isomorphism. Statement (a) follows by
1200	  transporting module-finiteness across the \(R\)-linear isomorphism \(e\).
1201	  Statement (b) checks the scalar-tower identity
1202	  \((a\,r)\cdot y = a\cdot(r\cdot y)\) on \(N\) by transporting it across \(e\) and applying
1203	  associativity of the scalar action on \(M\).
1204	\end{proof}
1205	
1206	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1207	[Finite transport along a ring isomorphism of the acting ring]
1208	  \label{lem:gf_finite_of_quotient_ringequiv}
1209	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.finite_of_quotientRingEquiv}
1210	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring and let \(\psi : B_1 \xrightarrow{\sim} B_2\) be a ring
1211	  isomorphism between two \(R\)-algebras that is compatible with the structure maps, i.e.\
1212	  \(\psi \circ (\text{algebra map } R \to B_1) = (\text{algebra map } R \to B_2)\). If
1213	  \(B_2\) is module-finite over \(R\) and \(M\) is module-finite over \(B_1\) (with the
1214	  scalar tower \(R \to B_1 \to M\)), then \(M\) is module-finite over \(R\).
1215	\end{lemma}
1216	\begin{proof}
1217	  Compatibility of \(\psi^{-1}\) with the structure maps follows from that of \(\psi\), so
1218	  \(\psi^{-1}\) is an \(R\)-algebra isomorphism; transporting \(R\)-finiteness of \(B_2\)
1219	  across it makes \(B_1\) module-finite over \(R\). Transitivity of module-finiteness through
1220	  the scalar tower \(R \to B_1 \to M\) then gives \(M\) module-finite over \(R\).
1221	\end{proof}
1222	
1223	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1224	[Descent of a localised-module structure along a scalar tower]
1225	  \label{lem:gf_islocalizedmodule_restrictscalars}
1226	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.isLocalizedModule_restrictScalars}
1227	  Let \(A_g \to R\) be a ring map with compatible \(A_g\)- and \(R\)-module structures on
1228	  \(M\) and \(M'\) (scalar towers \(A_g \to R \to M\) and \(A_g \to R \to M'\)), and let
1229	  \(S \subseteq A_g\) be a multiplicative subset. If an \(R\)-linear map
1230	  \(f : M \to M'\) realises \(M'\) as the localisation of \(M\) at the image submonoid
1231	  \(S \cdot 1 \subseteq R\) over \(R\), then its restriction of scalars to \(A_g\) realises
1232	  \(M'\) as the localisation of \(M\) at \(S\) over \(A_g\).
1233	\end{lemma}
1234	\begin{proof}
1235	  One verifies the universal property of a localised module for the restricted map. Each
1236	  \(s \in S\) acts invertibly on \(M'\): its action coincides, via the scalar tower, with
1237	  that of its image in \(R\), which is a unit because \(f\) is a localisation at the image
1238	  submonoid. Surjectivity onto \(M'\) by fractions \(x/s\) with \(s \in S\) and the
1239	  equality criterion likewise descend from the corresponding image-submonoid statements,
1240	  rewriting each \(R\)-scalar by an \(A_g\)-scalar through the scalar tower. Assembling
1241	  these conditions gives the claim.
1242	\end{proof}

1244	\begin{lemma}[Localisation identification across an associated denominator]
1245	  \label{lem:isLocalization_away_mul_of_associated}
1246	  \lean{IsLocalization.Away.mul_of_associated}
1247	  \mathlibok
1248	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
1249	  Let \(R \to S \to T\) be a tower of commutative rings with compatible structure maps, let
1250	  \(x, z \in R\) and \(y \in S\), and suppose \(S\) is the localisation of \(R\) away from
1251	  \(x\) and \(T\) is the localisation of \(S\) away from \(y\). If the image of \(z\) in \(S\)
1252	  is associated to \(y\) (the two differ by a unit), then \(T\) is the localisation of \(R\)
1253	  away from the single product \(x z\); that is, \(T \cong R[(x z)^{-1}]\).
1254	\end{lemma}

1256	\begin{lemma}[Freeness transports across a semilinear ring isomorphism]
1257	  \label{lem:module_free_of_ringEquiv}
1258	  \lean{Module.Free.of_ringEquiv}
1259	  \mathlibok
1260	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}

1262	  \(e : M \xrightarrow{\sim} M'\) be an additive isomorphism that is semilinear over a ring

1267	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1268	[L5 step-4 helper — descent of freeness across a tower of \(\mathrm{Away}\) localisations]
1269	  \label{lem:gf_away_tower_descent}
1270	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.free_localizationAway_of_away_tower}
1271	  \uses{lem:isLocalization_away_mul_of_associated, lem:module_free_of_ringEquiv}
1272	  % LEAN DEPS: the localisation-transport primitives the Lean proof consumes are
1273	  %   IsLocalization.surj                          (clear the denominator of h over A_g)
1274	  %   IsLocalization.map_units                     (the cleared denominator s ∈ powers g is a unit)
1275	  %   IsLocalization.Away.mul_of_associated        (key ring identification: Away(g·a) on A_h)
1276	  %   IsLocalization.algEquiv                      (ring iso Away(g·a) ≅ A_h)
1277	  %   Module.Free.of_ringEquiv                     (transport freeness across that ring iso)
1278	  %   IsLocalizedModule.linearEquiv                (the σ-semilinear module equivalence)
1279	  %   LinearEquiv.extendScalarsOfIsLocalization    (upgrade the R-linear equiv to Away(g·a)-linear)
1280	  % The one primitive NOT packaged by Mathlib — that the composite localisation map
1281	  %   ψ := mkLinearMap(powers h) ∘ mkLinearMap(powers g) : T → (T_g)_h
1282	  % satisfies IsLocalizedModule (powers (g·a)) ψ — is proved directly from the three
1283	  % IsLocalizedModule axioms (units act invertibly, surjectivity up to denominators, the
1284	  % equality criterion); there is no packaged localisation-of-localisation lemma to invoke.
1285	  % NOTE: the closed Lean proof DID find a packaged route — the composite-localisation
1286	  % `IsLocalizedModule (powers (g·a)) ψ` step goes through `IsBaseChange.comp` via
1287	  % `isLocalizedModule_iff_isBaseChange`, so the "no packaged lemma" remark above is superseded.
1288	  % WITNESS: the conclusion holds at the SINGLE product f := g·a (not at a square f²).
1289	  Let \(A\) be a domain and \(T\) an \(A\)-module. Let \(g \in A\), \(g \ne 0\), and let
1290	  \(h \in A_g := \mathrm{Away}(g)\) be non-zero. Write
1291	  \((T_g)_h\) for the iterated localisation of \(T\) — first away from \(g\), then away from
1292	  \(h\) — and \((A_g)_h := \mathrm{Away}(h)\) for the corresponding iterated localisation of the
1293	  base ring. If \((T_g)_h\) is free over \((A_g)_h\), then there exists \(f \in A\),
1294	  \(f \ne 0\), such that the single localisation \(T_f\) is free over \(A_f := \mathrm{Away}(f)\).
1295	  Concretely the witness is the single product \(f := g\,a\) for an element \(a \in A\) whose
1296	  image in \(A_g\) is associated to \(h\).
1297	\end{lemma}
1298	
1299	\begin{proof}
1300	  \uses{lem:isLocalization_away_mul_of_associated, lem:module_free_of_ringEquiv}
1301	  \emph{Clear the denominator of \(h\).} As \(h\) lies in \(A_g = \mathrm{Away}(g)\),
1302	  surjectivity of the localisation map \(A \to A_g\) onto fractions yields \(a \in A\) and a
1303	  power \(s\) of \(g\) with \(h \cdot \bar s = \bar a\) in \(A_g\), where \(\bar{\,\cdot\,}\)
1304	  denotes the image in \(A_g\). The image \(\bar s\) is a unit (a power of \(g\) is invertible
1305	  in \(A_g\)), so \(\bar a = h\,\bar s\) is associated to \(h\); in particular \(\bar a \ne 0\)
1306	  (because \(h \ne 0\)), hence \(a \ne 0\). Since \(A\) is a domain and \(g, a \ne 0\), the
1307	  product \(f := g\,a\) is non-zero.
1308	
1309	  \emph{Identify the base rings.} The associatedness of \(\bar a\) and \(h\), together with the
1310	  tower \(A \to A_g \to A_h\), identifies the iterated localisation
1311	  \((A_g)_h = \mathrm{Away}(h)\) with the single localisation of \(A\) away from the product
1312	  \(g\,a\): by \cref{lem:isLocalization_away_mul_of_associated} (with \(x = g\), \(z = a\),
1313	  \(y = h\)) the ring \(A_h\) is a localisation of \(A\) away from \(g\,a\), and uniqueness of
1314	  localisation furnishes a ring isomorphism \(\mathrm{Away}(g\,a) \xrightarrow{\sim} A_h\).
1315	
1316	  \emph{Identify the modules.} The composite localisation map
1317	  \[
1318	    \psi : T \longrightarrow (T_g)_h, \qquad
1319	    \psi := \mathrm{mk}_{\langle h\rangle} \circ \mathrm{mk}_{\langle g\rangle},
1320	  \]
1321	  first into \(T_g\) and then into \((T_g)_h\), realises \((T_g)_h\) as the localisation of
1322	  \(T\) at the powers of \(g\,a\): every \(g^n a^m\) acts invertibly on \((T_g)_h\), \(\psi\) is
1323	  surjective up to such denominators, and its kernel is the expected one. This
1324	  localisation-of-localisation identity is verified directly from the defining properties of a
1325	  localised module; no packaged lemma supplies it.
1326	
1327	  \emph{Transport freeness.} By hypothesis \((T_g)_h\) is free over \(A_h\). The ring
1328	  isomorphism \(\mathrm{Away}(g\,a) \cong A_h\) and the induced semilinear module equivalence
1329	  \(T_{g\,a} \simeq (T_g)_h\) — assembled from the localised-module identification of the
1330	  previous step and promoted to an \(\mathrm{Away}(g\,a)\)-linear equivalence — carry this
1331	  freeness back by \cref{lem:module_free_of_ringEquiv}: \(T_{g\,a}\) is free over
1332	  \(\mathrm{Away}(g\,a)\). Taking \(f := g\,a\) gives \(T_f\) free over \(A_f\), as required.
1333	\end{proof}

1335	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1336	[L5 — polynomial-ring core of generic freeness]
1337	  \label{lem:gf_polynomial_core}
1338	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.exists_free_localizationAway_polynomial}
1339	  % NOTE (signature): the Lean decl uses a SHARED universe
1340	  %   `(A N : Type u)` (not `(A : Type*) (N : Type*)`). This is load-bearing, not a
1341	  %   convenience: the base-domain-generalizing strong induction applies its IH at
1342	  %   `A_g = Localization.Away g`, and `LocalizedModule S M : Type (max u_R u_M)`, so the
1343	  %   reindexed module escapes `N`'s universe unless `A` and `N` share one. The geometric
1344	  %   target lives entirely in `Type u` (`Scheme.{u}`), so this is the natural setting.
1345	  \uses{lem:gf_finite_module, lem:gf_splice_shortExact, lem:gf_torsion_base,
1346	        lem:gf_splice_shortExact_free_transport, lem:gf_generic_rank_ses,
1347	        lem:gf_torsion_reindex, lem:gf_away_tower_descent}
1348	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'', proof, inductive step
1349	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1760-L1772).
1350	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
1351	  % "Let $m$ be the generic rank of the finite module $B_g$ over the domain
1352	  %  $A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$. Then we have a short exact sequence of
1353	  %  $A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$-modules of the form
1354	  %  $0\to A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]^{\oplus m} \to B_g \to T \to 0$ where $T$ is a finite
1355	  %  torsion module over $A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$. Therefore, the dimension of the
1356	  %  support of $K\otimes_{A_g}T$ as a $K\otimes_{A_g}(B_g)$-module is strictly less
1357	  %  than $n$. Hence by induction on $n$ (applied to the data $A_g$, $B_g$, $T$),
1358	  %  there exists some $h\ne 0$ in $A$ with $T_h$ free over $A_{gh}$. Taking $f=gh$,
1359	  %  the lemma follows from the above short exact sequence."
1360	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'' (polynomial-ring
1361	  core of the induction).}
1362	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain and \(d \ge 0\). Let \(N\) be a finite module over
1363	  the polynomial ring \(A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\), regarded as an \(A\)-module. Then there
1364	  exists \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\), such that \(N_f\) is free over \(A_f\).
1365	
1366	\end{lemma}
1367	
1368	\begin{proof}
1369	  \uses{lem:gf_finite_module, lem:gf_splice_shortExact, lem:gf_torsion_base,
1370	        lem:gf_splice_shortExact_free_transport, lem:gf_generic_rank_ses,
1371	        lem:gf_torsion_reindex, lem:gf_away_tower_descent}
1372	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1760-L1772).
1373	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (polynomial-ring core).}
1374	  % LEAN PROOF STRUCTURE: the induction must generalise the base domain A
1375	  % TOGETHER WITH N. Formally, prove
1376	  %   ∀ d, ∀ (A) [CommRing A] [IsDomain A] [IsNoetherianRing A] (N) [..module data..],
1377	  %        ∃ f : A, f ≠ 0 ∧ Module.Free (Localization.Away f)
1378	  %                              (LocalizedModule (Submonoid.powers f) N)
1379	  % by Nat.strong_induction_on d, with A (and its CommRing/IsDomain/IsNoetherianRing
1380	  % instances) AND all of N's d-dependent module instances REVERTED INTO THE MOTIVE.
1381	  % This is load-bearing, and is the real cause of the prior stall: the reindex
1382	  % lem:gf_torsion_reindex changes the base ring from A to A_g = Localization.Away g, so
1383	  % the inductive hypothesis must be applied at base A_g, NOT A. An IH that fixes A would
1384	  % not typecheck on the reindexed module T_g (which is finite over
1385	  % MvPolynomial (Fin m') A_g). A_g remains a noetherian domain (a localisation of a
1386	  % noetherian domain), so the motive's hypotheses hold at A_g. Because `A` is already an
1387	  % explicit argument of the pinned signature, this is a reversion of A into the motive,
1388	  % NOT a change to the public signature. See analogies/gf-generic-rank-ses.md.
1389	  We argue by strong induction on \(d\), \emph{generalising the base domain \(A\)} (along
1390	  with the module \(N\)): the inductive hypothesis is available for every \(d' < d\),
1391	  every noetherian domain in place of \(A\), and every finite module over the
1392	  corresponding polynomial ring.
1393	
1394	  \emph{Base case \(d = 0\).} The ring \(P_0 = A[X_1, \dots, X_0]\) is \(A\) itself, so
1395	  \(N\) is a finite \(A\)-module and the claim is exactly the finite-module leaf
1396	  \cref{lem:gf_finite_module}.
1397	
1398	  \emph{Inductive step \(d \ge 1\).} If \(N\) is already a torsion \(P_d\)-module over
1399	  \(A\) — equivalently \(N_K = 0\) for \(K = \mathrm{Frac}(A)\) — then the torsion base
1400	  case \cref{lem:gf_torsion_base} produces \(f \ne 0\) with \(N_f = 0\), which is free
1401	  over \(A_f\). Otherwise we proceed in five steps.
1402	  \begin{enumerate}
1403	    \item \textbf{Generic-rank SES.} Apply \cref{lem:gf_generic_rank_ses} to \(N\) over
1404	      \(P_d = A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\): there are \(m \in \mathbb{N}\) and an injective
1405	      \(P_d\)-linear map \(\varphi : P_d^{\oplus m} \to N\) with torsion cokernel
1406	      \(T := N / \operatorname{im}\varphi\), i.e.\ a short exact sequence of
1407	      \(P_d\)-modules
1408	      \[
1409	        0 \to P_d^{\oplus m} \xrightarrow{\ \varphi\ } N \to T \to 0,
1410	        \qquad T \text{ torsion}.
1411	      \]
1412	      No element of \(A\) is inverted at this step.
1413	    \item \textbf{Reindex the torsion cokernel.} Apply \cref{lem:gf_torsion_reindex} to the
1414	      finite torsion module \(T\): there are \(g \ne 0\) in \(A\) and \(m' < d\) such that
1415	      the localisation \(T_g\) is module-finite over the polynomial ring
1416	      \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_{m'}]\) in fewer variables over the base ring \(A_g = A[g^{-1}]\).
1417	    \item \textbf{Inductive hypothesis at the new base \(A_g\).} Since \(A_g\) is again a
1418	      noetherian domain and \(m' < d\), the inductive hypothesis applies \emph{at base
1419	      \(A_g\)} to the finite \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_{m'}]\)-module \(T_g\): there is
1420	      \(h \ne 0\) in \(A_g\) with \((T_g)_h\) free over \((A_g)_h\). This is exactly the
1421	      point at which the base ring of the induction must be allowed to change from \(A\)
1422	      to \(A_g\).
1423	    \item \textbf{Descend the witness from \(A_g\) to \(A\).} Clearing the denominator of
1424	      \(h\) over \(A_g\) produces a numerator \(a \in A\) whose image in \(A_g\) is associated
1425	      to \(h\); then the iterated localisation \((A_g)_h\) is the single localisation
1426	      \(A_{g a}\) and \((T_g)_h\) is \(T_{g a}\). Thus freeness of \((T_g)_h\) over
1427	      \((A_g)_h\) transports to freeness of \(T_{g a}\) over \(A_{g a}\) by the
1428	      iterated-\(\mathrm{Away}\)-tower descent \cref{lem:gf_away_tower_descent}. Set
1429	      \(f := g a \ne 0\) — the single product, \emph{not} a square — so that \(T_f\) is free
1430	      over \(A_f\).
1431	      % NOTE: the tower-descent helper lem:gf_away_tower_descent
1432	      % (free_localizationAway_of_away_tower) is now CLOSED and axiom-clean. The L5 sorry that
1433	      % remains is NOT a missing math step — it is a Lean elaboration blocker: the
1434	      % `OreLocalization` instance *presentations* on the localised quotient `(N ⧸ range φ)_g`
1435	      % (CommSemiring / AddCommMonoid / Module-SMul) emitted by `gf_torsion_reindex` are defeq
1436	      % but not instance-transparent-equal to the presentations the helper input expects. The
1437	      % clean fix is non-local: make `gf_torsion_reindex` emit its conclusion over the canonical
1438	      % `OreLocalization.*` instances (its final `exact` / existential binders), after which the
1439	      % recorded five-line assembly closes verbatim. Do NOT re-dispatch a raw L5 prover round
1440	      % without that instance-alignment refactor first.
1441	    \item \textbf{Splice.} The left end \(P_d^{\oplus m}\) of the sequence is a free
1442	      \(A\)-module (the polynomial ring \(P_d = A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\) is \(A\)-free, hence
1443	      so is any finite direct power; note \(P_d^{\oplus m}\) is \emph{not} module-finite
1444	      over \(A\), but freeness is all the splice requires), and a free module stays free
1445	      under the localisation \(A \to A_f\); so \(P_d^{\oplus m}\) localises to a free
1446	      \(A_f\)-module. With both ends free over \(A_f\) after inverting \(f = g a\),
1447	      \cref{lem:gf_splice_shortExact} splices the localised short exact sequence to
1448	      conclude that the middle term \(N_f\) is free over \(A_f\). Taking \(f = g a\)
1449	      completes the induction.
1450	  \end{enumerate}
1451	\end{proof}

1497	\subsection{Geometric bridges for the affine assembly}
1498	\label{sec:gf_geometric_bridges}
1499	
1500	The geometric form of generic flatness is assembled from the algebraic core by
1501	restricting to a non-empty affine open of \(S\) and working patchwise over a finite
1502	affine cover of the preimage. Two genuinely geometric steps are isolated here as
1503	named bridges: the affine-local finiteness of the section module of a finite-type
1504	quasi-coherent sheaf (G1), feeding the algebraic core its finite-module input; and
1505	the flat-locality assembly (G3), promoting per-patch freeness to flatness of the
1506	restricted sheaf over the base open. Neither is supplied directly by Mathlib: Mathlib
1507	does not provide the affine-local identification of \(\F|_W\) with the associated sheaf
1508	of \(\Gamma(\F, W)\) together with the finiteness this transports.
1509	
1510	% ============================================================================
1511	% G1 decomposition (Stacks 01PB). The affine-local finiteness of the section
1512	% module splits into TWO halves:
1513	%   Half 1 — LOCALITY reduction (already proved in Lean): if the section module
1514	%     is finite on each member of a finite basic-open cover, it is finite on the
1515	%     affine. Blocks: lem:finite_localizedModule_transfer,
1516	%     lem:gf_finite_sections_of_basicOpen_finite_cover.
1517	%   Half 2 — finite-type BASE CASE: on a globally-generated affine the section
1518	%     module is finite, via Gamma-exactness on affine quasi-coherent modules.
1519	%     Blocks (seams): lem:gf_finiteType_affine_finite_cover_generated (seam 1),
1520	%     lem:gf_affine_qcoh_Gamma_epi (seam 2),
1521	%     lem:gf_qcoh_finite_sections_globally_generated (base case = seam 2 + 3).
1522	% G1's proof then chains: cover-extraction (seam 1) + base case (Half 2) on each
1523	% member + locality glue (Half 1).
1524	% ============================================================================
1525	
1526	\begin{lemma}[Finiteness is local on a finite spanning family]
1527	  \label{lem:module_finite_of_localizationSpan}
1528	  \lean{Module.Finite.of_localizationSpan_finite}
1529	  \mathlibok
1530	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.LocalProperties.Submodule}).}
1531	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring, \(N\) an \(R\)-module, and \(t \subseteq R\) a
1532	  finite family with \(\mathrm{span}\,t = \top\) (the \(t\) generate the unit
1533	  ideal). Suppose that for each \(g \in t\) the localization \(N_g\) is a finite
1534	  module over the localization \(R_g\). Then \(N\) is a finite \(R\)-module.
1535	\end{lemma}
1536	
1537	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1538	[Half 1 helper — transfer of localized-module finiteness across models]
1539	  \label{lem:finite_localizedModule_transfer}
1540	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.finite_localizedModule_of_isLocalizedModule}
1541	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring, \(S \subseteq R\) a multiplicative submonoid,
1542	  and \(M\) an \(R\)-module. Let \(R_S\) be an \(R\)-algebra that localizes \(R\)
1543	  at \(S\), and let \(N\) be an \(R\)-module carrying a compatible \(R_S\)-module
1544	  structure, together with an \(R\)-linear map \(\varphi : M \to N\) exhibiting
1545	  \(N\) as the localization of \(M\) at \(S\) (\(\mathrm{IsLocalizedModule}\,S\,
1546	  \varphi\)). If \(N\) is a finite \(R_S\)-module, then the canonical localized
1547	  module \(\mathrm{LocalizedModule}\,S\,M\) is a finite module over the canonical
1548	  localization \(\mathrm{Localization}\,S\).
1549	\end{lemma}
1550	
1551	\begin{proof}
1552	  The localization map \(\varphi\) provides an \(R\)-linear isomorphism
1553	  \(e : N \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathrm{LocalizedModule}\,S\,M\) (universal property
1554	  of localized modules), and the algebra localization provides an \(R\)-algebra
1555	  isomorphism \(\psi : R_S \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathrm{Localization}\,S\). These
1556	  are compatible: \(e\) is semilinear over \(\psi\), i.e.\ \(e(a \cdot x) =
1557	  \psi(a) \cdot e(x)\) for \(a \in R_S\), \(x \in N\) (both sides are pinned by
1558	  clearing a common denominator \(s \in S\), where the \(R\)-actions agree). A
1559	  finite \(R_S\)-spanning set \(T\) of \(N\) therefore maps under \(e\) to a
1560	  finite set whose \(\mathrm{Localization}\,S\)-span is all of
1561	  \(\mathrm{LocalizedModule}\,S\,M\); hence the canonical model is finite over
1562	  \(\mathrm{Localization}\,S\).
1563	\end{proof}
1564	
1565	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1566	[Half 1 — G1 locality reduction over a finite basic-open cover]
1567	  \label{lem:gf_finite_sections_of_basicOpen_finite_cover}
1568	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gf_finite_sections_of_basicOpen_finite_cover}
1569	  \uses{lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen, lem:finite_localizedModule_transfer,
1570	        lem:module_finite_of_localizationSpan, lem:isLocalization_basicOpen_mathlib}
1571	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Properties of Schemes", Tag 01PB, proof, gluing step
1572	  % (read from references/stacks-properties.tex, L2107-L2109).
1573	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
1574	  % "Thus $M_{f_i}$ is finitely generated for each $i$. Hence we conclude by
1575	  %  Algebra, Lemma \ref{algebra-lemma-cover}."
1576	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Properties of Schemes", Tag 01PB (gluing step,
1577	  Algebra Lemma \texttt{lemma-cover}).}
1578	  Let \(X\) be a scheme, \(\F\) a quasi-coherent sheaf of modules on \(X\), and
1579	  \(W \subseteq X\) an affine open. Suppose \(t \subseteq \Gamma(X, W)\) is a finite
1580	  family with \(\mathrm{Ideal.span}\,t = \top\) (so the basic opens \(D(g)\),
1581	  \(g \in t\), cover \(W\)), and that for each \(g \in t\) the section module
1582	  \(\Gamma(\F, D(g))\) is finite over \(\Gamma(X, D(g))\). Then \(\Gamma(\F, W)\)
1583	  is a finite \(\Gamma(X, W)\)-module.
1584	\end{lemma}
1585	
1586	\begin{proof}
1587	  \uses{lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen, lem:finite_localizedModule_transfer,
1588	        lem:module_finite_of_localizationSpan, lem:isLocalization_basicOpen_mathlib}
1589	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Tag 01PB (gluing step).}
1590	  By the local-on-a-spanning-family criterion \cref{lem:module_finite_of_localizationSpan}
1591	  it suffices to show, for each \(g \in t\), that the localization of
1592	  \(\Gamma(\F, W)\) at \(g\) is finite over the localization
1593	  \((\Gamma(X, W))_g\). Fix \(g \in t\). The basic open \(D(g)\) is affine, and the
1594	  restriction \(\Gamma(X, W) \to \Gamma(X, D(g))\) exhibits \(\Gamma(X, D(g))\) as
1595	  the away-localization \((\Gamma(X, W))_g\)
1596	  (\cref{lem:isLocalization_basicOpen_mathlib}); moreover the section restriction
1597	  \(\Gamma(\F, W) \to \Gamma(\F, D(g))\) exhibits \(\Gamma(\F, D(g))\) as the
1598	  localized module \((\mathrm{powers}\,g)^{-1}\,\Gamma(\F, W)\)
1599	  (\cref{lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen}). Thus \(\Gamma(\F, D(g))\) is a
1600	  model of the localized module, finite over \(\Gamma(X, D(g)) = (\Gamma(X, W))_g\)
1601	  by hypothesis; the model-transfer lemma
1602	  \cref{lem:finite_localizedModule_transfer} carries this finiteness to the
1603	  canonical localized module of \(\Gamma(\F, W)\) at the powers of \(g\), over
1604	  \((\Gamma(X, W))_g\). This supplies the per-element hypothesis, and
1605	  \cref{lem:module_finite_of_localizationSpan} concludes that \(\Gamma(\F, W)\) is
1606	  finite over \(\Gamma(X, W)\).
1607	\end{proof}

1609	\begin{lemma}[Half 2, seam 1a — local generators restrict along a basic-open immersion]
1610	  \label{lem:gf_localGenerators_restrict}
1611	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gf_localGenerators_restrict}
1612	  % LEAN STATUS: seam-1 sub-piece, project-built (Mathlib-absent). Proposed decl
1613	  % AlgebraicGeometry.gf_localGenerators_restrict (TODO). Mathlib provides the
1614	  % SheafOfModules.IsFiniteType local-generators datum (GeneratingSections /
1615	  % LocalGeneratorsData) but no restriction-of-generators lemma along an open
1616	  % immersion; this is the project-built piece.
1617	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(\F : X.\mathrm{Modules}\) a quasi-coherent sheaf of
1618	  modules. Suppose that on an open \(Y \subseteq X\) the restriction \(\F|_Y\) carries a
1619	  finite generating family of sections, i.e. a datum exhibiting \(\F|_Y\) as globally
1620	  generated by finitely many sections (a finite \(\mathrm{LocalGeneratorsData}\) /
1621	  \(\mathrm{GeneratingSections}\) for \(\F.\mathrm{over}\,Y\)). Then for any open immersion
1622	  \(D(g) \hookrightarrow Y\) the restricted family — the images of those generators under
1623	  the section-restriction \(\Gamma(\F, Y) \to \Gamma(\F, D(g))\) — is again a finite
1624	  generating family of \(\F|_{D(g)}\). Generation survives passage to the smaller open
1625	  (a family that generates every stalk over \(Y\) generates every stalk over \(D(g)\)),
1626	  and the index set, and hence finiteness, is unchanged.
1627	\end{lemma}
1628	
1629	\begin{proof}
1630	  A family of sections \(\{s_i\}_{i \in I}\) generates \(\F|_Y\) precisely when the
1631	  induced map \(\OO_Y^{\oplus I} \to \F|_Y\) is surjective on every stalk
1632	  \(\F_y\,(y \in Y)\). Restricting along the open immersion \(D(g) \hookrightarrow Y\) the
1633	  generators map to \(\{s_i|_{D(g)}\}_{i \in I}\), and for a point \(x \in D(g)\) the stalk
1634	  \(\F_x\) and the comparison map agree with the value at \(x\) computed over \(Y\), since
1635	  stalks are insensitive to shrinking the ambient open. Hence the induced map
1636	  \(\OO_{D(g)}^{\oplus I} \to \F|_{D(g)}\) is surjective on every stalk over \(D(g)\), so
1637	  \(\{s_i|_{D(g)}\}_{i \in I}\) generates \(\F|_{D(g)}\). The index set \(I\) is the same
1638	  finite set, so finiteness is preserved.
1639	\end{proof}

1641	\begin{lemma}[Half 2, seam 1b — finite standard-basic-open subcover of an affine open]
1642	  \label{lem:gf_affine_finite_standard_subcover}
1643	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gf_affine_finite_standard_subcover}
1644	  % LEAN STATUS: seam-1 sub-piece, project-built (Mathlib-absent). Proposed decl
1645	  % AlgebraicGeometry.gf_affine_finite_standard_subcover (TODO). Mathlib supplies
1646	  % PrimeSpectrum.isBasis_basic_opens and quasi-compactness of an affine scheme, but
1647	  % the packaged refinement-to-finite-standard-subcover (output t with Ideal.span t = ⊤)
1648	  % is the project-built assembly.
1649	  Let \(W\) be an affine open of a scheme \(X\), with the canonical identification
1650	  \(W \cong \Spec \Gamma(X, W)\). Given an arbitrary open cover \(\{U_\alpha\}\) of \(W\),
1651	  there is a finite family \(t \subseteq \Gamma(X, W)\) with
1652	  \(\mathrm{Ideal.span}\,t = \top\) such that each basic open \(D(g)\) (\(g \in t\)) is
1653	  contained in some cover member \(U_\alpha\). In particular the \(D(g)\,(g \in t)\) form a
1654	  finite standard-basic-open subcover of \(W\) refining \(\{U_\alpha\}\).
1655	\end{lemma}
1656	
1657	\begin{proof}
1658	  Under \(W \cong \Spec \Gamma(X, W)\) the basic opens \(D(g)\,(g \in \Gamma(X, W))\) form a
1659	  basis of the topology (\(\mathrm{PrimeSpectrum.isBasis\_basic\_opens}\)). Hence each
1660	  cover member \(U_\alpha\) is a union of basic opens, and the collection of all basic
1661	  opens \(D(g)\) that are contained in some \(U_\alpha\) still covers \(W\). The affine
1662	  scheme \(W\) is quasi-compact, so finitely many of these basic opens
1663	  \(D(g_1), \dots, D(g_n)\) already cover \(W\); set
1664	  \(t = \{g_1, \dots, g_n\} \subseteq \Gamma(X, W)\), a finite family, each \(D(g_i)\)
1665	  contained in some \(U_\alpha\) by construction. That the \(D(g_i)\) cover
1666	  \(W = \Spec \Gamma(X, W)\) — i.e. no prime ideal contains all the \(g_i\) — is exactly
1667	  the condition \(\mathrm{Ideal.span}\,t = \top\).
1668	\end{proof}

1670	\begin{lemma}[Half 2, seam 1c — finite generation equals a finite free epimorphism]
1671	  \label{lem:gf_finite_gen_iff_free_epi}
1672	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gf_finite_gen_iff_free_epi}
1673	  % LEAN STATUS: seam-1 sub-piece, project-built (Mathlib-absent). Proposed decl
1674	  % AlgebraicGeometry.gf_finite_gen_iff_free_epi (TODO). The equivalence between a
1675	  % finite generating family and a finite-free epimorphism of O-modules is the
1676	  % project-built repackaging of the generators datum as an epi out of O^{⊕I}.
1677	  Let \(Y\) be an open of a scheme and \(\F\) a quasi-coherent sheaf of modules. Then
1678	  \(\F|_Y\) is globally generated by a finite family of sections if and only if there is a
1679	  finite index set \(I\) and an epimorphism of \(\OO_Y\)-modules
1680	  \(\OO_Y^{\oplus I} \twoheadrightarrow \F|_Y\).
1681	\end{lemma}
1682	
1683	\begin{proof}
1684	  A finite family of sections \(\{s_i\}_{i \in I}\) of \(\F|_Y\) is the same datum as an
1685	  \(\OO_Y\)-linear map \(\OO_Y^{\oplus I} \to \F|_Y\) sending the \(i\)-th basis section to
1686	  \(s_i\). The family generates \(\F|_Y\) (the induced map is surjective on every stalk)
1687	  exactly when this map is an epimorphism of sheaves of modules. Thus a finite generating
1688	  family yields a finite-free epimorphism, and conversely the images of the basis sections
1689	  of any epimorphism \(\OO_Y^{\oplus I} \twoheadrightarrow \F|_Y\) with \(I\) finite form a
1690	  finite generating family.
1691	\end{proof}

1713	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Properties of Schemes", Tag 01PB (standard
1714	  cover of generated affines).}
1715	  Let \(X\) be a scheme, \(\F\) a quasi-coherent sheaf of modules of finite type,
1716	  and \(W \subseteq X\) an affine open. Then there is a finite family
1717	  \(t \subseteq \Gamma(X, W)\) with \(\mathrm{Ideal.span}\,t = \top\) such that for
1718	  each \(g \in t\) the basic open \(D(g)\) is affine and \(\F|_{D(g)}\) is globally
1719	  generated by finitely many sections; equivalently, for each \(g \in t\) there is
1720	  a finite index set \(I_g\) and an epimorphism of \(\OO_{D(g)}\)-modules
1721	  \(\OO_{D(g)}^{\oplus I_g} \twoheadrightarrow \F|_{D(g)}\).

1725	  \uses{lem:gf_localGenerators_restrict, lem:gf_affine_finite_standard_subcover,
1726	        lem:gf_finite_gen_iff_free_epi}
1727	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Tag 01PB (standard cover of generated affines).}
1728	  Finite type of \(\F\) means that \(X\) has an open covering \(\{Y_\alpha\}\) on each
1729	  member of which \(\F\) carries a finite generating family of sections. The intersections
1730	  \(\{Y_\alpha \cap W\}\) form an open cover of the affine \(W\). By the finite standard
1731	  subcover refinement \cref{lem:gf_affine_finite_standard_subcover} there is a finite family
1732	  \(t = \{g_1, \dots, g_n\} \subseteq \Gamma(X, W)\) with
1733	  \(\mathrm{Ideal.span}\,t = \top\) and each basic open \(D(g_i)\) contained in some
1734	  \(Y_\alpha \cap W\) — in particular \(D(g_i) \subseteq W\) is affine, and \(D(g_i)\)
1735	  embeds by an open immersion into the member \(Y_\alpha\) on which \(\F\) is finitely
1736	  generated. By the restriction-of-generators lemma
1737	  \cref{lem:gf_localGenerators_restrict} the finite generating family on \(Y_\alpha\)
1738	  restricts to a finite generating family of \(\F|_{D(g_i)}\), so \(\F|_{D(g_i)}\) is
1739	  globally generated by finitely many sections. Finally, by the free-epimorphism
1740	  reformulation \cref{lem:gf_finite_gen_iff_free_epi}, this is equivalent to the existence
1741	  of a finite index set \(I_{g_i}\) and an epimorphism of \(\OO_{D(g_i)}\)-modules
1742	  \(\OO_{D(g_i)}^{\oplus I_{g_i}} \twoheadrightarrow \F|_{D(g_i)}\). That the \(D(g_i)\)
1743	  cover \(W = \Spec \Gamma(X, W)\) is exactly \(\mathrm{Ideal.span}\,t = \top\). This is
1744	  Stacks' passage from the local-generation datum to a standard finite cover of generated
1745	  affines.
1746	\end{proof}

1789	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Tag 01PB (sections of a generated sheaf over an
1790	  affine).}
1791	  Write \(V \cong \Spec B\) and let \(\Gamma\) be the affine section functor on
1792	  \((\Spec B).\mathrm{Modules}\), so that \(\Gamma(\pi) = \Gamma(\mathcal{G}, V) \to
1793	  \Gamma(\F, V)\) is the map in question. By \cref{lem:tilde_adjunction_mathlib} the
1794	  associated-sheaf functor \(\widetilde{(-)}\) is left adjoint to \(\Gamma\), with counit the
1795	  natural transformation whose component at a sheaf \(\mathcal{M}\) is
1796	  \(\mathcal{M}.\mathrm{fromTildeΓ} : \widetilde{\Gamma(\mathcal{M}, V)} \to \mathcal{M}\).
1797	
1798	  Because \(\mathcal{G}\) and \(\F\) are quasi-coherent, the qcoh-sections descent
1799	  (\cref{lem:qcoh_affine_section_localization}, fed through the counit-iso assembly
1800	  \cref{lem:isIso_fromTildeΓ_of_isLocalizedModule_restrict}) makes both counit components
1801	  \(\mathcal{G}.\mathrm{fromTildeΓ}\) and \(\F.\mathrm{fromTildeΓ}\) isomorphisms. Naturality of the
1802	  counit on \(\pi\) gives the commuting square
1803	  \[
1804	    \widetilde{\Gamma(\pi)} \circ \mathcal{G}.\mathrm{fromTildeΓ}^{-1}
1805	      = \F.\mathrm{fromTildeΓ}^{-1} \circ \pi,
1806	    \qquad\text{equivalently}\qquad
1807	    \widetilde{\Gamma(\pi)}
1808	      = \F.\mathrm{fromTildeΓ}^{-1} \circ \pi \circ \mathcal{G}.\mathrm{fromTildeΓ}.
1809	  \]

1811	  isomorphism, hence is itself an epimorphism. Thus \(\widetilde{\Gamma(\pi)}\) is an epimorphism
1812	  of sheaves of modules. Since the left adjoint \(\widetilde{(-)}\) is faithful, it reflects
1813	  epimorphisms; therefore \(\Gamma(\pi)\) is an epimorphism in \(\mathrm{Mod}\,B\), i.e. the
1814	  \(B\)-linear map \(\Gamma(\pi)\) is surjective.

1818	  obtained here directly from the affine adjunction without invoking a cohomology computation.

1963	  affine open \(U_0 = \Spec A \subseteq S\) with \(A\) a noetherian domain, a finite
1964	  affine cover \(\{W_j = \Spec B_j\}_j\) of \(p^{-1}(U_0)\), and an element
1965	  \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\), such that for every \(j\) the localized section module
1966	  \((M_j)_f\) (with \(M_j = \Gamma(\F, W_j)\)) is free over \(A_f\). Set
1967	  \(V = D(f) \subseteq U_0\). Then for every affine open \(U \le V\) of \(S\) and every
1968	  affine open \(W \le p^{-1}(U)\) of \(X\), the section module \(\Gamma(\F, W)\) is flat
1969	  over \(\Gamma(S, U)\).
1970	\end{lemma}
1971	
1972	\begin{proof}
1973	  \uses{lem:gf_qcoh_fintype_finite_sections}
1974	  \emph{Free \(\Rightarrow\) flat.} Each \((M_j)_f\) is free over \(A_f\) by hypothesis,
1975	  and a free module is flat (via free \(\Rightarrow\) projective \(\Rightarrow\) flat).
1976	  Hence each \((M_j)_f\) is flat over \(A_f\).
1977	
1978	  \emph{Flat-locality on the source.} Flatness of a module is local on the base ring: a
1979	  module is flat over a ring as soon as its localizations at the members of a covering
1980	  family (e.g. a spanning family of elements, or all maximal ideals) are flat over the
1981	  corresponding localizations of the ring. The affine cover
1982	  \(\{W_j = \Spec B_j\}_j\) of \(p^{-1}(U_0)\) provides exactly such a covering family of
1983	  the section algebra over \(A_f\); since each patch contribution \((M_j)_f\) is flat over
1984	  \(A_f\), the flat-locality criterion assembles the per-patch flatness into flatness of the
1985	  section module of \(\F\) over \(A_f = \Gamma(S, V)\) on the whole preimage \(p^{-1}(V)\).
1986	
1987	  \emph{Stability under base-restriction.} For an affine \(U \le V\) the ring
1988	  \(\Gamma(S, U)\) is a localization of \(\Gamma(S, V) = A_f\) (an open immersion of an
1989	  affine into a basic open of \(\Spec A_f\) realizes the smaller global sections as a
1990	  localization), and for an affine
1991	  \(W \le p^{-1}(U)\) the module \(\Gamma(\F, W)\) is the corresponding localization of the
1992	  finite section module over the relevant patch \(W_j\) — the finite section modules and
1993	  their localizations being identified across the cover by
1994	  \cref{lem:gf_qcoh_fintype_finite_sections}. Localization preserves flatness of both ring
1995	  and module: a localization of a flat module along a localization of the base ring is
1996	  again flat. Therefore \(\Gamma(\F, W)\) is flat over \(\Gamma(S, U)\), as claimed.
1997	\end{proof}
1998	
1999	
2000	\begin{theorem}
2001	
2002	
2003	
2004	\leanok
2005	[Generic flatness, geometric form]
2006	  \label{thm:generic_flatness}
2007	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.genericFlatness}
2008	  \uses{thm:generic_flatness_algebraic}
2009	  % NOTE (re-signed): the Lean signature `AlgebraicGeometry.genericFlatness`
2010	  % previously took `F : X.Modules` with NO coherence hypothesis, under which the
2011	  % statement is FALSE (a non-coherent module sheaf need not become flat on any
2012	  % non-empty open). The decl (not in archon-protected.yaml) was re-signed to carry
2013	  % the coherence encoding below; the Lean file is the source of truth for the exact
2014	  % binders and the full flatness conclusion.
2015	  %
2016	  % Mathlib has no single `IsCoherent` predicate at this pin; coherence over a
2017	  % locally noetherian base is encoded as the conjunction of two existing
2018	  % SheafOfModules predicates on `F`:
2019	  %     [F.IsQuasicoherent]   (SheafOfModules.IsQuasicoherent,
2020	  %                            Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Sheaf.Quasicoherent)
2021	  %     [F.IsFiniteType]      (SheafOfModules.IsFiniteType,
2022	  %                            Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Sheaf.Generators)
2023	  %
2024	  % LEAN SIGNATURE HEADER (matches the landed .lean file):
2025	  %   theorem genericFlatness {S X : Scheme.{u}} [IsIntegral S] [IsLocallyNoetherian S]
2026	  %       (p : X ⟶ S) [LocallyOfFiniteType p] [QuasiCompact p] (F : X.Modules)
2027	  %       [F.IsQuasicoherent] [F.IsFiniteType] :
2028	  %       ∃ (V : S.Opens), (V : Set S).Nonempty ∧ (the fibrewise flatness conclusion,
2029	  %       quantified over affine opens U ≤ V of S and affine opens W ≤ p⁻¹U of X, with
2030	  %       Γ(F,W) given its Γ(S,U)-module structure via p.appLE — verbatim form in the
2031	  %       .lean file, the source of truth).
2032	  %
2033	  % NOTE (iter-023): `[QuasiCompact p]` is essential — LocallyOfFiniteType does NOT
2034	  % entail QuasiCompact; without it the statement is false (counterexample:
2035	  % ⊔_i Spec ℤ → Spec ℤ with a finite-type torsion sheaf). It is the extra clause
2036	  % promoting `LocallyOfFiniteType` to `FiniteType`, and there is no instance
2037	  % `LocallyOfFiniteType → QuasiCompact`. The header above carries it, and the Step-1
2038	  % proof prose below invokes it directly (no false "finite type ⟹ quasi-compact" step).
2039	
2040	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4, Theorem ``generic flatness''
2041	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1781-L1787).
2042	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
2043	  % "Let \(S\) be a noetherian and integral scheme.
2044	  %  Let \(p: X\to S\) be a finite type morphism, and let \(\F\) be a
2045	  %  coherent sheaf of \({\mathcal O}_X\)-modules. Then there exists a non-empty
2046	  %  open subscheme \(U\subset S\) such that the restriction of \(\F\)
2047	  %  to \(X_U = p^{-1}(U)\) is flat over \({\mathcal O}_U\)."
2048	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, Theorem ``generic flatness''.}
2049	  Let \(S\) be a noetherian integral scheme, \(p : X \to S\) a finite-type morphism,
2050	  and \(\F\) a coherent \(\OO_X\)-module. Then there exists a non-empty open
2051	  subscheme \(U \subseteq S\) such that the restriction of \(\F\) to
2052	  \(X_U = p^{-1}(U)\) is \(\OO_U\)-flat.
2053	
2054	  The coherence hypothesis on \(\F\) is essential: a non-coherent \(\OO_X\)-module
2055	  need not become flat over any non-empty open of \(S\).
2056	\end{theorem}
2057	
2058	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "The above theorem has the following consequence, which
2059	% follows by restricting attention to a non-empty affine open subscheme of \(S\)."
2060	% (Source: nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1776-L1777. The
2061	% Nitsure proof is the single sentence quoted: deduce the geometric form from
2062	% the algebraic generic flatness statement (\cref{thm:generic_flatness_algebraic})
2063	% by restricting to a non-empty
2064	% affine open of \(S\), where the situation reduces to the algebraic statement.)
2065	\begin{proof}
2066	  \uses{thm:generic_flatness_algebraic, lem:gf_qcoh_fintype_finite_sections,
2067	        lem:gf_flat_locality_assembly}
2068	  Restrict to a non-empty affine open \(U_0 = \Spec A \subseteq S\), where \(A\) is a
2069	  noetherian domain (\(S\) integral and locally noetherian). We assemble the witness
2070	  \(V\) from the algebraic form on a finite affine cover.
2071	
2072	  \emph{Step 1 — finite affine cover above \(U_0\).} Since \(p\) is quasi-compact (by
2073	  the \([\mathrm{QuasiCompact}\,p]\) hypothesis) and \(U_0\) is affine hence
2074	  quasi-compact, the preimage \(X_{U_0} = p^{-1}(U_0)\) is quasi-compact; choose a
2075	  finite affine cover \(\{W_j = \Spec B_j\}_{j}\) of \(X_{U_0}\) by affine opens of
2076	  \(X\). Because \(p\) is locally of finite type, each ring \(B_j = \Gamma(X, W_j)\)
2077	  is a finite-type \(A\)-algebra.
2078	
2079	  \emph{Step 2 — read off a finite module on each patch.} On each \(W_j\), quasi-coherence
2080	  of \(\F\) identifies \(\F|_{W_j}\) with the associated sheaf \(\widetilde{M_j}\) of the
2081	  section module \(M_j = \Gamma(\F, W_j)\), and the finite-section bridge
2082	  \cref{lem:gf_qcoh_fintype_finite_sections} — applied to the affine open \(W_j\) — makes
2083	  \(M_j\) a finite \(B_j\)-module. Through the scalar tower \(A \to B_j \to M_j\),
2084	  \(M_j\) is the data fed to the algebraic form.
2085	
2086	  \emph{Step 3 — apply the algebraic form and take the common basic open.}
2087	  \cref{thm:generic_flatness_algebraic} applied to \((A, B_j, M_j)\) yields \(f_j \in A\),
2088	  \(f_j \ne 0\), with \((M_j)_{f_j}\) free over \(A_{f_j}\). As the cover is finite, set
2089	  \(f := \prod_j f_j \ne 0\) (a non-zero element of the domain \(A\)) and
2090	  \(V := D(f) \subseteq \Spec A \subseteq S\), a non-empty basic open; over \(V\) every
2091	  \((M_j)_f\) is free over \(A_f\).
2092	
2093	  \emph{Step 4 — freeness implies flatness.} On each patch the localized section module
2094	  is free, hence flat over \(A_f\); the flat-locality assembly
2095	  \cref{lem:gf_flat_locality_assembly} promotes these per-patch freeness statements to
2096	  the fibrewise flatness conclusion, via the criterion that a module flat at every
2097	  maximal ideal is flat together with the locality of flatness on the source cover. This
2098	  produces the required \(V\) with \(\F|_{X_V}\) flat over \(\OO_V\).
2099	\end{proof}
2100	
2101	\section{Mathlib status}
2102	\label{sec:flatstrat_mathlib_status}
2103	
2104	Mathlib (master \texttt{b80f227}) provides \texttt{Module.Flat}
2105	(\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Flat.Basic}), the module-theoretic notion of
2106	flatness applicable affine-locally to the section modules of \(\F\), together
2107	with the dévissage and Noether-normalisation infrastructure that the algebraic
2108	generic-flatness argument consumes
2109	(\texttt{IsNoetherianRing.induction\_on\_isQuotientEquivQuotientPrime},
2110	\texttt{Module.FinitePresentation.exists\_free\_localizedModule\_powers},
2111	\texttt{exists\_finite\_inj\_algHom\_of\_fg}).  It does \emph{not} yet contain
2112	the polynomial-ring core of generic freeness (a finite module over
2113	\(A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\) becomes free after inverting a single element), nor the
2114	geometric statement \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.genericFlatness}
2115	(\cref{thm:generic_flatness}) packaging the algebraic form
2116	(\cref{thm:generic_flatness_algebraic}) over a noetherian integral base.  These
2117	are introduced in
2118	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/FlatteningStratification.lean}.

1	\chapter{Regrouping isomorphism for the affine base-change tensor tower}
2	\label{chap:Cohomology_RegroupHelper}
3	% This chapter blueprints the standalone, geometry-free helper that lives in
4	% AlgebraicJacobian/Cohomology/RegroupHelper.lean (1:1 slug). It is the pure
5	% tensor-algebra core of lem:base_change_mate_regroupEquiv in the FlatBaseChange
6	% chapter; it is split into its own compilation unit so the object-level
7	% ModuleCat iso can be obtained by a single LinearEquiv.toModuleIso application
8	% (the separate import normalises the `Module A (A ⊗[R] R')` instance diamond
9	% that blocks the same-file construction).
10	
11	\section{The R'-linear regrouping equivalence}
12	
13	\begin{lemma}
14	
15	\leanok
16	[Regrouping linear equivalence for the tensor tower]
17	  \label{lem:base_change_regroup_linearEquiv}
18	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.base_change_regroup_linearEquiv}
19	  \uses{lem:isPushout_cancelBaseChange_mathlib}
20	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma
21	  % `lemma-affine-base-change' (\S\,Cohomology and base change, I), proof, the
22	  % "boils down to the equality" step (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex,
23	  % L933--938).
24	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Thus we see that the lemma boils down to the
25	  % equality
26	  % $$
27	  % (R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M = R' \otimes_R M
28	  % $$
29	  % as $R'$-modules."
30	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma ``Affine base
31	  change'' (proof, ``boils down to the equality'' step).}
32	  Let \(\psi : R \to R'\) and \(\varphi : R \to A\) be ring homomorphisms and \(M\)
33	  an \(A\)-module. On the \emph{canonical} Mathlib instances (with \(R'\) acting on
34	  \(A \otimes_R R'\) through the \(\texttt{Algebra.TensorProduct.rightAlgebra}\)
35	  factor, i.e.\ \(r' \cdot (a \otimes s) = a \otimes (r' s)\)) there is a bundled
36	  \(R'\)-linear isomorphism
37	  \[
38	    \operatorname{regroup} :
39	    (A \otimes_R R') \otimes_A M \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\; R' \otimes_R M ,
40	  \]
41	  the ``equality as \(R'\)-modules'' of the affine base-change source. On generators
42	  it sends \((a \otimes r') \otimes m \mapsto r' \otimes (a \cdot m)\); in
43	  particular \((1 \otimes r') \otimes m \mapsto r' \otimes m\), with inverse
44	  \(r' \otimes m \mapsto (1 \otimes r') \otimes m\). It carries \emph{no} flatness
45	  hypothesis.
46	\end{lemma}
47	
48	\begin{proof}
49	  \uses{lem:isPushout_cancelBaseChange_mathlib}
50	  \leanok
51	  This is pure tensor algebra, and it is supplied \emph{directly} by Mathlib's
52	  pushout-form base-change cancellation
53	  \(\operatorname{Algebra.IsPushout.cancelBaseChange}\)
54	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:isPushout_cancelBaseChange_mathlib}), instantiated at \(S = R'\),
55	  \(B = A \otimes_R R'\):
56	  \[
57	    \operatorname{cancelBaseChange} R\,R'\,A\,(A \otimes_R R')\,M :
58	    (A \otimes_R R') \otimes_A M \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\; R' \otimes_R M .
59	  \]
60	  The required pushout instance \(\operatorname{Algebra.IsPushout} R\,R'\,A\,
61	  (A \otimes_R R')\) is supplied for free by \(\operatorname{TensorProduct.isPushout'}\).
62	  The decisive structural point is that this equivalence is \emph{natively}
63	  \(R'\)-linear: the cancellation is bundled as a \(\operatorname{LinearEquiv}\) over
64	  \(R'\) (its base-ring slot is \(S = R'\)), so the \(R'\)-linearity is part of the
65	  Mathlib datum with no hand-written scalar-compatibility obligation.
66	  On the generator it computes, by \(\texttt{cancelBaseChange\_tmul}\),
67	  \[
68	    (a \otimes r') \otimes m \;\longmapsto\; r' \otimes (a \cdot m) ,
69	  \]
70	  with inverse \(r' \otimes m \mapsto (1 \otimes r') \otimes m\)
71	  (\(\texttt{cancelBaseChange\_symm\_tmul}\)); the \(R'\)-action enters through the
72	  \(A \otimes_R R'\) factor (\(r' \cdot (a \otimes s) = a \otimes (r' s)\)). Being a
73	  single bundled Mathlib linear equivalence it is an \(R'\)-linear isomorphism, and it
74	  carries no flatness hypothesis.
75	
76	\end{proof}
77
