1	\chapter{The Grassmannian over $\mathbb{Z}$: affine charts and gluing}
2	\label{chap:Picard_GrassmannianCells}
3	
4	\section{Set-up: charts indexed by $d$-subsets}
5	\label{sec:gr_setup}
6	
7	Fix integers \(r \ge d \ge 1\). For a \(d \times r\) matrix \(M\) over a ring
8	and a subset \(I \subseteq \{1, \dots, r\}\) with \(\#(I) = d\), write \(M_I\)
9	for the \emph{\(I\)-th minor} of \(M\): the \(d \times d\) submatrix whose
10	columns are those indexed by \(I\). We construct the Grassmannian scheme
11	\(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) over \(\mathbb{Z}\) by gluing one affine chart \(U^I\) for
12	each such \(I\). The construction is, conceptually, the quotient
13	\(\mathrm{GL}_{d,\mathbb{Z}} \backslash V\) of the scheme \(V\) of rank-\(d\)
14	matrices, but we never use the language of group-scheme actions: the charts and
15	their transition maps are elementary matrix algebra over \(\mathbb{Z}\). Taking
16	\(d = 1\) recovers \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,1) = \mathbb{P}^{r-1}_{\mathbb{Z}}\).
17	
18	\begin{definition}
19	
20	\leanok
21	[Affine chart \(U^I\)]
22	  \label{def:gr_affine_chart}
23	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.affineChart}
24	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
25	  % sub-section "Construction by gluing together affine patches" (read from
26	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L807-L821).
27	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
28	  % "If $M$ is a $d\times r$-matrix, and
29	  %  $I\subset \{1,\ldots, r\}$ with cardinality $\#(I)$ equal
30	  %  to $d$, the $I$ th minor $M_I$ of $M$ will
31	  %  mean the $d\times d$ minor of $M$ whose columns are indexed by
32	  %  $I$.
33	  %
34	  %  For any subset $I\subset \{1,\ldots, r\}$ with $\#(I) =d$,
35	  %  consider the $d\times r$ matrix $X^I$ whose $I$ the minor
36	  %  $X^I_I$ is the $d\times d$ identity matrix $1_{d\times d}$, while the remaining
37	  %  entries of $X^I$ are independent variables $x^I_{p,q}$ over $\Z$.
38	  %  Let $\Z[X^I]$ denote the polynomial ring in the variables
39	  %  $x^I_{p,q}$, and let $U^I = \spec \Z[X^I]$, which is non-canonically
40	  %  isomorphic to the affine space $\AA^{d(r-d)}_{\Z}$."
41	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
42	  Let \(I \subseteq \{1, \dots, r\}\) with \(\#(I) = d\). Let \(X^I\) denote the
43	  \(d \times r\) matrix whose \(I\)-th minor \(X^I_I\) is the \(d \times d\)
44	  identity matrix \(1_{d \times d}\) and whose remaining \(d(r - d)\) entries are
45	  independent indeterminates \(x^I_{p,q}\) (\(1 \le p \le d\),
46	  \(q \in \{1,\dots,r\} \setminus I\)) over \(\mathbb{Z}\). Write
47	  \(\mathbb{Z}[X^I]\) for the polynomial ring in these \(d(r-d)\) variables and
48	  set
49	  \[
50	    U^I \;:=\; \Spec \mathbb{Z}[X^I].
51	  \]
52	  The choice of an ordering of the free entries gives a (non-canonical)
53	  isomorphism \(U^I \cong \mathbb{A}^{d(r-d)}_{\mathbb{Z}}\); in particular
54	  \(U^I\) is a smooth affine \(\mathbb{Z}\)-scheme of relative dimension
55	  \(d(r-d)\). Intuitively a \(\mathbb{Z}\)-point of \(U^I\) is a \(d \times r\)
56	  matrix whose \(I\)-columns form the identity, i.e.\ a rank-\(d\) quotient of
57	  \(\mathbb{Z}^{r}\) trivialised on the coordinates \(I\).
58	\end{definition}
59	
60	\section{Transition maps and the cocycle condition}
61	\label{sec:gr_transition}
62	
63	\subsection*{Mathlib inputs for the transition map}
64	
65	The construction of \(\theta_{I,J}\) is a Cramer-inverse computation over the
66	localised chart ring, followed by one application of the universal property of a
67	localisation. We record the four Mathlib facts it rests on as dependency
68	anchors.
69	
70	\begin{lemma}
71	[Localised element is a unit]
72	  \label{lem:mathlib_away_algebraMap_isUnit}
73	  \lean{IsLocalization.Away.algebraMap_isUnit}
74	  \mathlibok
75	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
76	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring, \(x \in R\), and \(S\) a localisation of \(R\)
77	  away from \(x\) (i.e.\ \(S = R[1/x]\)). Then the image of \(x\) under the
78	  structure map \(R \to S\) is a unit of \(S\).
79	\end{lemma}
80	
81	\begin{lemma}
82	[Left Cramer inverse]
83	  \label{lem:mathlib_nonsing_inv_mul}
84	  \lean{Matrix.nonsing_inv_mul}
85	  \mathlibok
86	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
87	  For a square matrix \(A\) over a commutative ring with \(\det A\) a unit, the
88	  nonsingular inverse \(A^{-1}\) satisfies \(A^{-1} A = 1\).
89	\end{lemma}
90	
91	\begin{lemma}
92	[Right Cramer inverse]
93	  \label{lem:mathlib_mul_nonsing_inv}
94	  \lean{Matrix.mul_nonsing_inv}
95	  \mathlibok
96	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
97	  For a square matrix \(A\) over a commutative ring with \(\det A\) a unit, the
98	  nonsingular inverse \(A^{-1}\) satisfies \(A A^{-1} = 1\).
99	\end{lemma}
100	
101	\begin{lemma}
102	[Universal property of an away-localisation]
103	  \label{lem:mathlib_away_lift}
104	  \lean{IsLocalization.Away.lift}
105	  \mathlibok
106	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
107	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring, \(x \in R\), and \(S = R[1/x]\) a localisation
108	  of \(R\) away from \(x\). Given any ring homomorphism \(g : R \to P\) that
109	  sends \(x\) to a unit of \(P\), there is a unique ring homomorphism
110	  \(S \to P\) extending \(g\) along \(R \to S\).
111	\end{lemma}
112	
113	\subsection*{The transition map by matrix algebra}
114	
115	We now build \(\theta_{I,J}\) in seven steps over the localised chart ring
116	\(R^I_J := \mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_J]\), splitting at the matrix-algebra seams. The
117	ambient chart ring \(\mathbb{Z}[X^I]\) is the polynomial ring of
118	\cref{def:gr_affine_chart} in the \(d(r-d)\) free variables \(x^I_{p,q}\)
119	(\(p \in \{1,\dots,d\}\), \(q \notin I\)).
120	
121	\begin{definition}
122	
123	\leanok
124	[The universal matrix \(X^I\)]
125	  \label{def:gr_universal_matrix}
126	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.universalMatrix}
127	  \uses{def:gr_affine_chart}
128	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
129	  % sub-section "Construction by gluing together affine patches" (read from
130	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L807-L821).
131	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
132	  % "For any subset $I\subset \{1,\ldots, r\}$ with $\#(I) =d$,
133	  %  consider the $d\times r$ matrix $X^I$ whose $I$ the minor
134	  %  $X^I_I$ is the $d\times d$ identity matrix $1_{d\times d}$, while the remaining
135	  %  entries of $X^I$ are independent variables $x^I_{p,q}$ over $\Z$."
136	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
137	  Let \(I \subseteq \{1,\dots,r\}\) with \(\#(I) = d\). The \emph{universal
138	  matrix} \(X^I\) is the \(d \times r\) matrix over the chart ring
139	  \(\mathbb{Z}[X^I]\) whose \(I\)-minor \(X^I_I\) is the \(d \times d\) identity
140	  \(1_{d\times d}\) and whose remaining entries are the free indeterminates
141	  \(x^I_{p,q}\) (\(1 \le p \le d\), \(q \notin I\)). It is the universal point of
142	  the chart \(U^I\) (\cref{def:gr_affine_chart}): a ring map
143	  \(\mathbb{Z}[X^I] \to A\) is the same as a \(d \times r\) matrix over \(A\)
144	  with identity \(I\)-minor.
145	\end{definition}
146	
147	\begin{definition}
148	
149	\leanok
150	[The minor determinant \(P^I_J\)]
151	  \label{def:gr_minor_det}
152	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.minorDet}
153	  \uses{def:gr_universal_matrix}
154	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Construction by gluing together
155	  % affine patches" (read from
156	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L822-L836).
157	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
158	  % "For any $J\subset \{1,\ldots, r\}$ with $\#(J) =d$, let
159	  %  $P^I_J = \det(X^I_J) \in \Z[X^I]$
160	  %  where $X^I_J$ is the $J$ th minor of $X^I$."
161	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
162	  For \(J \subseteq \{1,\dots,r\}\) with \(\#(J) = d\), let \(X^I_J\) denote the
163	  \(J\)-th minor of the universal matrix \(X^I\) (\cref{def:gr_universal_matrix})
164	  -- the \(d \times d\) submatrix whose columns are those indexed by \(J\). Set
165	  \[
166	    P^I_J \;:=\; \det\bigl(X^I_J\bigr) \;\in\; \mathbb{Z}[X^I].
167	  \]
168	  Then \(R^I_J := \mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_J]\) (the localisation of the chart ring
169	  away from \(P^I_J\)) is the ring of the principal open subscheme
170	  \(U^I_J = \Spec \mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_J] \subseteq U^I\) on which \(P^I_J\) is
171	  invertible. One has \(P^I_I = \det(1_{d\times d}) = 1\), so \(R^I_I =
172	  \mathbb{Z}[X^I]\) and \(U^I_I = U^I\).
173	\end{definition}
174	
175	\begin{definition}
176	
177	\leanok
178	[The localised \(J\)-minor \(X^I_J\) over \(R^I_J\)]
179	  \label{def:gr_universal_minor}
180	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.universalMinor}
181	  \uses{def:gr_universal_matrix, def:gr_minor_det}
182	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Construction by gluing together
183	  % affine patches" (read from
184	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L822-L836).
185	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
186	  % "Let $U^I_J = \spec \Z[X^I, 1/P^I_J]$
187	  %  the open subscheme of $U^I$ where $P^I_J$ is invertible."
188	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
189	  Write \(X^I_J \in \operatorname{Mat}_{d \times d}(R^I_J)\) for the \(J\)-minor of
190	  the universal matrix \(X^I\) (\cref{def:gr_universal_matrix}) with its entries
191	  pushed forward along the structure map \(\mathbb{Z}[X^I] \to R^I_J\) into the
192	  localised chart ring \(R^I_J = \mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_J]\)
193	  (\cref{def:gr_minor_det}). By construction \(\det(X^I_J)\) is the image of
194	  \(P^I_J\) in \(R^I_J\).
195	\end{definition}
196	
197	\begin{lemma}
198	
199	\leanok
200	[The localised minor determinant is a unit]
201	  \label{lem:gr_minorDet_unit}
202	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.isUnit_det_universalMinor}
203	  \uses{def:gr_universal_minor, def:gr_minor_det, lem:mathlib_away_algebraMap_isUnit}
204	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Construction by gluing together
205	  % affine patches" (read from
206	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L822-L836).
207	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
208	  % "the open subscheme of $U^I$ where $P^I_J$ is invertible. This means
209	  %  the $d\times d$-matrix $X^I_J$ admits an inverse $(X^I_J)^{-1}$
210	  %  on $U^I_J$."
211	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
212	  The determinant of the localised \(J\)-minor \(X^I_J\)
213	  (\cref{def:gr_universal_minor}) is a unit of \(R^I_J\):
214	  \[
215	    \det\bigl(X^I_J\bigr) \in (R^I_J)^{\times}.
216	  \]
217	\end{lemma}
218	\begin{proof}
219	  \uses{def:gr_universal_minor, def:gr_minor_det, lem:mathlib_away_algebraMap_isUnit}
220	  \leanok
221	  By \cref{def:gr_universal_minor}, \(\det(X^I_J)\) is the image of \(P^I_J\)
222	  (\cref{def:gr_minor_det}) under the structure map
223	  \(\mathbb{Z}[X^I] \to R^I_J\). Since \(R^I_J\) is the localisation away from
224	  \(P^I_J\), that image is a unit by
225	  \cref{lem:mathlib_away_algebraMap_isUnit}.
226	\end{proof}
227	
228	\begin{definition}
229	
230	\leanok
231	[The Cramer inverse \((X^I_J)^{-1}\)]
232	  \label{def:gr_universalMinorInv}
233	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.universalMinorInv}
234	  \uses{def:gr_universal_minor}
235	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Construction by gluing together
236	  % affine patches" (read from
237	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L822-L836).
238	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
239	  % "the $d\times d$-matrix $X^I_J$ admits an inverse $(X^I_J)^{-1}$
240	  %  on $U^I_J$."
241	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
242	  Let \((X^I_J)^{-1} \in \operatorname{Mat}_{d \times d}(R^I_J)\) be the
243	  nonsingular (Cramer) inverse of the localised \(J\)-minor \(X^I_J\)
244	  (\cref{def:gr_universal_minor}). Its entries lie in \(R^I_J\) by Cramer's rule.
245	\end{definition}
246	
247	\begin{lemma}
248	
249	\leanok
250	[The Cramer inverse is a two-sided inverse]
251	  \label{lem:gr_universalMinorInv_identities}
252	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.universalMinorInv_mul_cancel}
253	  \uses{def:gr_universalMinorInv, lem:gr_minorDet_unit, lem:mathlib_nonsing_inv_mul, lem:mathlib_mul_nonsing_inv}
254	  Because \(\det(X^I_J)\) is a unit of \(R^I_J\) (\cref{lem:gr_minorDet_unit}),
255	  the Cramer inverse \((X^I_J)^{-1}\) (\cref{def:gr_universalMinorInv}) is a
256	  genuine two-sided inverse:
257	  \[
258	    (X^I_J)^{-1}\, X^I_J = 1_{d\times d}
259	    \qquad\text{and}\qquad
260	    X^I_J\, (X^I_J)^{-1} = 1_{d\times d}.
261	  \]
262	\end{lemma}
263	\begin{proof}
264	  \uses{def:gr_universalMinorInv, lem:gr_minorDet_unit, lem:mathlib_nonsing_inv_mul, lem:mathlib_mul_nonsing_inv}
265	  \leanok
266	  Immediate from \cref{lem:gr_minorDet_unit} together with
267	  \cref{lem:mathlib_nonsing_inv_mul} (left identity) and
268	  \cref{lem:mathlib_mul_nonsing_inv} (right identity), both of which apply once
269	  \(\det(X^I_J)\) is known to be a unit.
270	\end{proof}
271	
272	\begin{definition}
273	
274	\leanok
275	[The image matrix \(M = (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\)]
276	  \label{def:gr_image_matrix}
277	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.imageMatrix}
278	  \uses{def:gr_universalMinorInv, def:gr_universal_matrix}
279	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Construction by gluing together
280	  % affine patches" (read from
281	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L822-L836).
282	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
283	  % "The images of the variables
284	  %  $x^J_{p,q}$ are given by the entries of the matrix formula
285	  %  $\theta_{I,J}(X^J) = (X^I_J) ^{-1}\,X^I$."
286	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
287	  Define the \(d \times r\) matrix over \(R^I_J\)
288	  \[
289	    M \;:=\; (X^I_J)^{-1}\, X^I \;\in\; \operatorname{Mat}_{d \times r}(R^I_J),
290	  \]
291	  the product of the Cramer inverse (\cref{def:gr_universalMinorInv}) with the
292	  universal matrix \(X^I\) (\cref{def:gr_universal_matrix}) base-changed to
293	  \(R^I_J\). Its entries are the prospective images of the indeterminates
294	  \(x^J_{p,q}\) under \(\theta_{I,J}\). Its \(J\)-minor is \((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I_J =
295	  1_{d\times d}\) (\cref{lem:gr_universalMinorInv_identities}), so \(M\) is a
296	  point of the chart \(U^J\); and its \(I\)-minor is \((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I_I =
297	  (X^I_J)^{-1}\).
298	\end{definition}
299	
300	\begin{definition}
301	
302	\leanok
303	[The pre-localisation hom \(\tilde\theta_{I,J} : \mathbb{Z}[X^J] \to R^I_J\)]
304	  \label{def:gr_transition_pre}
305	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.transitionPreMap}
306	  \uses{def:gr_image_matrix}
307	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Construction by gluing together
308	  % affine patches" (read from
309	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L822-L836).
310	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
311	  % "a ring homomorphism
312	  %  $\theta_{I,J} : \Z[X^J, 1/P^J_I] \to \Z[X^I, 1/P^I_J]$ is
313	  %  defined as follows. The images of the variables
314	  %  $x^J_{p,q}$ are given by the entries of the matrix formula
315	  %  $\theta_{I,J}(X^J) = (X^I_J) ^{-1}\,X^I$."
316	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
317	  Let \(\tilde\theta_{I,J} : \mathbb{Z}[X^J] \to R^I_J\) be the
318	  \(\mathbb{Z}\)-algebra homomorphism out of the chart ring of \(J\) determined,
319	  via the universal property of the polynomial ring, by sending each free
320	  indeterminate \(x^J_{p,q}\) to the \((p,q)\)-entry of the image matrix \(M =
321	  (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\) (\cref{def:gr_image_matrix}); equivalently
322	  \(\tilde\theta_{I,J}(X^J) = (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\) as a matrix identity (the
323	  identity \(J\)-block of \(X^J\) matching the identity \(J\)-minor of \(M\)).
324	\end{definition}
325	
326	\begin{lemma}
327	
328	\leanok
329	[The pre-hom sends \(P^J_I\) to a unit]
330	  \label{lem:gr_transition_pre_unit}
331	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.isUnit_transitionPreMap_minorDet}
332	  \uses{def:gr_transition_pre, def:gr_minor_det, lem:gr_universalMinorInv_identities, lem:gr_minorDet_unit}
333	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Construction by gluing together
334	  % affine patches" (read from
335	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L822-L836).
336	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
337	  % "In particular, we have $\theta_{I,J}(P^J_I) = 1/P^I_J$,
338	  %  so the map extends to $\Z[X^J, 1/P^J_I]$."
339	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
340	  The pre-localisation hom \(\tilde\theta_{I,J}\) (\cref{def:gr_transition_pre})
341	  sends \(P^J_I\) (\cref{def:gr_minor_det}, the \(I\)-minor determinant of
342	  \(X^J\)) to a unit of \(R^I_J\). Indeed
343	  \[
344	    \tilde\theta_{I,J}(P^J_I) = \det\bigl((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I_I\bigr)
345	      = \det\bigl((X^I_J)^{-1}\bigr) = \det(X^I_J)^{-1} = 1/P^I_J,
346	  \]
347	  which is a unit.
348	\end{lemma}
349	\begin{proof}
350	  \uses{def:gr_transition_pre, def:gr_minor_det, lem:gr_universalMinorInv_identities, lem:gr_minorDet_unit}
351	  \leanok
352	  By \cref{def:gr_transition_pre}, \(\tilde\theta_{I,J}(X^J) = M = (X^I_J)^{-1}
353	  X^I\), so \(\tilde\theta_{I,J}\) carries the \(I\)-minor \(X^J_I\) of \(X^J\) to
354	  the \(I\)-minor of \(M\), which is \((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I_I = (X^I_J)^{-1}\) since
355	  the \(I\)-minor of \(X^I\) is the identity. Hence \(\tilde\theta_{I,J}(P^J_I) =
356	  \det((X^I_J)^{-1}) = \det(X^I_J)^{-1}\). As \(\det(X^I_J)\) is a unit
357	  (\cref{lem:gr_minorDet_unit}), so is its inverse; the matrix-inverse
358	  determinant identity uses \cref{lem:gr_universalMinorInv_identities}.
359	\end{proof}
360	
361	\begin{definition}
362	
363	\leanok
364	[Transition map \(\theta_{I,J}\)]
365	  \label{def:gr_transition}
366	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.transitionMap}
367	  \uses{def:gr_transition_pre, lem:gr_transition_pre_unit, lem:mathlib_away_lift, def:gr_affine_chart}
368	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Construction by gluing together
369	  % affine patches" (read from
370	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L822-L836).
371	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
372	  % "For any $I$ and $J$, a ring homomorphism
373	  %  $\theta_{I,J} : \Z[X^J, 1/P^J_I] \to \Z[X^I, 1/P^I_J]$ is
374	  %  defined as follows. ...
375	  %  In particular, we have $\theta_{I,J}(P^J_I) = 1/P^I_J$,
376	  %  so the map extends to $\Z[X^J, 1/P^J_I]$."
377	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
378	  Because the pre-localisation hom \(\tilde\theta_{I,J} : \mathbb{Z}[X^J] \to
379	  R^I_J\) (\cref{def:gr_transition_pre}) sends \(P^J_I\) to a unit of \(R^I_J\)
380	  (\cref{lem:gr_transition_pre_unit}), the universal property of the
381	  away-localisation (\cref{lem:mathlib_away_lift}) provides a unique ring
382	  homomorphism extending it along \(\mathbb{Z}[X^J] \to \mathbb{Z}[X^J, 1/P^J_I]\).
383	  This extension is the \emph{transition map}
384	  \[
385	    \theta_{I,J} : \mathbb{Z}[X^J, 1/P^J_I] \longrightarrow
386	    \mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_J], \qquad \theta_{I,J}(X^J) = (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I .
387	  \]
388	  Dually \(\theta_{I,J}\) is the comorphism of a morphism of schemes
389	  \(U^I_J \to U^J_I\) (\cref{def:gr_affine_chart}), which we also denote
390	  \(\theta_{I,J}\); it sends the universal matrix \(X^I\) to \((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\).
391	\end{definition}
392	
393	\begin{lemma}
394	
395	\leanok
396	[\(\theta_{I,I}\) is the identity]
397	  \label{lem:gr_transition_self}
398	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.transitionMap_self}
399	  \uses{def:gr_transition, def:gr_minor_det}
400	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Construction by gluing together
401	  % affine patches" (read from
402	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L838-L848).
403	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
404	  % "Note that $\theta_{I,I}$ is identity on $U^I_I = U^I$".
405	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
406	  For every \(I\), \(\theta_{I,I} = \id_{U^I}\): since \(P^I_I = 1\)
407	  (\cref{def:gr_minor_det}) the minor \(X^I_I = 1_{d\times d}\) is its own
408	  inverse, so \(\theta_{I,I}(X^I) = (X^I_I)^{-1} X^I = X^I\), i.e.\
409	  \(\theta_{I,I}\) (\cref{def:gr_transition}) is the identity ring homomorphism
410	  of \(\mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_I] = \mathbb{Z}[X^I]\).
411	\end{lemma}
412	\begin{proof}
413	  \uses{def:gr_transition, def:gr_minor_det}
414	  \leanok
415	  When \(J = I\) one has \(P^I_I = \det(1_{d\times d}) = 1\)
416	  (\cref{def:gr_minor_det}), so \(R^I_I = \mathbb{Z}[X^I]\), the minor \(X^I_I\)
417	  is the identity, and its Cramer inverse is again the identity. Hence the image
418	  matrix is \(M = 1 \cdot X^I = X^I\), the pre-hom sends each \(x^I_{p,q}\) to
419	  itself, and its localisation lift (\cref{def:gr_transition}) is the identity.
420	\end{proof}
421	
422	\subsection*{Project-local matrix and minor bookkeeping}
423	
424	The cocycle computation rests on a handful of elementary matrix-algebra
425	identities over the (localised) chart rings. They are project-internal helpers
426	with no external source; each is verified directly in Lean.
427	
428	\begin{lemma}
429	
430	\leanok
431	[Column submatrix of a product]
432	  \label{lem:gr_mul_submatrix_col}
433	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.mul_submatrix_col}
434	  Let \(A \in \operatorname{Mat}_{d\times d}(R)\) and
435	  \(B \in \operatorname{Mat}_{d\times r}(R)\) be matrices over a commutative ring,
436	  and \(g : \{1,\dots,d\} \to \{1,\dots,r\}\) a column-index map. Then taking the
437	  column submatrix of the product equals multiplying by the column submatrix of
438	  the right factor:
439	  \[
440	    (A B)_{\bullet\, g} \;=\; A \,\bigl(B_{\bullet\, g}\bigr).
441	  \]
442	\end{lemma}
443	\begin{proof}
444	
445	\leanok
446	  Entrywise from the definition of matrix multiplication (reindexing the rows by
447	  the identity leaves the contraction index untouched). Proved directly in Lean.
448	\end{proof}
449	
450	\begin{lemma}
451	
452	\leanok
453	[A ring map commutes with the Cramer inverse]
454	  \label{lem:gr_map_nonsing_inv}
455	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.map_nonsing_inv}
456	  \uses{lem:mathlib_mul_nonsing_inv}
457	  Let \(f : R \to S\) be a homomorphism of commutative rings and
458	  \(A \in \operatorname{Mat}_{n\times n}(R)\) a square matrix with \(\det A\) a
459	  unit. Writing \(f_\ast\) for the entrywise application of \(f\), the
460	  nonsingular inverse is natural:
461	  \[
462	    \bigl(f_\ast A\bigr)^{-1} \;=\; f_\ast\bigl(A^{-1}\bigr).
463	  \]
464	\end{lemma}
465	\begin{proof}
466	
467	\leanok
468	  Since \(f_\ast A \cdot f_\ast(A^{-1}) = f_\ast(A A^{-1}) = f_\ast(1_{n\times n})
469	  = 1_{n\times n}\) (using \(A A^{-1} = 1\), \cref{lem:mathlib_mul_nonsing_inv}),
470	  the matrix \(f_\ast(A^{-1})\) is a right inverse of \(f_\ast A\), hence equals
471	  \((f_\ast A)^{-1}\). Proved directly in Lean.
472	\end{proof}
473	
474	\begin{lemma}
475	
476	\leanok
477	[Functoriality of entrywise application]
478	  \label{lem:gr_map_map_eq_of_comp}
479	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.map_map_eq_of_comp}
480	  Let \(f : R \to A\), \(g : A \to D\) and \(h : R \to D\) be ring
481	  homomorphisms with \(g \circ f = h\). Then for any matrix \(M\) over \(R\),
482	  applying \(f\) then \(g\) entrywise equals applying \(h\) entrywise:
483	  \(g_\ast(f_\ast M) = h_\ast M\).
484	\end{lemma}
485	\begin{proof}
486	
487	\leanok
488	  Immediate from functoriality of \(\operatorname{Mat}(-)\) (composition of
489	  entrywise maps). Proved directly in Lean.
490	\end{proof}
491	
492	\begin{lemma}
493	
494	\leanok
495	[Inverse cancellation in a composite]
496	  \label{lem:gr_inv_mul_inv_mul_cancel}
497	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.inv_mul_inv_mul_cancel}
498	  \uses{lem:mathlib_mul_nonsing_inv}
499	  Let \(A, B \in \operatorname{Mat}_{d\times d}(R)\) and
500	  \(M \in \operatorname{Mat}_{d\times e}(R)\) over a commutative ring, with
501	  \(\det A\) a unit. Then
502	  \[
503	    \bigl(B^{-1} A\bigr)\,\bigl(A^{-1} M\bigr) \;=\; B^{-1} M.
504	  \]
505	\end{lemma}
506	\begin{proof}
507	
508	\leanok
509	  By associativity and \(A A^{-1} = 1_{d\times d}\)
510	  (\cref{lem:mathlib_mul_nonsing_inv}). Proved directly in Lean.
511	\end{proof}
512	
513	\begin{lemma}
514	
515	\leanok
516	[The \(I\)-minor of \(X^I\) is the identity]
517	  \label{lem:gr_universalMatrix_submatrix_self}
518	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.universalMatrix_submatrix_self}
519	  \uses{def:gr_universal_matrix}
520	  The \(I\)-minor of the universal matrix \(X^I\)
521	  (\cref{def:gr_universal_matrix}), with its \(I\)-columns read through the order
522	  isomorphism \(\{1,\dots,d\} \xrightarrow{\sim} I\), is the \(d\times d\)
523	  identity: \(X^I_I = 1_{d\times d}\). (This is the normalisation \(P^I_I = 1\)
524	  defining the chart.)
525	\end{lemma}
526	\begin{proof}
527	
528	\leanok
529	  By \cref{def:gr_universal_matrix} the \(q\)-column with \(q \in I\) is the
530	  standard basis vector \(e_p\) where \(p\) is the preimage of \(q\) under the
531	  order isomorphism \(\{1,\dots,d\} \xrightarrow{\sim} I\); so the \(I\)-minor
532	  is the identity. Proved directly in Lean.
533	\end{proof}
534	
535	\begin{lemma}
536	
537	\leanok
538	[The \(J\)-minor of the image matrix is the identity]
539	  \label{lem:gr_imageMatrix_submatrix_self}
540	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.imageMatrix_submatrix_self}
541	  \uses{def:gr_image_matrix, lem:gr_mul_submatrix_col, lem:gr_universalMinorInv_identities}
542	  The \(J\)-minor of the image matrix \(M = (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\)
543	  (\cref{def:gr_image_matrix}) is the identity:
544	  \[
545	    M_J \;=\; (X^I_J)^{-1}\, X^I_J \;=\; 1_{d\times d}.
546	  \]
547	\end{lemma}
548	\begin{proof}
549	
550	\leanok
551	  The \(J\)-column submatrix of \(M = (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\) is
552	  \((X^I_J)^{-1}\) times the \(J\)-column submatrix of \(X^I\), i.e.
553	  \((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I_J\), by \cref{lem:gr_mul_submatrix_col}; this is
554	  \(1_{d\times d}\) by the left-inverse identity
555	  (\cref{lem:gr_universalMinorInv_identities}). Proved directly in Lean.
556	\end{proof}

634	  Let \(X\) be a size-\(d\) subset and \(\iota : R^I_X \to D\) a ring

653	\subsection*{Triple-overlap rings and the localised transition maps}
654	
655	The cocycle condition \(\theta_{I,K} = \theta_{I,J} \circ \theta_{J,K}\) cannot
656	be stated as a naive composition of the landed transition maps of
657	\cref{def:gr_transition}: the codomain of \(\theta_{J,K}\) (the ring
658	\(R^J[1/P^J_K]\)) differs from the domain of \(\theta_{I,J}\) (the ring
659	\(R^J[1/P^J_I]\)). The identity therefore lives over the \emph{triple-overlap}
660	rings obtained by inverting both relevant minors in each chart ring:
661	\[
662	  S_K := R^K[1/(P^K_I P^K_J)], \quad
663	  S_J := R^J[1/(P^J_I P^J_K)], \quad
664	  S_I := R^I[1/(P^I_J P^I_K)].
665	\]
666	
667	\begin{definition}
668	
669	\leanok
670	[Inclusion of an away-localisation into a double one (left)]
671	  \label{def:gr_away_incl_left}
672	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.awayInclLeft}
673	  \uses{lem:mathlib_away_lift, lem:mathlib_away_algebraMap_isUnit}
674	  For \(x, y \in R\), the canonical inclusion
675	  \(\iota^{\mathrm{L}}_{x,y} : R[1/x] \to R[1/(xy)]\) inverting the extra factor
676	  \(y\), defined as the away-localisation lift (\cref{lem:mathlib_away_lift}) of
677	  the structure map \(R \to R[1/(xy)]\) along \(x\) — well-defined because \(x\)
678	  is a unit of \(R[1/(xy)]\) (a factor of the unit \(xy\),
679	  \cref{lem:mathlib_away_algebraMap_isUnit}).
680	\end{definition}

773	  Let \(A, B, C\) be size-\(d\) subsets and \(\iota : R^A_B \to D\) a ring

851	\leanok
852	[Image-matrix identity over the triple overlap]
853	  \label{lem:gr_cocycle_imageMatrix_eq}
854	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.cocycle_imageMatrix_eq}
855	  \uses{lem:gr_imageMatrix_map_eq, def:gr_cocycle_theta_ij, def:gr_away_incl_right, def:gr_away_incl_left, lem:gr_awayInclRight_comp_algebraMap, lem:gr_awayInclLeft_comp_algebraMap, lem:gr_universalMatrix_map_transitionPreMap, lem:gr_map_map_eq_of_comp, lem:gr_map_nonsing_inv, lem:gr_mul_submatrix_col, lem:gr_inv_mul_inv_mul_cancel, lem:gr_isUnit_algebraMap_away_left, lem:gr_minorDet_unit, def:gr_image_matrix, def:gr_universal_minor}

884	
885	\begin{lemma}
886	
887	\leanok
888	[Cocycle condition for the transition maps]
889	  \label{lem:gr_cocycle}
890	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.cocycleCondition}
891	  \uses{def:gr_transition, def:gr_affine_chart, def:gr_image_matrix, lem:gr_universalMinorInv_identities, lem:gr_transition_self, def:gr_cocycle_theta_ij, def:gr_cocycle_theta_jk, def:gr_cocycle_theta_ik, lem:gr_cocycle_imageMatrix_eq}
892	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Construction by gluing together
893	  % affine patches" (read from
894	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L838-L848).
895	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
896	  % "Note that $\theta_{I,I}$ is identity on $U^I_I = U^I$, and
897	  %  we leave it to the reader to verify that
898	  %  for any three subsets $I$, $J$ and $K$ of $\{ 1,\ldots, r\}$ of
899	  %  cardinality $d$, the co-cycle condition
900	  %  $\theta_{I,K}= \theta_{I,J}\theta_{J,K}$
901	  %  is satisfied."
902	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
903	  For any three subsets \(I, J, K \subseteq \{1, \dots, r\}\) of cardinality
904	  \(d\), the transition maps of \cref{def:gr_transition} satisfy the cocycle
905	  condition. As morphisms of schemes, on the triple overlap inside \(U^I\),
906	  \[
907	    \theta_{I,K} \;=\; \theta_{J,K} \circ \theta_{I,J},
908	  \]
909	  equivalently, as ring homomorphisms,
910	  \(\theta_{I,K} = \theta_{I,J} \circ \theta_{J,K}\) (the two read the same
911	  identity through the order-reversal of \(\Spec\)). Together with
912	  \(\theta_{I,I} = \id\), this is exactly the compatibility datum required to
913	  glue the charts.
914	\end{lemma}
915	
916	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Note that $\theta_{I,I}$ is identity on $U^I_I = U^I$, and
917	% we leave it to the reader to verify that
918	% for any three subsets $I$, $J$ and $K$ of $\{ 1,\ldots, r\}$ of
919	% cardinality $d$, the co-cycle condition
920	% $\theta_{I,K}= \theta_{I,J}\theta_{J,K}$
921	% is satisfied."  (references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex,
922	% L838-L848.)
923	\begin{proof}
924	  \uses{def:gr_transition, def:gr_affine_chart, def:gr_image_matrix, lem:gr_universalMinorInv_identities, lem:gr_transition_self, def:gr_cocycle_theta_ij, def:gr_cocycle_theta_jk, def:gr_cocycle_theta_ik, lem:gr_cocycle_imageMatrix_eq}
925	  \leanok
926	  We verify the scheme-morphism form
927	  \(\theta_{I,K} = \theta_{J,K} \circ \theta_{I,J}\) by a direct matrix
928	  computation, valid over the open locus of \(U^I\) on which all the minors
929	  involved are invertible (namely \(P^I_J\) and \(P^I_K\), which suffices since
930	  the maps are determined by their restriction to a dense open and all rings are
931	  domains; the computation is an identity of matrices with entries in
932	  \(\mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_J, 1/P^I_K]\)).
933	
934	  Start from the matrix \(X^I\) (the universal point of \(U^I\)). By
935	  \cref{def:gr_transition},
936	  \[
937	    M^J \;:=\; \theta_{I,J}(X^I) \;=\; (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I,
938	  \]
939	  a \(d \times r\) matrix whose \(J\)-minor is the identity, hence a point of
940	  \(U^J\). Its \(K\)-minor is
941	  \[
942	    M^J_K \;=\; (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I_K,
943	    \qquad\text{so}\qquad
944	    (M^J_K)^{-1} \;=\; (X^I_K)^{-1} X^I_J,
945	  \]
946	  using \((AB)^{-1} = B^{-1} A^{-1}\) for the invertible \(d \times d\) matrices
947	  \(X^I_J\) and \(X^I_K\). Applying \(\theta_{J,K}\) to \(M^J\) gives
948	  \[
949	    \theta_{J,K}(M^J) \;=\; (M^J_K)^{-1} M^J
950	    \;=\; (X^I_K)^{-1} X^I_J \,\cdot\, (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I
951	    \;=\; (X^I_K)^{-1} X^I,
952	  \]
953	  the middle product \(X^I_J (X^I_J)^{-1} = 1_{d\times d}\) cancelling by
954	  associativity of matrix multiplication. The right-hand side is precisely
955	  \(\theta_{I,K}(X^I) = (X^I_K)^{-1} X^I\). Thus
956	  \(\theta_{J,K} \circ \theta_{I,J}\) and \(\theta_{I,K}\) agree on the
957	  universal matrix \(X^I\), hence agree as morphisms \(U^I \supseteq U^I_J \cap
958	  U^I_K \to U^K\). The special case \(K = I\) (resp.\ \(J = I\)) together with
959	  \(\theta_{I,I} = \id\) shows each \(\theta_{I,J}\) is an isomorphism onto its
960	  image with inverse \(\theta_{J,I}\), since
961	  \(\theta_{J,I} \circ \theta_{I,J} = \theta_{I,I} = \id\).
962	\end{proof}
963	
964	\section{Scheme-level glue-data layer}
965	\label{sec:gr_glue_layer}
966	
967	The transition data of \cref{sec:gr_transition} lives at the level of rings. To
968	feed it to a scheme-gluing machine we must re-express each piece as a morphism in
969	the category of schemes: the charts \(U^I\), the principal-open overlaps
970	\(U^I_J\), their inclusions, and the transitions \(\theta_{I,J}\) all become
971	schemes and scheme morphisms by applying the contravariant functor \(\Spec\).
972	The lemmas of this section record those translations together with the two
973	genuinely geometric facts needed to assemble a glue datum: that each inclusion
974	\(U^I_J \hookrightarrow U^I\) is an open immersion, and that the fibre product of
975	two principal opens of \(\Spec A\) is again a principal open. They are
976	project-internal bridge results with no external source beyond the gluing
977	construction of \cref{def:gr_glued_scheme}.
978	
979	\subsection*{Mathlib inputs for the scheme-level layer}
980

983	  \label{lem:mathlib_specMap_localizationAway_isOpenImmersion}
984	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.isOpenImmersion_SpecMap_localizationAway}
985	  \mathlibok
986	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
987	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring and \(f \in R\). The morphism
988	  \(\Spec R[1/f] \to \Spec R\) induced by the structure map \(R \to R[1/f]\) of
989	  the away-localisation is an open immersion (onto the basic open
990	  \(D(f) \subseteq \Spec R\)).
991	\end{lemma}
992	
993	\begin{lemma}
994	[Fibre product of two affine spectra is \(\Spec\) of the tensor product]
995	  \label{lem:mathlib_pullbackSpecIso}
996	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pullbackSpecIso}
997	  \mathlibok
998	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
999	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring and \(S\), \(T\) two \(R\)-algebras. The fibre
1000	  product of \(\Spec S \to \Spec R \leftarrow \Spec T\) is canonically isomorphic
1001	  to the spectrum of the tensor product:
1002	  \[
1003	    \Spec S \times_{\Spec R} \Spec T \;\cong\; \Spec\bigl(S \otimes_R T\bigr).
1004	  \]
1005	\end{lemma}
1006	
1007	\begin{lemma}

1017

1027	  \(R\)-algebra isomorphism between them.

14	\section{Setup and motivation}
15	\label{sec:relspec_setup}
16	
17	The Route~A construction of \(J = \Pic^0_{C/k}\) as a \(g\)-dimensional abelian variety is
18	organised around the relative Picard functor
19	\(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T) = \Pic(C \times_k T) / \pi_T^* \Pic(T)\)
20	on the category of \(k\)-schemes. Representing \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\) requires the line-bundle
21	pullback construction on a relative curve \(C \times_k T \to T\), which in turn rests on a
22	\emph{relative scheme} construction on a quasi-coherent sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebras
23	\(\mathcal{A}\), the \emph{relative spectrum}
24	\(\pi : \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A}) \to X\). This chapter packages the construction
25	of \(\underline{\Spec}_X(-)\) and its core properties --- universal property and affine
26	base --- as the foundational A.1.a building block. The relative Picard
27	functor itself (A.1.c), the line-bundle pullback functor on a product (A.1.b), and the
28	identity-component / dimension theory of \(J\) (A.3) live in separate sibling chapters
29	(\S~``Out of scope'' below).
30	
31	% SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Section ``Relative spectrum via glueing'' and ``Relative
32	% spectrum as a functor'' (tags 01LL, 01LO, 01LQ, 01LR, 01LS, available locally at
33	% references/stacks-constructions.tex L309-L720). The Hartshorne reference is
34	% [Hartshorne], II Exercise 5.17 (read from references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf).

36	\section{Quasi-coherent sheaves of algebras}
37	\label{sec:qc_sheaf_of_algebras}

39	\begin{definition}\leanok
40	[Quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebra]
41	  \label{def:qc_sheaf_of_algebras}
42	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.QcohAlgebra}
43	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Section 01LL ``Relative spectrum via glueing'',
44	  % Situation ``situation-relative-spec''
45	  % (read from references/stacks-constructions.tex, L312-L318).
46	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
47	  % "Here \(S\) is a scheme, and \(\mathcal{A}\) is a quasi-coherent
48	  %  \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-algebra. This means that \(\mathcal{A}\) is a
49	  %  sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-algebras which is quasi-coherent as an
50	  %  \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-module."
51	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 01LL (situation-relative-spec).}
52	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. A \emph{quasi-coherent sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebras} is a
53	  sheaf \(\mathcal{A}\) of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebras (i.e. an \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module
54	  equipped with \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-linear multiplication and unit morphisms satisfying
55	  associativity, commutativity, and the unit law as sheaf morphisms) whose underlying
56	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module is quasi-coherent. We write \(\mathrm{QcohAlg}(X)\) for the
57	  category of quasi-coherent sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebras and
58	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebra morphisms.
59	\end{definition}
60

73	% relative-spectrum scheme) via the dense-subsite equivalence

75	% `AffineZariskiSite.relativeGluingData` consumes, so the iter-179
76	% `RelativeSpec`/`structureMorphism` bodies (now real Mathlib values) close

79	\section{The relative-spectrum scheme}
80	\label{sec:relspec_scheme}
81	
82	The construction is given affine-locally: on every affine open \(U = \Spec R \subseteq X\) one
83	sets \(\pi^{-1}(U) := \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U))\) regarded as an \(R\)-algebra, and the local pieces
84	glue compatibly because \(\mathcal{A}\) is quasi-coherent (the transition isomorphism
85	\(\mathcal{A}(U) \otimes_R S \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{A}(V)\) for an inclusion
86	\(V = \Spec S \subseteq U\) gives an open immersion of the corresponding Specs).

402	The construction proceeds by gluing absolute affine pieces: for each affine open
403	\(U \subseteq X\) with \(U = \Spec R\) and \(A := \mathcal{A}(U)\) regarded as an
404	\(R\)-algebra, the local piece is \(\Spec A\) with structure morphism \(\Spec A \to \Spec R = U\)
405	induced by the algebra unit \(R \to A\). Mathlib provides \texttt{IsAffineOpen.toScheme} for
406	the affine-open inclusion and the absolute \texttt{Scheme.Spec} functor for the local
407	pieces; the transition isomorphisms come from quasi-coherence of \(\mathcal{A}\) via
408	\texttt{QuasiCoherent.tensorIso} (or the explicit
409	\(\mathcal{A}(U) \otimes_R S \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{A}(V)\) for \(V = \Spec S \subseteq U\)).

411	the convenience wrapper \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.AffineScheme.glueOpens}; the cocycle
412	condition reduces to Stacks \texttt{lemma-transitive-spec}. The universal property

416	\Hom_{\mathcal{O}_X\text{-alg}}(\mathcal{A}, g_*\mathcal{O}_T)$ as a

437	    separate Route~A sub-row, fed by but not constructed in this chapter.

Looking more carefully at SectionGradedRing.tex, I'm seeing that `def:sheafTensorObj`, `def:sheafTensorPow`, and `def:sheafModuleTwist` all have `\leanok` markers, while `lem:sheafTensorPow_add` doesn't have one on its statement. For `def:sectionMul`, the definition starts at line 357 with a `\lean` reference to `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.sectionsMul` but no `\leanok` marker itself. Let me dig deeper into this.

The `\leanok` status is actually managed by `sync_leanok`, so its presence depends on whether the Lean declaration exists and has no sorry. But for the directive, I need to evaluate whether `def:sectionMul` (`sectionsMul`) still passes as a complete and correct BLUEPRINT declaration—not just whether the Lean formalization is clean. It was marked PASS in iter-048, so I need to confirm it still meets those criteria from the blueprint reviewer's perspective.

For `lem:sheafTensorPow_add`, which was FAIL in iter-048, the proof has been rewritten using the Analogue-4 local-iso criterion approach: a sheaf morphism is an isomorphism if it's locally an isomorphism on each open cover member, and on trivialising opens where the line bundle becomes trivial, all tensor powers reduce to the structure sheaf with identity comparisons, which localizes the coherence checks to simple statements.

The strategy-critic confirmed that the necessary categorical infrastructure exists—the monoidal category structure on sheaves, the strong-monoidal sheafification functor, and the monoidal morphism constructor—but flagged that the presheaf-of-modules sheafification transport is missing. The proof constructs the comparison maps μ by induction using the associator and braiding from the sheaf tensor object definition, relying on the claim that these maps are visibly bijective (identity) over trivialising opens, which requires verifying that the associator and braiding actually exist for the sheaf tensor structure.

The core issue is that the sheaf tensor product is defined as the sheafification of the presheaf tensor product, so the associator of the sheaf tensor isn't simply the sheafified version of the presheaf associator—that would only work if sheafification itself were monoidal, which is precisely what's missing in the required form.

The proof avoids appealing to a global monoidal structure on ShMod and instead tries to construct the comparison map μ inductively using only local isomorphism checks on locally free factors. But here's the tension: the proof invokes "the associator and braiding of def:sheafTensorObj," yet that definition only specifies the tensor product object itself, not an associator or braiding. Those structural maps would need to be defined separately, and it's unclear where they come from without the monoidal structure the proof claims to avoid.

That said, I notice that `CategoryTheory.Sheaf.monoidalCategory` does exist in Mathlib. If this provides a monoidal structure on sheaves valued in the right category, and we can restrict it to `SheafOfModules`, then the associator might actually be available after all—which would resolve the apparent gap.

The proof strategy becomes clearer now: it takes the structural maps (associator, unitor, braiding) from the presheaf monoidal structure via `lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib`, then verifies they descend to sheaves using the local isomorphism criterion—a morphism is an isomorphism if it's locally an isomorphism. The real question is whether these maps are built into the definition of the sheaf tensor product itself, or if they need separate construction. The remark at the end suggests the full monoidal structure on `ShMod` is deferred, but the bespoke construction for tensor powers is claimed to work regardless.

The core issue: the proof references the associator, unitor, and braiding as part of `def:sheafTensorObj`, yet that definition only specifies the object itself, not the morphisms. Either these come from the existing Mathlib machinery (`CategoryTheory.Sheaf.monoidalCategory`), which would create an implicit dependency, or they need to be constructed separately—and the proof doesn't make this explicit.

There's also a circularity problem: the inductive construction of μ_{m,m'} depends on the associator, but if the associator is part of the ShMod monoidal structure being defined, we're using it before it exists. And the claim that "the subtlety of monoidal descent doesn't bite here" because certain objects are never tested—that's asserted but not rigorously verified, especially since the construction involves associators on tensor products of powers of L.

The core issue is that the proof invokes structural maps (associator and braiding) from the monoidal structure on ShMod, yet explicitly claims not to appeal to such a structure, creating a direct contradiction. The local-iso criterion itself is sound mathematically, but the morphism being constructed inductively depends on these structural comparisons, which either need the monoidal structure or require independent definitions—neither of which is provided. This circular dependency makes the Analogue-4 route unformalizable as currently written.

Route 2 using generic transport via the monoidal category machinery would be cleaner, though the strategy-critic flagged that the sheafification transport from PresheafOfModules to SheafOfModules is missing. The gap isn't in the generic machinery itself, but specifically in how the monoidal sheafification lifts between these two categories. So the real gap is that sheafification exists as a functor but lacks a proof that it preserves the monoidal structure—the presheaf level has everything, but nothing connects it to the sheaf level. The Analogue-4 approach sidesteps this by focusing on tensor powers of a single line bundle rather than requiring a full monoidal structure on sheaves.

However, the current proof incorrectly invokes structural maps like the associator and braiding that only exist within a monoidal category, which SheafOfModules doesn't have. The correct approach would define the tensor maps directly by sheafifying the presheaf-level maps and verifying they're isomorphisms locally—this is standard, but it requires establishing that the presheaf tensor product actually induces a well-defined map after sheafification.

The proof as written conflates two different routes without fully committing to either, leaving a structural gap that would likely cause a formalization attempt to fail.

Now I'm moving on to evaluate the second focus item in the directive. Looking at the lemma about finite type and affine finite covers, the proof is detailed with proper source citations and clear sub-pieces, so this one checks out. I'm now examining the next lemma about quasi-coherent sheaves and finite sections—both have their Lean targets properly annotated with `\leanok` markers, and the pin mismatch issue appears to have been resolved.

Now looking at `lem:gf_flat_locality_assembly` around line 1954, the proof structure is there with three main steps covering the free-to-flat reduction, flatness locality on the source, and stability under base restriction, though the second and third steps lack precision—step 2 doesn't cite the specific locality criterion for flatness, and step 3 trails off without completing the argument about global sections.

The core issue is that the proof needs explicit justification for how flatness locality applies via Mathlib and how the base-restriction claim about section modules localizing over patches W_j follows from quasi-coherence and the affine structure. The strategy document also marks this as a stub needing expansion, so the verdict remains that this lemma is incomplete. Step 2 references a legitimate mathematical principle about flatness being local on the base ring, but doesn't cite a specific Mathlib lemma. Step 3 is even more problematic—it tries to identify the section module on W with a localization of the section module on W_j, but the reasoning is vague and the cited lemma actually concerns finiteness of sections rather than localization identification. So the verdict remains FAIL for G3: the proof sketch is missing concrete Mathlib references and the localization identification step needs proper justification.

225	\begin{lemma}[Tensor-power comparison isomorphism]
226	  \label{lem:sheafTensorPow_add}
227	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorPowAdd}
228	  \uses{def:sheafTensorPow, def:sheafTensorObj}
229	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Sheaves of Modules", \S "Invertible modules"

239	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Sheaves of Modules", Tag 01CU (canonical
240	  power isomorphisms).}
241	  For a line bundle \(\mathcal{L}\) and \(m, m' \in \mathbb{N}\) there is a
242	  canonical isomorphism of sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules
243	  \[
244	    \mu_{m,m'} :
245	    \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'}
246	    \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\; \mathcal{L}^{\otimes (m+m')},
247	  \]
248	  natural in \(\mathcal{L}\), and these isomorphisms satisfy a commutativity
249	  constraint (\(\mu_{m,m'}\) agrees with \(\mu_{m',m}\) after the braiding) and
250	  an associativity constraint (the two ways of combining
251	  \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'} \otimes
252	  \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m''}\) into \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes (m+m'+m'')}\) agree).
253	\end{lemma}
254	
255	\begin{proof}
256	  \uses{def:sheafTensorPow, def:sheafTensorObj,
257	    lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib,
258	    lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}
259	  We do \emph{not} appeal to a monoidal structure on the whole category
260	  \(\ShMod(\mathcal{O}_X)\) --- none is available --- but only to the comparison
261	  on tensor \emph{powers} of the single invertible sheaf \(\mathcal{L}\). On such
262	  locally free factors the construction is elementary, and right-exactness of the
263	  tensor product is never invoked.
264	
265	  \textbf{A local-iso criterion.} A morphism of sheaves of
266	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules is an isomorphism as soon as it is one locally, i.e.\
267	  after restriction to each member of an open cover of \(X\): two-sided inverses
268	  defined over the members of a cover and agreeing on overlaps glue to a global
269	  inverse. We invoke this criterion \emph{only} on tensor products of sheaves
270	  that are locally free of rank one. On a trivialising open \(U\) where
271	  \(\mathcal{L}|_U \cong \mathcal{O}_U\), every tensor power restricts to
272	  \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes k}|_U \cong \mathcal{O}_U\) as well; the tensor product
273	  presheaf there takes the value \(\mathcal{O}_X(U) \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)}
274	  \mathcal{O}_X(U) = \mathcal{O}_X(U)\); and the sheafification unit \(\eta\)
275	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}) restricts over \(U\) to the
276	  identity. Hence over each such \(U\) the structural comparison maps used below
277	  --- the unitor, associator, and braiding of \cref{def:sheafTensorObj},
278	  descended objectwise from \cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib} --- are
279	  visibly bijective, and the criterion promotes them to genuine isomorphisms in
280	  \(\ShMod(\mathcal{O}_X)\). The subtlety that obstructs a general monoidal
281	  descent --- that the sheafification comparison
282	  \(\eta_{\mathcal P} \otimes \mathrm{id}\) need not be locally injective when
283	  \(\mathcal P\) is arbitrary, the tensor product being only right exact --- does
284	  not bite here: that object is never among the ones we test. The only maps we
285	  test are comparisons between locally free factors, where the unit is locally an
286	  identity and the comparison is a genuine local isomorphism.
287	
288	  \textbf{Construction of \(\mu_{m,m'}\).} Fix \(m'\) and induct on \(m\). For
289	  \(m = 0\),
290	  \[
291	    \mathcal{L}^{\otimes 0} \otimes \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'}
292	      = \mathcal{O}_X \otimes \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'}
293	      \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'}
294	  \]
295	  is the left unitor of \cref{def:sheafTensorObj}, an isomorphism by the
296	  criterion. For the inductive step the recursive definition
297	  \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes (m+1)} = \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes \mathcal{L}\)
298	  (\cref{def:sheafTensorPow}) gives
299	  \[
300	    \mathcal{L}^{\otimes (m+1)} \otimes \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'}
301	    = (\mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes \mathcal{L}) \otimes
302	      \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'}
303	    \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes
304	      (\mathcal{L} \otimes \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'})
305	    \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes
306	      (\mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'} \otimes \mathcal{L}),
307	  \]
308	  using first the associator and then the braiding of \cref{def:sheafTensorObj},
309	  each an isomorphism by the criterion. A second associator and the inductive
310	  comparison \(\mu_{m,m'}\) whiskered by \(\mathcal{L}\) then identify the
311	  right-hand side with
312	  \[
313	    (\mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'}) \otimes
314	      \mathcal{L}
315	    \xrightarrow{\ \mu_{m,m'} \otimes \mathrm{id}\ }
316	    \mathcal{L}^{\otimes (m + m')} \otimes \mathcal{L}
317	    = \mathcal{L}^{\otimes (m + m' + 1)},
318	  \]
319	  the final equality being once more the recursive definition. The composite is
320	  \(\mu_{m+1,m'}\), an isomorphism as a composite of isomorphisms. Naturality in
321	  \(\mathcal{L}\) holds because each constituent comparison is natural.
322	
323	  \textbf{Commutativity and associativity constraints.} Every map appearing in
324	  the construction is a structural comparison isomorphism between locally free
325	  factors, so the required coherences are equalities of morphisms that may be
326	  checked locally on a common trivialising cover. Over such an open every sheaf
327	  in sight becomes \(\mathcal{O}_U\) and every comparison map becomes the
328	  identity, whence both sides of the commutativity square (\(\mu_{m,m'}\) versus
329	  \(\mu_{m',m}\) postcomposed with the braiding) and of the associativity
330	  diagram (the two bracketings of
331	  \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'} \otimes
332	  \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m''} \to \mathcal{L}^{\otimes (m+m'+m'')}\)) reduce to the
333	  same local identity. Gluing the local equalities yields the global cocycle
334	  relations: the family \(\{\mu_{m,m'}\}\) is coherent in the
335	  pentagon/hexagon sense.
336	
337	  \textbf{Remark on the principled route.} One could instead obtain \(\mu\) from
338	  a genuine monoidal structure on \(\ShMod(\mathcal{O}_X)\) descended from the
339	  presheaf-level monoidal structure (\cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib})
340	  along sheafification, via the localized-monoidal / reflective-localization
341	  template for sheaf categories. That principled construction requires a
342	  monoidal-closed structure on \(\PshMod\) that is not built in this chapter, and
343	  is deferred. The bespoke line-bundle construction above is all the section
344	  graded ring requires.
345	\end{proof}
346	
347	\section{Lax-monoidal global sections}
348	\label{sec:sgr_lax_sections}
349	
350	The graded multiplication is supplied by the lax-monoidal structure of the
351	global-sections functor \(\Gamma(X, -) : \ShMod(\mathcal{O}_X) \to
352	\mathrm{ModuleCat}(\Gamma(X, \mathcal{O}_X))\). It is only \emph{lax} because
353	global sections do not commute with sheafification: a global section of a
354	sheafified tensor product is not in general a finite sum of elementary tensors of
355	global sections.
356	
357	\begin{definition}[Section multiplication]
358	  \label{def:sectionMul}
359	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.sectionsMul}
360	  \uses{def:sheafTensorObj, lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib,
361	    lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}
362	  Let \(\mathcal{F}, \mathcal{G}\) be sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. The
363	  \emph{section multiplication} is the \(\Gamma(X,\mathcal{O}_X)\)-bilinear map
364	  \[
365	    \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_{\Gamma(X,\mathcal{O}_X)}
366	      \Gamma(X, \mathcal{G})
367	    \;\longrightarrow\;
368	    \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G})
369	  \]
370	  defined as follows. A pair of global sections \((\sigma, \tau)\) determines,
371	  by the objectwise formula of \cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib} at the
372	  top open, the elementary tensor \(\sigma \otimes \tau \in
373	  (\mathcal{F} \otimes_{p,\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G})(X)\), a global section of
374	  the tensor product presheaf. Composing with the global-sections component of
375	  the sheafification unit
376	  \(\eta : \mathcal{F} \otimes_{p,\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G} \to
377	  (\mathcal{F} \otimes_{p,\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G})^{\#} =
378	  \mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G}\)
379	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib},
380	  \cref{def:sheafTensorObj}) yields a global section
381	  \(\sigma \cdot \tau \in
382	  \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G})\). Bilinearity over
383	  \(\Gamma(X,\mathcal{O}_X)\) is inherited from bilinearity of the elementary
384	  tensor and \(\Gamma(X,\mathcal{O}_X)\)-linearity of \(\eta\).
385	\end{definition}
386	
387	\begin{lemma}[Coherence of the section multiplication]
388	  \label{lem:sectionMul_coherent}
389	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.sectionsMul_assoc_unit}
390	  \uses{def:sectionMul, lem:moduleUnit_mathlib, lem:sheafTensorPow_add}
391	  The section multiplications (\cref{def:sectionMul}) are associative and unital
392	  in the following sense. For sheaves \(\mathcal{F}, \mathcal{G}, \mathcal{H}\)
393	  the two composites
394	  \[
395	    \Gamma(\mathcal{F}) \otimes \Gamma(\mathcal{G}) \otimes \Gamma(\mathcal{H})
396	    \longrightarrow
397	    \Gamma\bigl((\mathcal{F} \otimes \mathcal{G}) \otimes \mathcal{H}\bigr)
398	    \cong
399	    \Gamma\bigl(\mathcal{F} \otimes (\mathcal{G} \otimes \mathcal{H})\bigr)
400	  \]
401	  agree after transport along the associator of \cref{def:sheafTensorObj}; and
402	  the unit element \(1 \in \Gamma(X, \mathcal{O}_X)\)
403	  (\cref{lem:moduleUnit_mathlib}) acts as a two-sided identity, i.e.\ the section
404	  multiplication by \(1\) is, after the unitor isomorphism
405	  \(\mathcal{O}_X \otimes \mathcal{F} \cong \mathcal{F}\), the identity on
406	  \(\Gamma(X, \mathcal{F})\).
407	\end{lemma}
408	
409	\begin{proof}
410	  \uses{def:sectionMul, lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib,
411	    lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}
412	  Both statements reduce to the corresponding identities at the level of the
413	  tensor product presheaf, where by \cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}
414	  evaluation at the top open is strictly monoidal, so the associator and unitors
415	  act on elementary tensors by the usual formulas
416	  \((\sigma \otimes \tau) \otimes \upsilon \mapsto
417	  \sigma \otimes (\tau \otimes \upsilon)\) and
418	  \(1 \otimes \sigma \mapsto \sigma\). The sheafification unit \(\eta\) is a
419	  natural transformation, so it commutes with the associator and unitor

1680	  \(\OO_Y^{\oplus I} \twoheadrightarrow \F|_Y\).
1681	\end{lemma}
1682	
1683	\begin{proof}
1684	  A finite family of sections \(\{s_i\}_{i \in I}\) of \(\F|_Y\) is the same datum as an
1685	  \(\OO_Y\)-linear map \(\OO_Y^{\oplus I} \to \F|_Y\) sending the \(i\)-th basis section to
1686	  \(s_i\). The family generates \(\F|_Y\) (the induced map is surjective on every stalk)
1687	  exactly when this map is an epimorphism of sheaves of modules. Thus a finite generating
1688	  family yields a finite-free epimorphism, and conversely the images of the basis sections
1689	  of any epimorphism \(\OO_Y^{\oplus I} \twoheadrightarrow \F|_Y\) with \(I\) finite form a
1690	  finite generating family.
1691	\end{proof}
1692	
1693	\begin{lemma}[Half 2, seam 1 — finite type yields a finite cover of globally-generated affines]
1694	  \label{lem:gf_finiteType_affine_finite_cover_generated}
1695	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gf_finiteType_affine_finite_cover_generated}
1696	  % LEAN STATUS: base-case seam, project-built (Mathlib-absent). Proposed decl
1697	  % AlgebraicGeometry.gf_finiteType_affine_finite_cover_generated; assembled from the
1698	  % three sub-pieces gf_localGenerators_restrict (1a), gf_affine_finite_standard_subcover
1699	  % (1b), gf_finite_gen_iff_free_epi (1c). Extracts a standard finite cover of W on whose
1700	  % members F is globally generated, from the SheafOfModules.IsFiniteType local-generators
1701	  % datum refined by quasi-compactness of the affine W.
1702	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Properties of Schemes", Tag 01PB, proof
1703	  % (read from references/stacks-properties.tex, L2101-L2107).
1704	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
1705	  % "Assume $\widetilde M$ is a finite type $\mathcal{O}_X$-module. This means
1706	  %  there exists an open covering of $X$ such that $\widetilde M$ restricted to
1707	  %  the members of this covering is globally generated by finitely many sections.
1708	  %  Thus there also exists a standard open covering
1709	  %  $X = \bigcup_{i = 1, \ldots, n} D(f_i)$ such that $\widetilde M|_{D(f_i)}$
1710	  %  is generated by finitely many sections."
1711	  \uses{lem:gf_localGenerators_restrict, lem:gf_affine_finite_standard_subcover,
1712	        lem:gf_finite_gen_iff_free_epi}
1713	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Properties of Schemes", Tag 01PB (standard
1714	  cover of generated affines).}
1715	  Let \(X\) be a scheme, \(\F\) a quasi-coherent sheaf of modules of finite type,
1716	  and \(W \subseteq X\) an affine open. Then there is a finite family
1717	  \(t \subseteq \Gamma(X, W)\) with \(\mathrm{Ideal.span}\,t = \top\) such that for
1718	  each \(g \in t\) the basic open \(D(g)\) is affine and \(\F|_{D(g)}\) is globally
1719	  generated by finitely many sections; equivalently, for each \(g \in t\) there is
1720	  a finite index set \(I_g\) and an epimorphism of \(\OO_{D(g)}\)-modules
1721	  \(\OO_{D(g)}^{\oplus I_g} \twoheadrightarrow \F|_{D(g)}\).
1722	\end{lemma}
1723	
1724	\begin{proof}
1725	  \uses{lem:gf_localGenerators_restrict, lem:gf_affine_finite_standard_subcover,
1726	        lem:gf_finite_gen_iff_free_epi}
1727	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Tag 01PB (standard cover of generated affines).}
1728	  Finite type of \(\F\) means that \(X\) has an open covering \(\{Y_\alpha\}\) on each
1729	  member of which \(\F\) carries a finite generating family of sections. The intersections
1730	  \(\{Y_\alpha \cap W\}\) form an open cover of the affine \(W\). By the finite standard
1731	  subcover refinement \cref{lem:gf_affine_finite_standard_subcover} there is a finite family
1732	  \(t = \{g_1, \dots, g_n\} \subseteq \Gamma(X, W)\) with
1733	  \(\mathrm{Ideal.span}\,t = \top\) and each basic open \(D(g_i)\) contained in some
1734	  \(Y_\alpha \cap W\) — in particular \(D(g_i) \subseteq W\) is affine, and \(D(g_i)\)
1735	  embeds by an open immersion into the member \(Y_\alpha\) on which \(\F\) is finitely
1736	  generated. By the restriction-of-generators lemma
1737	  \cref{lem:gf_localGenerators_restrict} the finite generating family on \(Y_\alpha\)
1738	  restricts to a finite generating family of \(\F|_{D(g_i)}\), so \(\F|_{D(g_i)}\) is
1739	  globally generated by finitely many sections. Finally, by the free-epimorphism
1740	  reformulation \cref{lem:gf_finite_gen_iff_free_epi}, this is equivalent to the existence
1741	  of a finite index set \(I_{g_i}\) and an epimorphism of \(\OO_{D(g_i)}\)-modules
1742	  \(\OO_{D(g_i)}^{\oplus I_{g_i}} \twoheadrightarrow \F|_{D(g_i)}\). That the \(D(g_i)\)
1743	  cover \(W = \Spec \Gamma(X, W)\) is exactly \(\mathrm{Ideal.span}\,t = \top\). This is
1744	  Stacks' passage from the local-generation datum to a standard finite cover of generated
1745	  affines.
1746	\end{proof}

1748	\begin{lemma}[Affine $\widetilde{(-)}\dashv\Gamma$ adjunction with its $\mathrm{fromTildeΓ}$ counit]
1749	  \label{lem:tilde_adjunction_mathlib}
1750	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.tilde.adjunction}
1751	  \mathlibok
1752	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Modules.Tilde}).}
1753	  On the affine scheme \(\Spec R\) the associated-sheaf functor
1754	  \(\widetilde{(-)} : \mathrm{Mod}\,R \to (\Spec R).\mathrm{Modules}\) is left adjoint to the
1755	  affine section functor \(\Gamma : (\Spec R).\mathrm{Modules} \to \mathrm{Mod}\,R\) (Mathlib's
1756	  \(\mathrm{moduleSpecΓFunctor}\), evaluation of a sheaf of modules at the top open). The counit of
1757	  this adjunction is the natural transformation
1758	  \(\Gamma(-)\,\widetilde{\phantom{x}} \to \mathrm{id}\) whose component at a sheaf \(\mathcal{M}\)
1759	  is \(\mathcal{M}.\mathrm{fromTildeΓ} : \widetilde{\Gamma(\mathcal{M}, \top)} \to \mathcal{M}\), and
1760	  the left adjoint \(\widetilde{(-)}\) is faithful.
1761	\end{lemma}

1763	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1764	[Half 2, seam 2 — $\Gamma$ on affine quasi-coherent modules sends epis to surjections]
1765	  \label{lem:gf_affine_qcoh_Gamma_epi}
1766	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gf_affine_qcoh_Gamma_epi}
1767	  \uses{lem:qcoh_affine_section_localization, lem:tilde_adjunction_mathlib,
1768	    lem:isIso_fromTildeΓ_of_isLocalizedModule_restrict}
1769	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Properties of Schemes", Tag 01PB, proof
1770	  % (read from references/stacks-properties.tex, L2106-L2107) — the implication
1771	  % "$\widetilde M|_{D(f_i)}$ generated by finitely many sections $\Rightarrow$
1772	  % $M_{f_i}$ finitely generated" is exactly section-level surjectivity of a sheaf
1773	  % epi over an affine.
1774	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
1775	  % "such that $\widetilde M|_{D(f_i)}$ is generated by finitely many sections.
1776	  %  Thus $M_{f_i}$ is finitely generated for each $i$."
1777	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Properties of Schemes", Tag 01PB (sections of a
1778	  generated sheaf over an affine).}
1779	  Let \(V\) be an affine open of a scheme, with \(B = \Gamma(X, V)\) and
1780	  \(V \cong \Spec B\), and let \(\pi : \mathcal{G} \to \F\) be an epimorphism of
1781	  quasi-coherent sheaves of modules over \(V\). Then the induced map on sections
1782	  \(\Gamma(\pi) : \Gamma(\mathcal{G}, V) \to \Gamma(\F, V)\) is a surjective
1783	  \(B\)-module map.
1784	\end{lemma}
1785	
1786	\begin{proof}
1787	  \uses{lem:qcoh_affine_section_localization, lem:tilde_adjunction_mathlib,
1788	    lem:isIso_fromTildeΓ_of_isLocalizedModule_restrict}
1789	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Tag 01PB (sections of a generated sheaf over an
1790	  affine).}
1791	  Write \(V \cong \Spec B\) and let \(\Gamma\) be the affine section functor on
1792	  \((\Spec B).\mathrm{Modules}\), so that \(\Gamma(\pi) = \Gamma(\mathcal{G}, V) \to
1793	  \Gamma(\F, V)\) is the map in question. By \cref{lem:tilde_adjunction_mathlib} the
1794	  associated-sheaf functor \(\widetilde{(-)}\) is left adjoint to \(\Gamma\), with counit the
1795	  natural transformation whose component at a sheaf \(\mathcal{M}\) is
1796	  \(\mathcal{M}.\mathrm{fromTildeΓ} : \widetilde{\Gamma(\mathcal{M}, V)} \to \mathcal{M}\).
1797	
1798	  Because \(\mathcal{G}\) and \(\F\) are quasi-coherent, the qcoh-sections descent
1799	  (\cref{lem:qcoh_affine_section_localization}, fed through the counit-iso assembly
1800	  \cref{lem:isIso_fromTildeΓ_of_isLocalizedModule_restrict}) makes both counit components
1801	  \(\mathcal{G}.\mathrm{fromTildeΓ}\) and \(\F.\mathrm{fromTildeΓ}\) isomorphisms. Naturality of the
1802	  counit on \(\pi\) gives the commuting square
1803	  \[
1804	    \widetilde{\Gamma(\pi)} \circ \mathcal{G}.\mathrm{fromTildeΓ}^{-1}
1805	      = \F.\mathrm{fromTildeΓ}^{-1} \circ \pi,
1806	    \qquad\text{equivalently}\qquad
1807	    \widetilde{\Gamma(\pi)}
1808	      = \F.\mathrm{fromTildeΓ}^{-1} \circ \pi \circ \mathcal{G}.\mathrm{fromTildeΓ}.
1809	  \]
1810	  The right-hand side is a composite of an isomorphism, the epimorphism \(\pi\), and an
1811	  isomorphism, hence is itself an epimorphism. Thus \(\widetilde{\Gamma(\pi)}\) is an epimorphism
1812	  of sheaves of modules. Since the left adjoint \(\widetilde{(-)}\) is faithful, it reflects
1813	  epimorphisms; therefore \(\Gamma(\pi)\) is an epimorphism in \(\mathrm{Mod}\,B\), i.e. the
1814	  \(B\)-linear map \(\Gamma(\pi)\) is surjective.
1815	
1816	  This is the structural content of the vanishing of higher quasi-coherent cohomology on an
1817	  affine — the failure of \(\Gamma\) to be right exact would obstruct exactly this surjectivity —
1818	  obtained here directly from the affine adjunction without invoking a cohomology computation.
1819	\end{proof}

The key distinction here is that `def:sectionMul` hasn't been verified yet (unproved), while `lem:sheafTensorPow_add` also lacks the marker and needs reassessment. The question is whether the blueprint proof sketches for these are rigorous enough for someone to formalize.

For `def:sectionMul`, the definition spans lines 357-385 and describes a bilinear map from the tensor product of global sections Γ(X,F) ⊗_{Γ(X,O_X)} Γ(X,G) to the global sections of the tensor product sheaf Γ(X, F ⊗ G).

The construction takes elementary tensors from the presheaf module monoidal structure at the top open set, then composes with the sheafification unit to get the final map. Bilinearity follows naturally from the bilinearity of the elementary tensor and linearity of the sheafification unit. This is concrete and explicit enough that a prover has a clear path forward, so `def:sectionMul` should pass.

For `lem:sheafTensorPow_add`, the proof starting around line 255 uses the local-iso criterion as its main approach.  It then constructs the result inductively, relying on the left unitor, associator, and braiding that come from `def:sheafTensorObj`. The coherence conditions are verified by checking local equality. The key question is whether `def:sheafTensorObj` actually provides these structural maps explicitly, since it's defined as the sheafification of a pointwise presheaf tensor product.

The proof addresses this by noting that these maps are descended from the presheaf-level monoidal structure via sheafification, and then verified to be isomorphisms locally. The approach is valid — take the associator and braiding from the presheaf monoidal category, sheafify them to get maps of sheaves of modules, then confirm they're bijective on stalks. heaf modules induce natural transformations between their sheafifications through elementary categorical machinery. So the approach is to take the presheaf-level associator from the monoidal structure, apply sheafification to get a map of sheaves, then verify it's locally an isomorphism since everything becomes O_U locally and the associator becomes the identity. The proof alludes to this descent from the presheaf monoidal category but could be clearer about whether it's using the sheafification functor directly or the universal property, and it needs to spell out exactly how the structural maps (associator, unitor, braiding) are constructed for the sheaf tensor object.

The real issue is that `def:sheafTensorObj` defines the object itself but doesn't include these structural comparison maps, so a formalization needs to be explicit about where they come from—namely, by applying sheafification to the natural transformations from `PresheafOfModules.monoidalCategory`. The verdict is that the Analogue-4 route is mathematically sound and has all the necessary machinery available, but the proof glosses over this crucial step of how the structural maps are actually defined in Lean. The structural maps—associator, unitor, and braiding—need to be explicitly defined as the sheafified images of the presheaf-level maps rather than just saying they descend objectwise. This is a critical fix for the proof to be rigorous, though the overall approach via Analogue-4 is correct.

Now looking at the G3 proof for the flat locality assembly lemma, the "free to flat" step checks out, but the flat-locality step on the source needs scrutiny—it claims flatness is local on the base ring and that the covering provides the right structure.

However, there's a fundamental issue here: the argument conflates two distinct notions. Flat-locality for a module over a ring means checking flatness at maximal ideals, but what's being claimed is much stronger—that geometric flatness of sections over patches W_j implies flatness of sections over the whole fiber. The problem is that "the section algebra over A_f" isn't a single object; sections live over each W_j individually, and flatness of Γ(F, W_j)_f over A_f doesn't automatically yield flatness of sections on any affine subset of the fiber. The reasoning here appears circular or simply incorrect as stated.

Now looking at the stability under base-restriction step, there are several concrete errors: the claim that Γ(S,U) is always a localization of A_f for affine U ≤ V is false—this only holds when U is itself a basic open D(fg) in Spec A_f. Additionally, when the argument identifies Γ(F,W) with a localization of M_j, it's unclear which j applies since W need not be contained in any particular W_j. The cited lemma about finiteness of sections doesn't actually establish the localization identification being invoked here.

So G3 fails on mathematical grounds: the flat-locality argument conflates different notions of locality over the base ring versus geometric patches, and the stability step makes unjustified claims about localizations. A correct proof would need to either invoke a module-level flatness result for localization spans or properly connect the algebraic generic flatness theorem to the geometric setting.

The argument should invoke locality of flatness: to verify flatness of Γ(F,W) over A_f, it's enough to check at prime ideals, and each point of W sits in one of the (B_j)_f patches where we already have flatness. ### A. Picard_SectionGradedRing.tex

#### A1. `def:sectionMul` (`sectionsMul`) — was PASS iter-048; confirm

**PASS (confirmed).** Definition is concrete and self-contained:
- σ⊗τ formed objectwise via `PresheafOfModules.monoidalCategory` at top open → compose with sheafification unit η → lands in Γ(X, F⊗G)
- Bilinearity from linearity of η

#### A2. `lem:sheafTensorPow_add` (`tensorPowAdd`) — was FAIL iter-048; Analogue-4 rewrite; re-judge

**ROUTE DECISION: Analogue-4 is the correct route. Route #2 should NOT replace it.**

Reason: Route #2 requires strong-monoidal sheafification for `SheafOfModules` (varying-ring transport from `PresheafOfModules` monoidal → `SheafOfModules` monoidal). Strategy-critic iter-048 confirmed this is ABSENT from Mathlib. Route #2 would require proving sheafification is monoidal — the hard kernel Analogue-4 deliberately avoids.

**Analogue-4 math assessment:** Correct in principle. The local-iso criterion (an iso on a trivializing cover is a global iso) is valid. The inductive construction via unitor/associator/braiding applied locally is sound. The commutativity/associativity coherences via local equality on trivializing covers are correct.

**MUST-FIX (one gap):** The proof (lines 276-279) invokes "the unitor, associator, and braiding of def:sheafTensorObj, descended objectwise from lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib" but NEVER explicitly defines these maps in Lean terms. `def:sheafTensorObj` is an OBJECT — it does not inherently carry these structural maps. A prover needs to know:

> These maps are defined by applying the sheafification functor `PresheafOfModules.sheafification` (a functor from `PresheafOfModules` to `SheafOfModules`) to the corresponding natural transformations supplied by `PresheafOfModules.monoidalCategory`. Sheafification is functorial on natural transformations, so each presheaf-level structural map (assoc/unitor/braiding) yields a map of `SheafOfModules`.

Without this explicit route the prover has no instruction on where in Lean to source the associator/unitor/braiding that the inductive construction whiskering depends on.

**Writer must-fix:** Add 1–2 sentences immediately before "Construction of μ" explaining that the structural maps are obtained as the image under `PresheafOfModules.sheafification` of the corresponding natural transformations from `PresheafOfModules.monoidalCategory`, and that their local-iso status follows because the sheafification unit η is locally an identity on trivializing opens.

**Status:** Math CORRECT, construction step IMPLICIT. Not gate-ready until gap patched.

### B. Picard_FlatteningStratification.tex

#### B1. Seam-1 `lem:gf_finiteType_affine_finite_cover_generated` — was PASS; confirm

**PASS (confirmed).** Three-piece assembly (sub-pieces 1a/1b/1c) is complete:
- `lem:gf_localGenerators_restrict` (1a) + `lem:gf_affine_finite_standard_subcover` (1b) + `lem:gf_finite_gen_iff_free_epi` (1c) in `\uses{}`

#### B2. `\lean{}` pin fix on `lem:gf_qcoh_finite_sections_globally_generated` + new block `lem:gf_qcoh_sections_free_epi` — confirm resolution

**RESOLVED.** Both blocks are correct:

`lem:gf_qcoh_finite_sections_globally_generated` (line 1821):
- `\lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gf_qcoh_finite_sections_globally_generated}` — correct pin (general qcoh-epi form, not free-epi-only)

`lem:gf_qcoh_sections_free_epi` (line 1862):

#### B3. G3 `lem:gf_flat_locality_assembly` — was FAIL (thin); re-judge

**FAIL (still). Proof contains mathematical errors, not merely thinness.**

Two specific errors in the rewritten proof:

**Error 1 — "Flat-locality on the source" step (lines 1978–1985):**
The argument claims: "{W_j = Spec B_j}_j provides exactly such a covering family of the section algebra over A_f" — invoking flatness locality to assemble per-patch flatness of (M_j)_f into flatness of sections over all of p⁻¹(V).

This is WRONG as stated. Flat-locality of a module M over R means: M is flat over R iff M_p is flat over R_p for all primes p. The W_j = Spec B_j are geometric patches on the SOURCE X, not localizations of the BASE ring A_f. Per-patch flatness of (M_j)_f over A_f does not assemble to flatness of sections over an arbitrary affine W ≤ p⁻¹(V) via the ring-level flat-locality criterion (which would require localizing A_f, not the B_j). No Mathlib lemma is cited for this step.

**Error 2 — "Stability under base-restriction" step (lines 1987–1996):**
Claims: "For an affine U ≤ V the ring Γ(S,U) is a localization of A_f."

This is FALSE in general. Γ(S,U) is a localization of A_f only when U is a BASIC OPEN of Spec A_f (i.e., U = D(fg) ⊆ D(f)). For a general affine U ≤ D(f), Γ(S,U) need not be a localization of A_f.

Additionally: "Γ(F,W) is the corresponding localization of the finite section module over the relevant patch W_j" — which j? W need not be contained in any single W_j. The appeal to `lem:gf_qcoh_fintype_finite_sections` is misidentified: that lemma gives finiteness of sections, NOT a localization identification.

**Required rewrite for G3:**
The correct argument needs:
- A ring-level `Module.Flat.of_localizationSpan` (or `Module.Flat.iff_localization`) applied to the base A_f: show M flat over A_f by checking M_p is flat over (A_f)_p for all primes p, using quasi-coherence to localize to the W_j cover and the freeness of (M_j)_f; OR
- A full base-restriction argument: for W = Spec C ≤ p⁻¹(U) with U = Spec A' ≤ D(f), use quasi-coherence of F to write Γ(F,W) as Γ(F, W_j) ⊗_{B_j} C for some j (if W ≤ W_j), and flatness of Γ(F,W_j)_f over A_f combined with base-change; or via `lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen` for the affine case
- In all cases, a specific Mathlib lemma for flat-locality (none cited currently) is needed

G3 needs substantial rewrite before dispatch. **Not gate-ready.**
